This praise of words in yourhonour composed with Your own words is like the worship of lights in honour of the sun done withits own rays, the oblation in honour of the moonwith the water emanating from the moonstone,and the pleasing of the ocean with its own waters. Saundaryalahari Verse 100 Here is a mystic accessible to anyone and everyone, with whom you can converse and in whose presence you can feel God. She is humble but firm as the Earth. She is simple yet beautiful like the full moon. She is Love, she is Truth, she is the embodiment of Renunciation and self-sacrifice. She not only teaches but does. She is a giver of everything and a receiver of nothing. She is soft like a flower but hard like a diamond. She is a Great Master and a Great Mother. Such is Mata Amritanandamayi. Vi PREFACE She was born in full Awareness. Having undergone or dis­ played (we know not which) rigorous sadhana (spiritual dis­ cipline), she then embraced the entire world with love and compassion of indescribable dimensions, the love and com­ passion that is her very fibre and being. From her tender childhood she sought out the Divine Mother and Father even without the guidance of a Guru. She withstood the attacks of her kith and kin, of rationalists and miscreants all of whom tried to destroy her. All alone in the midst of this battlefield, she confronted everything unper­ turbed and with steadfast courage. At the age of 21 she out­ wardly manifested her state of God-Realisation and at 22 be­ gan to initiate seekers of Truth into spiritual life. By the age of 27, the Holy Mother had established the spiritual headquar­ ters of her international Mission in the house of her birth. Five years later there were nearly 20 branch Ashrams throughout India and abroad. At the age of 33, in response to the invita­ tion of her devotees in America and Europe, the Holy Mother made her first world tour inspiring and uplifting many people around the world. Above all she has counselled, wiped the tears and removed the burdens of thousands and thousands of people from all walks of life and from every corner of the Earth. It is left to you, dear reader, to decide who and what she is through the intuition of your heart.... Amritatma ChaitanyaTHE LEGEND In the Alappad Panchayat,1 district of Quilon, Kerala state, south India, there is situated a small village named Parayakadavu. This village lies amidst an endless expanse of coconut palms stretching along a narrow peninsula sepa­ rated from the mainland to the east by an intercoastal water­ way while the western shore
2: of the village is buffeted by the sparkling blue-green Arabian Sea. 1 An alliance of five villages, the governing body overseeing local affairs. 2 Another name for Sri Muruga, the brother of Sri GaneshThe people of this village belong to the humble clanoffisherfolk who proudly trace their ancestry as far back asthesage Parasara. It is sage Parasara who married thefishermaidSatyavati, the mother of Sri Veda Vyasa, therenowned codi­fier of the Vedas. There are many legends toldabout the sanc­tity and greatness of this village where dailylife and socialcustom are still closely associated with suchdivine mythologies which the fisherfolk strongly believehappened thou­sands of years ago. One such legend is asfollows: Once Lord Subramanya,2 son of Lord Siva and Goddess Parvati, committed a serious error. Infuriated by the trans­ gression of His son. Lord Siva cursed Subramanya to take the birth of a fish. Dejected by the ill-fate of Her son, Parvati re­ quested the Lord to forgive Subramanya’s fault. Instead of consoling Her, Siva became more angry and cursed Parvati as well to take birth as a fisherwoman.Later when Lord Siva’s anger had subsided He told Subramanya that He Himself would come and liberate both of them at the correct time and thus blessed them.THE LEGEND viii In accordance with Lord Siva’s curse. Lord Subramanya assumed the form of a fish, rather, a huge whale. Appearing in the sea of Alappad this huge whale caused the fishermen ter­ rible harm. Accustomed to fishing both during the day and night time, the fishermen could now no longer venture into the seas. Sometimes the whale would tear the cast nets of the fishermen to shreds and at other times it would overturn their boats, endangering their very lives.In due course, the villag­ ers were thrown in the midst of poverty and starvation. The king of the fisherfolk also failed to find a solution. His treasury was becoming nearly bankrupt as he was feeding the starving people. Finally, in an attempt to solve the problem, he made a proclamation. The person who could catch the huge troublesome whale would be richly rewarded and would also be given the fair hand of the king’s only daughter in royal marriage. Yet the huge whale was so dreadful that no­ body came forth to accept this challenge and to test their for­ tune. Both the king and the subjects were completely de­ jected. At this juncture, an old man mysteriously appeared from the north. Nobody knew who he was or from where he came. He approached the king, his back bowed with age, and de­ clared that he could catch the huge whale and save the fisher­ folk from complete devastation. Accompanied by the aston­ ished king and his subjects, the old man walked towards the sea. Making a long rope by using a certain type of creeper, the old man threw one end of it into the sea while holding the other end tightly in his hand. The rope made of creepers en­ circled the place where the
3: huge whale was submerged. Next the old man gave the rope to the fishermen and instructed them to pull with all their might. While pulling, the old man told the fishermen to chant a particular mantra in order to trap the whale in the rope made of creepers. Thus instructed by the old man, the native fishermen started pulling the rope while chanting the mantra taught by him. After a few hours of tremendous effort the giant fish, en­THE LEGEND IX trapped in the string made of creepers, was dragged to the shore. But to everyone’s wonderment, the whale vanished into thin air as soon as it reached the shore. The old man who had caught the whale was none other than Lord Siva. Thus Lord Subramanya had been released from the curse. A temple for Lord Subramanya was built where the giant fish had been shored. That temple stands as a living monument to remind us of the old story’. But the legend does not end there. Now Lord Siva in the guise of the old man stepped forward and stood before the king. The king and his subjects understood the meaning of his action. The king, who had promised his only daughter in marriage to the one who caught the fish, was now in a di­ lemma. Both the king and his subjects were again completely distraught. How could a father, especially the king, give his young and unsurpassingly beautiful daughter, the princess, in marriage to an old man? The king requested the old man to ask for anything else in the entire kingdom except for his daughter. The old man calmly replied that the king should keep his promise and be truthful to his word. Now the king was in a real quandry. Truth is the strength of the fisherfolk. They so firmly believe in Truth as their pro­ tector that it is said that without being truthful, if one goes fishing, it is like jumping into the wide open, fierce mouth of death. The king was unable to make any decision. He could neither break his vow nor give his beloved princess in mar­ riage to the old man. At this juncture, the princess, who was in fact Goddess Parvati Herself, stepped forward and without hesitation spoke. “Father and most noble king, it is everyone's duty to protect and preserve righteousness (dharma). Nothing should stand against it.” Both the king and the subjects were despondent to see the princess depart with the old man but no other choice remained to be seen. Little did the fisherfolk suspect that their humble fishing kingdom had become the stage for a divine drama where Lord Siva and Goddess Parvati would be reunited. The natives, with heavy hearts, followed X THE LEGEND the divine couple as they departed from the kingdom. After going a long distance the people asked, " Where are you going? We would also like to come with you." They replied, uWe don't have any particular dwelling place
4: (uru); where we reach will be our dwelling place (chellunna uru)." Lord Siva and Goddess Parvati continued on their way fol­ lowed by the fisherfolk and after a long time they at last reached a particular spot where they stopped. Lord Siva and Goddess Parvati stood facing cast and west respectively and became stone images. Chclluruna uru (the place reached) later became Chenganoor of the present day. In due course of time a temple was constructed and daily worship was commenced, but something very strange occurred at this time. Whenever water was brought to the Sanctum Sanctorum3 to perform the worship, the priests would find a fish init. Due to this occurence it became impossible to perform thedaily worship. In order to find a solution, the temple authoritiesmade an astrological calculation and discovered the whole storyof Lord Siva, Goddess Parvati and the curse of LordSubramanya. The astrological forecast further revealed that themarriage ceremonies of the old man and the princess had neverbeen conducted. According to the sociial custom, the people ofAlappad coastal area where Goddess Parvati was bom as a fish-ermaid should come with down’ and such other marriage pres­entations to Chengannoor so as to conduct the marriage. Sub­sequently the necessary preparations were arranged in Chen­gannoor and Alappad. The fisherfolk of Alappad duly as­sembled all the paraphenalia and went to Chengannoor to con­duct the divine marriage ceremony,To this day, every year dur­ing the festival season this custom is followed in memory of thisancient legend. This temple still remains a centre of attraction tothousands of devotees. A few decades ago an interesting incident took place in con­ nection with the aforesaid story. One year the people from the Alappad coastal area did not participate in the festival by ob­ serving the customary rules and preparation thinking that it wasTHE LEGEND xi meaningless and wasteful to travel all the way to Chengannoor spending a lot of money. They thought, “Why should we co-op- crate in a festival which is conducted in a distant place?” But mysterious were the happenings which immediately resulted in the Chengannoor temple. The decorated elephant which was supposed to earn" the Lord’s idol in the procession stood still and refused to take even a step. All efforts to make it move failed. Word was immediately sent to Alappad of this inauspicious oc- curence but it was too late. The symptoms of small pox had al­ ready appeared in Alappad. Realizing their foolish mistake and with deep remorse, they made their way to Chengannoor with­ out delay bringing all preparations for participating in the festi­ val as was customary. Such is the ancient lore which is intimately and unmistakea- bly intenvoven with this coastal landscape and its people. Is it a wonder then that these hallowed surroundings have again be­ come the centre stage for a divine drama?PART 1 LifeCHAPTER ONE FROM BIRTH ITSELF From birth itself I had an intense liking for the Divine Name. So much so, I would repeat the Lord's Name incessantly with every breath and
5: a constant flow of divine thoughts was kept up in my mind irrespective of the place where I was or the work I was attending to. This unbroken recollection of God with love and devotion would be of immense help to any aspirant in at­ taining Divine Realization. -Mata Amritanandamayi-The Great Saints impart sanctity to places of pilgrimage, render actions righteous and good, and give spiritual authority to the scriptures. When such a Saint is bom, the fathers rejoice, the Gods dance in joy and this Earth gets a savi­ our. NARADA BHAKTI SUTRAS VERSES 69-71 Ancestry Idamannel was an ancient family of Parayakadavu village which comprised a small part of Alappad Panchayath. Though their hereditary work was fishing, they also did other work. Part and pael of their daily life was to perform reli­ gious practices and to observe various vows. The fisherfolk were also known for their munificent nature. When they re­ turned from the sea with their day’s catch,the first thing they would do is give some fish to all the villagers who had gathered without accepting any money. After selling their day’s catch they would take a handful of coins and distribute it among all the children. Many pious souls had been born in the Idamannel family. Sri Velayudhan was such a man. He was a very compassionate. 6 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI truthful and generous person who firmly held onto the ideal of ahimsa (non-killing). He would not allow even a little rat to be killed. Velayudhan was married to Srimati Madhavi, a chaste and pious woman. It was her habit to get up early morn­ ing before dawn in order to make flower garlands for all the deities in the family shrine room. All the while she could be heard chanting the Divine Names of God. Even today, though she is in her eighties, one can still find her seated be­ fore the temple stringing garlands with the same devoted spirit Sugunanandan was their eldest son of five children. In­ spired by the devotional atmosphere of his family, he became an ardent devotee of Lord Krishna. When he was nine or ten years old he began studying Kathakali, a classical dance drama of Kerala which depicts the spottings of the Gods and Goddesses. While the actors present the story through dance and mudras (divine gestures), the singers will narrate the story through songs. Sugunanandan’s favourite character to enact was Sri Krishna. Once during a Kathakali performance he became so identified with his role of Krishna that he fell unconscious on the stage. The atmosphere around Idamannel was very peaceful and calm. On three sides it was skirted by the backwaters which were alive with wildlife and surrounded by lush vegetation consisting of coconut, fruit and cashew trees. There were very few houses nearby in those days. Coming home from school when he was a young boy of 13 or 14, Sugunanandan and his cousin would partake in their favourite pastime of climbing the cashew trees and eating
6: the delicious cashew fruits. One fine day found the two boys amidst the trees busily plucking cashews when they noticed a sannyasin1 with long hair and beard approaching Idamannel. He had never before been seen in that area and the boys were intrigued by his radi­ ant appearance. The sannyasin roamed about the property for a few minutes. All of a sudden he burst into blissful laugh­ 1 A renunciate or monk.From Birth Itself 7 ter and proclaimed loudly, “I can see many ascetics sitting im­ mersed in deep meditation in this place. Previously this was the abode of many great souls whose tombs lie under this spot. Many sannyasins will take samadhi (attain Liberation) here. This will become a holy place.” The sannyasin again burst into a rapturous laughter and continued on his way never to be seen or heard of again. Puzzled by the mendicant’s statement, the boys resumed their play. Many years would pass before Sugunanandan and his cousin would shake their heads in wonder recalling the prophetic words of the wander­ ing monk. Before long, Sugunanandan became busy in his hereditary’ work of marketing fish and when he was twenty-one he mar­ ried Damayanthi, a twenty year old girl from the neighbour­ ing village. Bhandaraturuttu. Damayanthi was born into a devout family where religious practices were performed daily without fail. Her family even had its own family temple. From childhood onwards, Damayanti led a virtuous life. Her father Punyan and mother Karutta Kunya were also exem­ plary devotees of God. In short, the whole family atmosphere was conducive for her to lead a pure, religious life. Damayanti was so pious that she was reverently called by the villagers, ‘Pattathi Amma' or the ‘brahmana lady’. Devo­ tion to God being her mainstay in life, she would observe vari­ ous religious vows almost everyday of the week. She fre­ quently undertook fasting and on such occasions she would break her fast by drinking the water of tender coconuts which would mysteriously fall from the trees. All told, thirteen children were born to Damayanti and Sugunanandan, though four died at birth and another after 53 days. The remaining children, four daughters and four sons, are named as follows: (in chronological order from eldest to youngest) Kasturbai, 2 Sunil Kumar3 , Sudhamani, 2 Henceforth referred to as Kasturi, the colloquial form of her given name. 3. Henceforth referred to as Subhagan.8 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Sugunamma, Sajani, Suresh Kumar, Satheesh Kumar, and Sudhir Kumar. Of these children, it was Sudhamani who was destined to become known throughout the world as Mata Amritanandamayi, the Mother of Immortal Bliss. It was during her fourth pregnancy that Damayanthi be­ gan having strange visions. Sometimes she would have won­ derful dreams about Lord Krishna; at other times she beheld the divine sportings of Lord Siva and Devi, the Divine Mother. One such night Damayanti dreamt that a mysterious figure came and entrusted her with an idol of Sri Krishna which was cast in pure gold. Around the
7: same time, Sugunan- andan had a dream of the Divine Mother. As he was a devotee of Lord Krishna he was unable to understand why Devi should suddenly appear to him. Upon relating this story to Damayanti, he found that she had recently many strange vi­ sions also. They were both wondering what the significance was and whether some great good fortune would soon befall them. At this time, Sugunanandan and Damayanthi were living in a small hut right on the seaside as this was more convenient for their fishing business than staying at their other hut on the Idamannel family property some five minutes walk inland. During her three previous pregnancies, Damayanthi had al­ ways experienced a swelling of her whole body a few weeks before her delivery date. This was a signal for her to relax her daily routine and return to her own family house in Bhandara- turutthu where she would be cared for during the delivery. Damayanthi was still waiting for the swelling to occur before making ready for the birth of her fourth child. One night, Damayanthi had a wonderful dream that she gave birth to Krishna and He was lying on her lap drinking her breast milk. The next morning she was at the seaside engaged in some work when suddenly she had a feeling that she was going to deliver. However, Damayanthi discounted this sen­ sation as the tell-tale swelling had not yet appeared. This strange feeling persisted though and Damayanthi put her work aside. For some reason she felt an inexplicable urge to go From Birth Itself 9 to Idamannel and, crossing the backwaters all alone, she headed inland. Reaching there, she entered the small hut and began collecting a few items. A moment later, she felt a famil­ iar sensation and realised that she was about to deliver. By the time she had spread a mat and laid down, the child had been bom! Damayanthi was shocked. She noticed that the baby was a girl. The atmosphere that enveloped the child’s birth was completely silent and peaceful. Apart from the initial sen­ sation which had alerted her, Damayanthi had not felt a thing. In fact, now that she was coming to her senses, she was filled with concern. Was the child even alive? She heard no cries typical of a newborn. Anxiously, she examined the child. Now Damayanthi was even more shocked. The babe had a beaming smile on her tiny face! The gaze of the child pene­ trated Damayanthi’s innermost heart and was never to be for­ gotten. At this moment, a woman from a neighbouring house ap­ peared in the doorway of the hut. Quickly realizing what Damayanthi’s situation was, she scurried around making her and the baby comfortable. Thus it was that on the morning of the 27th of September 1953, a tiny girl child was born in a humble hut of woven palm leaves amidst the sound of the ocean waves ever echoing upon the nearby shoreline. The
8: parents were puzzled by the babe’s dark blue complex­ ion and the fact that the child lay in padmasana4 * holding her fingers in chinmudra5 with the tip of her thumb and forefin­ ger touching to form a circle. They feared that this dark blue shade might be the symptom of some strange disease and that the peculiar posture might be due to abnormal bone structure or dislocation. Various doctors were consulted. The fear of bone abnormality was allayed after it was confirmed during the medical exam that there was no such handicap. As for the skin colour, it could not be attributed to heredity since both 4 The lotus posture of hatha yoga. 5.This mudra symbolizes the oneness of the individual self with the Supreme Self.10 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Damayanthi and Sugunanandan were light tan in skin tone. Hence, the parents were advised not to bathe the child for six months in the hope that the mysterious ailment would be cured. Yet six months passed and still the baby girl retained the dark blue hue reminiscent of Lord Krishna and Divine Mother Kali. Eventually, over the course of time, this dark­ blue changed into black. Yet, when the little girl's desire to behold the vision of Lord Krishna intensified, her skin colour once again assumed its blue hue. Even today, especially dur­ ing the Divine Moods of Krishna and Devi, one can observe this dark blue skin tone. Ironically, it was due to this blue-black hue that in the fu­ ture, Damayanthi and the other family members would look upon the child with great disdain. This aversion for the dark child would eventually become the cause of her becoming the downtrodden servant of the family and relatives. In fact, only a few close relatives were even informed of the birth as not much importance was given to this newborn child. It was a girl and Damayanthi had already given birth to three other children besides. Who could imagine that this strangely blue-coloured child born smiling benignly in a small hut along the shores of the Arabian Sea was, in fact, a spiritual giant come to this world to shower peace and godly love upon ailing humanity? Who could foresee this little one's spiritual legacy of helping thousands upon thousands of seekers to cross the Ocean of Transmigration?6 6 The metaphorical representation of the cycle of birth, death and rebirth.From the moment of the tiny girl's birth, the family began noticing unusual signs which would only be understood years later. Typically, before walking, a child will progress through various stages of development. First it will lie on its back, then roll over, then lie on the belly and push itself up with the forearms and so on. Eventually, the child begins From Birth Itself 11 crawling and after some months it will get up on its two legs by holding onto something. All this will culminate in the tod­ dling stage around one year of age. This little girl's ease was entirely
9: different as none of these stages occurred. One day, after turning six months old, the little girl suddenly stood up and straight-away walked across the verandah. Soon after this, she started running which filled everyone’s heart with wonder and joy. Ambrosial Jewel The parents gave the name Sudhamani, which means 'Ambrosial Jewel’, to their remarkable baby daughter. Un­ like most other children her age, Sudhamani started speaking her mother tongue Malayalam when she was barely six months old. Her passion to sing the Divine Names mani­ fested as soon as she began to speak fairly well. At the tender age of two, without instruction from anybody, she began saying prayers and singing short songs in praise of Sri Krishna. Needless to say, the family was quite taken aback on such occasions when they happened to overhear the little one. Over the course of the next year, Sudhamani established a daily habit of melodiously chanting the Divine Names aloud and to this day, this custom persists without interruption. Now aged four, she would sing with devotional fervour her one or two line compositions seated before her small pet por­ trait of the Lord. From her infancy, Sudhamani was full of vigour and brisk­ ness. She grew up as an obedient child and was well loved by everyone. Even unacquainted people would feel an inexpli­ cable attraction and affection for little Sudhamani. Love for God, concern for others and other such noble traits mani­ fested themselves in her from early childhood. Due to these virtuous qualities, everyone in the village would call her by the pet name 'Kunju' which means 'the little one’. Strangely enough, it would be these same qualities that later became the focal point for severe abuse and maltreatment which would be meted out to her by her own family and relatives.12 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI By the time Sudhamani turned five, a visible stream of in­ born devotion to Lord Sri Krishna started flowing from her heart and before long, this love took the form of full-fledged devotional songs. The little one’s songs were filled with poignant longing for her beloved Krishna and her enchant­ ing, soulful singing of these simple, yet at the same time deeply mystical songs, became well-known throughout the village. While chanting or singing, she would focus her eyes on a picture of Krishna which she always kept tucked inside her shirt. Then she would sit unmoving for a long time. This extraordinary behaviour and intense devotion amazed every­ one and attracted the attention of all the devout villagers. They would arise early morning just to hear the little one’s angelic singing greeting the newborn day. O Lord Who has protected Gokulam as the Dear Child of Ampati, O Lord of the Ocean of Milk, Who art the colour of clouds, O Thou with the lotus eyes, I adore Thee with joined palms... Please give relief from sin to the sinners, O Thou Who art the colour of dark clouds. Please show compassion to the poor ones
10: in this village... O Lord of the flute Who wears yellow raiment, O Thou Who wears the garland of jasmine, please come playing the flute. O Destroyer of Putana, please protect me! O the One Who reclines on a huge serpent, O Lord of Gokulam Who has prevented the torren­ tial rain, please make me one with Thy Lotus Feet thereby ridding me of the pain in my soul... Even at this age, certain visible traits of divinity were no­ ticed in Sudhamani. While engaged in childhood spottings or other daily activities, she would suddenly become indrawn ir­ respective of time or place. On such occasions, the parents or From Birth Itself 13 other family members would find her sitting motionless in some lonely, isolated place. At other times, she would be found sitting near the backwaters looking intently into the water or silently gazing at the blue sky as if transported to another world. It was not uncommon to find her sitting in solitude with closed eyes. When she was aroused, her mood would be abstracted. Unable to understand the import of these unusual states of their daughter, the parents would scold Sudhamani for not being playful like the other children. This marked the begin­ ning of a long period of malignment of their daughter and mis­ interpretation of her undaunted flight to the realm of the Divine For their part, the parents were worried that her pecu­ liar way of acting may pave the way for a psychological disor­ der. When Sudhamani turned five, she was enrolled as a stu­ dent of the first grade at Srayicadu School in the nearby vil­ lage. Even at this age, she exhibited brilliant intelligence and memory’. Having heard a lesson once, she would not forget even a portion of it. She could effortlessly recite any of the subjects which had been taught in the class or which she had read. When Sudhamani was studying in the second grade, she would easily recite the lessons of the advanced grades simply by overhearing the text when it had been read aloud. Her upper classmates, including her elder brother and sister, would sometimes receive harsh punishment from the teacher for being unable to learn verses of poetry by heart. Mean­ while, little Sudhamani, who was studying in the lower grade, would melodiously sing the poems and dance to the tune like a delicate butterfly. All the teachers liked her and were sur­ prised by Kunju’s memory the likes of which they had never seen. She scored full marks in all subjects and was ranked first in the class despite the fact that she was often absent due to household responsibilities. Another incident which illustrates Sudhamani’s remark­ able memory is as follows. One day five months after Damay­ anthi had given birth, she went outside the house and left the14 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI child under the care of Sugunanandan. For some unknown reason the babe became restless and began weeping. Unac­ customed to such behaviour, try as
11: he might, Sugunanandan could not console the child. This went on for quite some time and eventually Sugunanandan became exasperated, lost his patience and flung her on the cot. Many years later, Sudhamani remarked to her father, “Oh, the way you threw me away that day! You were about to kill me!” At first, Sugunanandan did not understand the import of Sudhamani’s words. After a few moments, the old incident flashed through his mind and he was once again astounded by his daughter’s memory’. Whatever free time Sudhamani had in school would be spent in completing her homework with the thought that after reaching home, she could utilize that much time in the remembrance of God. Returning home, the little girl would first help her mother in the household chores. Otherwise, she would forget herself singing devotional songs. Even from childhood, Sudhamani was very’ careful about the proper application of time. Never would she waste even a moment sitting idle. While attending to her steadily mount­ ing household duties, Sudhamani would always be heard chanting the Divine Name of Lord Krishna. Visualizing her beloved Krishna’s beautiful form within her heart while re­ peating His Divine Name, Kunju spent her days and nights in her own world. The house where Sudhamani spent her childhood days consisted in its entirety of two tiny rooms and a kitchen. Owing to the inconvenience caused by such tight living quar­ ters, Sugunanandan built a small room alongside the cow­ shed. This was used as a study room for the children, but it was in the cowshed that little Sudhamani spent her childhood days meditating and singing devotional songs. There were two other refugees in the same cowshed. One orphan woman 7 In The general proximity of where the old Bhava Darshan Temple now stands. From Birth Itself 15 named Potichi, who was a barber, and her child. Taking pity on their helpless condition, Sugunanandan had allowed them to stay there. Potichi, the barber woman, loved Sudha­ mani very much. She would always carry the little one on her hip and in those days it was Potichi who looked after Sudha­ mani much more than Damayanthi. Thus we find gentle Sudhamani living in the cowshed all the while focusing her heart and soul upon the enchanting form of Lord Krishna. Just as cows were very dear to Sri Krishna, the little girl also adored them. Any spare time she got would be spent sitting there in solitude lost in a divine reverie. The little one remained free to revel in the bliss of longing to behold the resplendent Vision of Krishna. On account of Sudhamani’s loving nature, she was always surrounded by children. Whenever they got a chance they would come to Idamanncl to play with her. Together, they would go to gather grass for the cows. Though Sudhamani’s little friends were not interested in doing such work, in order to enjoy her cheerful company they would happily join in. All of them had a mysterious attraction
12: and strong bond of love for her. Having completed all the work, Sudhamani would involve the children in various games and attract other chil­ dren by her enactment of Krishna Leela, the sportings of Sri Krishna as a child. Without much difficulty, she would en­ courage the whole group to sing in loud chorus the devo­ tional songs which were ever flowing through her mind. Nobody could understand Sudhamani’s devotional moods which were intensifying day by day. As weeks and months passed she became increasingly absorbed in her devo­ tional activities. Day in and day out the little one would be heard singing full of deep longing to see the divine beauty of her Lord. Her ecstatic moods became more and more fre­ quent and were not always confined to the cowshed. Oblivi­ ous to the world around her, Sudhamani would sometimes dance in ecstacy while moving in a circle and singing devo­ tional songs. The following is a song composed by Kunju at the age of seven:16 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Protect me, O Supreme Lord who resides In the city of Guruvayoor... O Child Krishna who acted as a cowherd boy, O Lord of the Universe, Consort of Goddess Lakshmi, Protect me, O Krishna, the Beloved of Radha, O Krishna, the Beloved of the Gopis, O Krishna, the son of Nanda, O Krishna who is worshipped and adored by everyone... The family and neighbours were totally ignorant about the exalted moods of little Sudhamani and took it to be her childhood sportings. Who could imagine that this seven year old girl, having received no spiritual instruction, was swim­ ming in the Ocean of Pure Love and Bliss? Lost to this world, Kunju would sometimes lock herself in a room and sing and dance in ecstacy. During one such occasion Damayanthi peeped through the door and exclaimed, “Look and see how our daughter dances’ We should give her dancing lessons!" Poor parents! They were only acquainted with worldly dance. Never had they heard or read about someone dancing in God-intoxicated bliss.Had there been somebody present who had studied the lives of the Great Souls, then perhaps they would have recognized Sudhamani’s spiritual states. Even then, who would expect to find such a rapturous state in a child at this young age? As it was, the family thought it was merely the antics of their slightly eccentric and over imagina­ tive daughter. Sudhamani’s longing to behold and merge in her Supreme Lord continued unabated. Time and time again one would find her gazing at the small portrait of Krishna which she kept safely tucked in her shirt. Pouring out her heart to Him, either in song or prayer, the little girl would cry out, “O my darling Krishna, I am seeing troubles and suffering all around me! O Krishna! Please do not forget to look after this tiny child. I am always calling You, won't You come and sport with me?”From Birth Itself 17 The following song was composed by Sudhamani at the
13: age of eight and gives a glimpse of the depth and breadth of her spiritual intensity: O Essence of Mercy, O Compassionate One, O Krishna, give me refuge! O Krishna, is the story of these burning Tears that flow out unknown to Thee? Offering flowers at Thy Feet that have Crushed the serpent Kaliya I will worship Thee, O Krishna... Thou came as the charioteer of Arjuna At Kurukshetra and protected Truth and Righteousness... O Lord Who preserves Dharma Show a bit of compassion to us! O Lord of the Gita, Lover of Divine Music, Give the capacity to sing Thy song... O Lover of devotional singing, Do You not hear Thy sacred Names Uttered from the innermost heart? the little one’s despondent-looking face and her sorrow­ ful songs captured the sympathetic hear of the villagers. But the great mystery of Sudhamani’s inner life still remained un­ known to all. Who could imagine the ecstatic jubilation of her childhood devotion? Who could understand it except for the Wise? **********CHAPTER TWO THE DIVINE SERVANT Mother is the servant of servants. She has no special place to dwell. She dwells in your heart... -Mata Amritananandamayi- O Treasure-house of Beauty! Thou who thus didst during nights confer on the love-lornGopikas that same immense and intense joy ofthe Spirit which only Yogis attain, and therebydidst make them respect-worthy even forBralnna and Shiva. May Thou, O Krishna oflovely form, accessible only to men endowedwith devotion, be pleased to protect me! SRIMAD NARAYANEEYAM CANTO 69, VERSE 11 At the age of nine, Sudhamani duly reached the fourth grade. By now she had been entrusted with many of the house­ hold duties by her chronically ailing mother. Getting up be­ fore dawn, she would attend to her duties and only after fin­ ishing the work would she hurry off to school. Returning home in the evening, whatever time was not spent in chores would be spent in prayer and meditation. Still carrying her pet portrait everywhere, she would shed tears embracing and kissing it. The picture would become drenched by her tear­ drops. Sometimes Damayanthi would go to fetch water in a dis­ tant place leaving little Sudhamani behind. Unobserved, she would follow, thinking that she may be of assistance to her 22 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI mother. If Damayanthi ever tried to prevent her from com­ ing, Sudhamani would loudly protest. Due to her stubborn nature in this matter sometimes Damayanti would even lock her up in a room. She would try to frighten the little girl say­ ing, “Here comes a ghost! It is coming to get you!” But no­ body could frighten Sudhamani. Though a small child, she was fearless and courageous from the youngest age. This fact also commanded the respect of the local villagers who already had affection for the virtuous little girl. There was a woman in the village well known for frighten­ ing small children. If children became too mischievous, the parents would call her to frighten them into obedience. Her name was
14: Appisil Amma and sometimes she was called to Idammanel to frighten little Sudhamani. This notorious woman would sneak up to the window where little Sudha­ mani was sitting. She would cover her head with a sack and jump and shriek while showing frightening gestures. Peering through the window the little one would boldly announce, “Go away, I know who you are. You are that Appisil Amma. Don't try to frighten me!" Like a gnef stricken, forsaken child Sudhamani would call on her beloved Krishna. The villagers now considered her as one who dwelled in another world. Unable to understand the reason for the little one's agony they used to sympathize with her condition saying, “What a pity! Poor child. What hap­ pened to her? Teardrops are always streaming down her cheeks. What a deplorable condition. Was she bom only to shed tears? are the family members giving her a hard time? If so, what has she done to undergo all these hardships?” Every­ one took pity on Sudhamani and some even tried to console her. But who else other than the Beloved of the Gopis could appease her unquenchable thirst for spiritual Unity? As mentioned previously, the little one’s equal vision, noble character, compassion towards all living creatures and melodious singing had endeared her to all the villagers. All those whose good fortune it was to come in contact with her would soon find themselves opening their heart to her. How­ ever, fate was not so kind when it came to her own familyThe Divine Servant 23 members. Sudhamani’s mother and elder brother were par­ ticularly at odds with the little one because of her unusual behavior. At this juncture, due to the birth of five more children Damayanthi's health completely deteriorated and she was rendered incapable of tending to any household chores. This task, already done in part by Sudhamani, now fell entirely on her shoulders. Kasturi, the eldest daughter, was studying in a local college and Subhagan, the eldest son, was also studying. Thus, it was that the whole responsibility became Sudha- mani's. This was the beginning of a life of trials and tribula­ tions for the little one. Sudhamani toiled day in and day out. Getting up as early as 3 o’clock she would set to work cleaning the house, sweeping the compound, fetching water, cooking the food, tending and milking the cows and other sundry jobs like washing the family members clothes and scrubbing the cooking vessels. Such a hard routine would have been backbreaking even for an adult not to mention poor little Sudhamani. Even to look after the family livestock and poultry was enough work for one person. Yet Sudhamani would patiently and ardently do all the work without a grumble. Due to the heavy work load, Sudhamani’s education nearly came to an end at this point. Overburdened with work the little one could not reach school in time most of the days. Somehow she would manage to discharge her duties and in a haste
15: run to class. But the classes would have already begun and as a punishment for being tardy, the teachers would make the little one stand outside the classroom. Even though she was forced to stand outside, Sudhamani would keenly attend the classes. Somehow she managed to complete the fourth grade. However, by the time she reached the fifth grade it became impossible for the little one to continue looking after the never ending stream of household chores as well as her stud­ ies. Thus at the age of ten, little Sudhamani was forced to abandon her schooling. Having done so, she became fully 24 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI involved in the domestic chores. From early morning before dawn until late in the night she would toil. Yet, even while engaged in this burdensome work, the little one was always heard singing or chanting the Divine Names of her beloved Krishna. Sometimes in the middle of a job she would become absorbed in her devotional mood and would forget the exter­ nal circumstances. As mentioned, the little one’s day began well before dawn. If ever she happened to sleep a bit more due to exhaustion, Damayanthi would not hesitate to pour a pitcher of cold wa­ ter on Sudhamani. Soon after waking up, the first thing to do was to beat the coconut husks with a pestle to turn it into a soft fibre. This would eventually be used to manufacture coir, a local product. After this the first round of work began with tidying the house and yard, fetching the water from the vil­ lage water tap located some distance away, washing the uten­ sils, cooking the meals and getting her younger brothers and sisters ready for school. The next set of work involved bathing the cows, feeding them fodder, again washing the cooking vessels after serving lunch, washing all the clothing of the family and going out to gather grass for the cows. By now it would lx * 4 o'clock in the afternoon and time for her brothers and sisters to return from school. The little one would pre­ pare a light snack and tea for them. In the course of her duties she would somehow have to find time to visit the neighbour­ ing houses to collect any vegetable scraps or rice gruel for feeding the cows. Besides this, Damayanthi instructed the little girl to discharge any of the household duties in the houses she visited if she noticed that they had not been prop­ erly attended to. Again the little one would prepare the eve­ ning meal for the family and wash the vessels. No one would ever lend any assistance to the poor little girl; she was now considered to be the servant and these were her duties. Damayanthi's sharp eyes would scrutinize each and every action and work of the little one. If any error, how­ ever slight, was detected, there would come swift punish- The Divine Servant 25 ment. Sudhamani’s only friend was Krishna, the Name
16: that she would never forget. Her sole inspiration was that Name. Even while engaged in the various tasks, the intense thought of her beloved Lord would bring tears to her eyes and she would cry and cry for hours together. Finally Sudhamani’s day would come to an end around eleven in the night. This was the only time that the innocent girl had for taking rest. But not to rest lying on the bed, rather to rest in herself...that is to call on her Lord. When everybody was fast asleep, the little one would sit in the small family shrine room pouring her heart out to Lord Krishna through devotional singing. In the darkness of the night, Sudhamani would cry, heartbroken and sing until she would finally fall asleep. O Krishna, please show compassion to me! O Lord Vishnu, I adore Thee with joined palms! You please rid me of the burden of speech, Mind and body! You please protect me with affection! O Krishna, do You, Who are the Friend of the Miserable, not have even a bit of compassion? Are You dwelling only in the golden temple? Have Thy shining eyes become dim? O Ocean of Compassion, Thou art affectionate towards the devotees! Thy Feet are the Eternal Support! Even in those days, Sudhamani’s mind was so high flying that it would soar to the heights of divinity at any moment in­ spired by any heart-capturing sight or song. One day, the little one went to the market to make some purchases. While returning home, she heard the strains of a devotional song coming from some distant place. Without a second thought, attracted by the song and in a semi-conscious mood, Sudha-26 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI mani turned and walked in the direction of the singing. Reaching that place, it turned out that the source of the heart­ capturing song was a Christian family where somebody had died that day. The relatives were sitting around the corpse singing hymns in a lone full of sorrow. The little one’s heart was immediately stolen and she became lost to the world standing motionless in a God-intoxicated mood. Her eyes were closed and tears rolled down her cheeks. The purchased goods fell from her hands and the people gathered there knew not what to make of this unknown girl’s sudden transforma­ tion mistakenly thinking that she was also moved by the death of their relative. One half hour passed like this before Sudhamani partially regained her normal consciousness. Picking up the fallen pur­ chases, she hurriedly continued on her way home. But it was too late. As expected, Damayanthi was standing there in a huff. In a fit of fury she chastised Sudhamani and severely beat her. The little one was still in an indrawn state and received Damayanthi’s harsh treatment silently and unmoved. What external force can distract a mind which is absorbed in God? Besides her exceptional brilliance, undaunted cheerful­ ness, exemplary devotion and soul-stirring singing, Sudha­ mani was known most of
17: all for her uncompromising compas­ sion for the poor and needy. Although Sudhamani did her best to assist her mother in the household chores, Damayan­ thi, who was hot-tempered by nature, would not hesitate to severely punish her for the slightest error. As previously mentioned, there was a particular reason for Damayanthi’s aversion to little Sudhaman, i.e., her dark complexion. Be­ sides that, sometimes Damayanthi would catch the little one sneaking off with butter, milk and curd just like the notorious butter thief Sri Krishna. What Damayanthi didn’t discover for some time was that these edibles were being supplied to the starving families which Sudhamani would befriend. Unnoticed by others, the little one would surrepticiously sneak off with milk and curds after replacing the pilfered quantity with water. If discovered, she would get a good beat­The Divine Servant 27 ing. Oftentimes the little one's compassionate tendency was exploited by her brothers and sisters who would also steal ed­ ibles, but for themselves, and then accuse Sudhamani. Though she knew who the real thief was, she would not utter a word against them. Silently she would endure the harsh blows which were her lot. If Sudhamani came to know of any family which was starv­ ing she would snitch money from her mother's little collec­ tion box so that the necessary purchases could be made. If this was not possible, then she would obstinately pester her father until he would give her a bit of money. If these two means failed to secure the needed amount then the little one would take raw foodstuffs from her own family’s meagre storeroom and give it to the family in need. Except for certain childhood pranks and lighter moods, all mischiefs of Sudhamani had a selfless intention as the basis. Such actions were the result of her inborn compassion for the suffering masses. However, such charitable service did not endear her to Damayanthi who was quick to inflict a harsh dose of corporeal punishment. Regardless of the sacrifice and suffering which she had to undergo, the little one found immense satisfaction and bliss in giving peace and extending help to others. She was not at all deterred from these benevo­ lent practices despite the punishment. She would never let others know the renunciation which she had to endure in order to render her services to the poverty stricken villagers. Most of the time Sugunanandan would be absent from Idamannel for days together in connection with his fishing business returning only late in the night when all the children were fast asleep. On such occasions the first thing that Damayanthi would do was enumerate the accusations against little Sudhamani. On one such occasion Sudhamani, who was pretending to be asleep suddenly shouted out, I am not your daughter, I must be your daughter-in-law! " Damay­ anthi was taken aback hearing Sudhamani’s replay. The little one's implication was clear; she was reminding Damayanthi that a real mother would patiently forgive the faults of her 28 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI
18: daughter whereas only a mother-in-law would so meticu­ lously report the mistakes of a daughter-in-law exaggerated ten times over at that Who could imagine that the virtuous little Sudhamani’s insatiable appetite for relieving the sorrow and suffering of the people would soon bring the whole world flocking to the distant shores of the Arabian Sea just as one dying of thirst seeks out the oasis? How can one grasp, let alone fully appre­ ciate, the fact that Sudhamani, not yet aged 10 years, was cre­ ating an impact in this remote fishing village which was des­ tined to be felt the world over? Though she discharged her duties with utmost sincerity, her mother would not spare the rod even for the slightest mistake. If Sudhamani showed any laxity in the work Damay- anthi would warn her saying, “Hey girl, don't be lazy! If you sit idle God won’t give you any work and at last you will die out of starvation. Always pray to God, ‘O God, please give me work’. That is how everyone Pray’s.” Hearing these words, Sudhamani thenceforth would pray, "O Krishna, please do give me work, please give me Your work!” The patience, forbearance and sacrifice which Sudhamani showed was incredible. Her capacity to take everything in stride while incessently’ remembering her Beloved foreshad­ owed the arrival of one more Great Soul in India’s unbroken legacy’ of God-Realized Saviours. Although the little one had to undergo countless ordeals of life and was tortured piti­ lessly, she took everything as the benevolence of Divine Providence. All her sorrow was kept within and confided only in the Divine Flute Player, Lord Krishna. In the dark of the night behind the dosed doors of the fam­ ily shrine room, she would pray to Krishna with tear-filled eyes, "O my Beloved Krishna, nobody except You can under­ stand my heart. This world is full of sorrow and suffering. Selfishness reigns supreme. People seek only the own happi­ ness and pleasure. My darling Kanna,1 I desire nothing else 1. A name of Krishna.The Divine Servant 29 except complete oneness with You. O Lord, didn’t You see my suffering today? O Lord, please come! Let me see Your Divine Form! These miseries are nothing for me, but the sepa­ ration from You is heartbreaking.” The following is a song composed by Sudhamani during this period: O Embodiment of Compassion Who art of black Colour, deign to open Thy eyes. Art Thou not the Destroyer of Sorrow? This being so, do remove my sufferings! In this world Thou art the shelter, O bright-coloured One with eyes like the petals Of a red lotus. I worship Thee forever With the flowers of my tears, O Krishna... O Gopala, Enchanter of the mind, I am groping in the darkness. O Thou Who fillest the 14 worlds, O Sridhara. Open Thy eyes and rid me of sorrow... Thus, three years of intense yearning and ordeals passed. Sudhamani, now aged thirteen, still worked hard. It seemed that as
19: she grew older, her responsibilities also grew. With­ out uttering a complaint, she continued her struggle as be­ fore. At the same time, her spiritual practices also became more and more intense. One could see the little one’s lips al­ ways moving while chanting the Divine Name. Internally and externally the Sacred Name flowed like a never ending stream. Life With the Relatives Servants were not available in the coastal area for kitchen work and other such domestic chores as there was much more profitable work such as sewing fishing nets and manufactur­30 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI ing coir. Not only that, the fisherfolk considered any job other than their hereditary work of fishing as something dis­ graceful. Because of this, any girl who had discontinued her studies would work day in and day out doing all the domestic chores. Besides that, they would often be sent to their rela­ tives' houses to render service. It was the custom for the rela­ tives to ask the parents of such girls for their services. And so it was with Sudhamani. Her relatives insisted that she be given in service to their households. Finally, giving into their persistent disturbance regarding this matter, Sudhamani’s parents were forced to send her to the home of her maternal grandmother. Thus it came to pass that Sudha­ mani spent the next four years playing the role of domestic servant in various relatives' homes. Sudhamani’s grandmother lived six kilometers to the south of Parayakadavu. The name of that place was Bhandara- turuttu. One could reach there either by travelling in a boat along the backwaters or by walking along the shore of the Arabian Sea. As one could imagine, either of these venues would have an intoxicating effect on little Sudhamani. Rid­ ing in the ferry boat, she would gaze ar the blue sky and shed tears of joy thinking of the blue-coloured Krishna. It became her habit to chant ‘Aum’ in consonance with the humming boat engine. She would focus her attention on the small ripples dancing on the water while visualizing the form of her Beloved there and imagining His divine sports. Before long she would enter into a state of devotional fervour and her soft ‘Aum’ would be transformed into devotional singing. Her fellow boat passengers would greatly enjoy the little one's melodious singing and were not at all surprised by her con­ duct as they had always considered her to be one from another world. Due to such practices, Sudhamani would never feel the distance or boredom of the travel. But the joy of the boat journey was short-lived. One day when she asked her mother for the boat fare Damayanthi was quick to scold, “Who are you to travel in a boat? Are you a col­ lege girl? For you it is enough to walk." It was during this timeThe Divine Servant 31 that Kasturi had commenced her college studies, a rarity among the coastal area. Damayanthi was thus a bit proud of this fact
20: and would give enough money for all her daily ex­ penses. For a parent’s child to become a college student was really a great thing as most of the families were far too poor to send their children for advanced studies. Even if the parents could afford to send their children, often there was such a lack of interest and initiative that the idea was quickly abandoned. Such being the case it was no surprise that Damayanthi was showing a bit of vanity. Sudhamani, being black and a mere servant, was unno­ ticed, uncared for and completely misunderstood by her own family. Yet even at this young age one could find her residing peacefully amidst prejudice and poverty' full of nothing else but Krishna. She was not at all displeased by the rude behavi­ our of her mother. Quite the contrary! The little one was happy to walk along the seashore to her grandmother's house. Singing and dancing in ecstacy alone in the solitude, what a blessing! The six kilometre walk became an inexpress­ ible experience for the little Sudhamani who considered the ocean to be her own mother. One can easily imagine the little one walking along the shore loudly singing while the ocean waves kept up the tune. Forgetting the outer world, soon her steps would become slower and slower. The sight of the dark blue ocean and blue- grey storm clouds overhead would steal away her mind. The roaring sound of the ocean resembled ‘Aum' and invariably had a divinely intoxicating effect on Sudhamani. Seeing Krishna in the waves, sometimes she would try to embrace them! The ocean breeze was nothing but the soft caress of Sri Krishna. Sometimes she would cry aloud, “Krishna! Krishna .’".Getting deeply absorbed in that supreme state of devotion, she would move along the sand with faltering steps. Eventually, she would lose all external consciousness and fall to the sand. Partially regaining her normal consciousness, Sudhamani would burst into tears and pray, ‘Kanna, my dear Krishna.32 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI come running! Where have You gone leaving me here? Why did You leave me on this unknown shore? Where am I? O Be loved Krishna, come running before the waves of this Ocean of Transmigration swallow me up! O Krishna, uplift this des­ titute from the sand of pleasures. Are You not the Saviour of Your devotees? Are You not knowing my heart’s pain? What mistake have I committed for You to allow me to suffer like this? O Lord of all worlds, won't You show a little bit of com­ passion to this humble servant of Yours? Daily I am waiting to hear the melodious sound of Your divine flute playing. O Krishna, please come...please come!!” After some time, she would return to a manageable state of mind and continue on her way along the seashore singing in ecstacy. Again the little one would be seen falling several more times on the sand oblivious to the world around her. O Krishna, Ocean of Compassion,
21: Life’s miseries are ever increasing. There is no peace far the mind... Alas, confusion is so much... Forgiving all wrongs, Wipe the sweat from my brow. O Kanna, now I have no support other than Thy worshipful Lotus Feet... O Krishna, the throat is drying up, The eyes are failing, The feet are tired, I am falling to the ground, O Krishna... In this way, drinking the nectar of Supreme Love and De­ votion, Sudhamani would somehow reach her grand­ mother’s house only to confront a back-breaking workload. Yet, the young girl would contentedly chant the Names of Lord Krishna and carry on with her labours. As far as the little one was concerned, each and every moment of life whether The Divine Servant 33 positive or negative was considered to be an opportunity given by the Supreme Lord to serve and remember Him. Occasionally, Sudhamani would be sent to a grain mill some distance from her grandmother’s house in order to husk rice paddy. Cheerfully she would make the journey all the while singing her favorite devotional songs. On the way to the granary, the little one had to pass through a section of the vil­ lage where many families lived in the midst of dire poverty. Sudhamani, whose very' nature was compassion, would feel very dejected seeing their plight. While returning home after husking the paddy she used to give some rice away to those families who had been starving for days together. Sometimes her grandmother would detect the missing portion of rice and would think that Sudhamani had sold it in exchange for a snack. The grandmother would scold and beat the little one. However much Sudhamani was pressed she would never di­ vulge the name of the family to whom the rice had been given. She thought that if she told it the grandmother would surely go and quarrel with them. While staying at Bhandaraturuttu, Sudhamani would sometimes be sent to guard the newly sown paddy fields from crows and poultry. The field was in a distant place and, as was the habit of the little one, she turned every situation into a chance to remember God. This duty was a rare chance for her to be away from all family members and to spend time in soli­ tude remembering and praying to the Lord. Each and every breath would carry’ the sound of ‘Krishna’ with it. Each step would be taken remembering His Divine Form. Due to the intensity’ of her love and devotion, sometimes the little one would be found collapsed near the field crying and crying in ecstacy. One great solace for Sudhamani was that her grandmother was a devotee of Lord Krishna. There was a portrait of Krishna hanging on the wall which Sudhamani would stand in front of and sing devotional songs for a long time until duty' called her. During such occasions, her uncle Ratnadasan, who loved the little one very much, would bring a stool for her 34 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI
22: to sit on while praying so that she did not have to stand for such a long time. Then the little one would say, uOh, how can I sit when Krishna is standing!’’ The portrait of the Lord was not a painted piece of paper for her, it was Krishna standing there in flesh and blood. For a true devotee there is no such thing as inert matter, everything is the manifested glory of the Lord. Attracted by the heart capturing songs of Sudhamani, the neighbours used to come and listen to her. The little one’s ex­ alted way of singing would fill their minds with love and devo­ tion. Gradually, they also learned Sudhmani’s compositions and would sing them in their own shrine room. To prevent the cast of the evil eye,2 Sudhamani's uncle would smear sacred ash which had been imbued with special prayers on the fore­ head of his niece. 2 A superstitious belief of this area.Autumn, winter, spring and summer passed one by one. Sudhamani was now fourteen. She was sent to the house of Damayanthi’s elder sister. As usual the litrle one toiled under a heavy work load all on her own. First she would boil the un­ husked rice paddy and then dry it in the sun. The duties of cooking, cleaning and washing all the clothes were hers as well. All the children of this family were in college and consid­ ered it a shameful thing to do any household work. They had no faith in God and would tease the little one mercilessly for her devotional attitude. They would try to prevent her from singing devotional songs. Poor Sudhamani! What could she do against these heartless people? Hiding her face in her hands she would burst into tears when they finally temporar­ ily succeeded in stopping her singing. Though externally si­ lenced, no one could check the incessant flow of her heart towards her Beloved. Due to the nearness of the ocean, all the waters surround­ ing the house were salty. In order to fetch drinking water, Sudhamani would row a small boat across the backwaters to the distant fresh water tap. Sometimes she would even act as boatman and fern’ her relatives’ children across the backwa­The Divine Servant ' 35 ters to school and college. With great pleasure she would ferry other people’s children as well. Sitting in the canoe on the return trip, the little one would greatly enjoy the natural scenery. Crying to her Lord without any hindrance, her longing heart’s desire to see Krishna would increase tremendously on such occasions. She would converse with the tiny ripples rising up on the surface of the river saying, "O little waves, did any of you see my Krishna Who is the colour of a dark blue storm cloud? Have you ever heard the sweet, melodious music of His enchanting flute?” Seeing the little waves still rising up on the water Sudhamani would think that this was a negative reply. She would
23: burst into tears thinking, Oh, these little waves are also in deep agony like me without seeing Krishna.' The little one per­ ceived the reflection of her own excruciating pain of separa­ tion everywhere. Looking at Nature and crying aloud, she would implore, dark blue clouds of the infinite sky, where did you hide my beloved Krishna? O white cranes swiftly flying across the sky, are you going to Vrindavan?3 If you happen to meet Krishna, please tell Him of this poor little girl who is always shedding tears thinking of Him!” Soon, Sudha­ mani would lose all external consciousness and sit in the boat motionless like a statue. Slowly regaining her normal state of awareness the little one would find herself still sitting in the boat which had drifted hither and thither. Because of these exalted spritual moods which would occur irrespective of time and place, there were occasions when Sudhamani had to face great dangers which could have easily stolen away her life. The following incident occurred while staving at her aunt’s house. One day little Sudhamani had finished the job of husking paddy and began rowing her tiny canoe back to the house. While rowing she looked around and enjoyed the natural scenery. Gazing at the sky she saw the dark blue clouds mov- 3.The place where Sri Krishna spent His childhood, where even todaycountless of His devotees stay.36 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI ing across the horizon. This sight filled her innocent heart with the sweet memory of her beloved blue-coloured Krishna. The next instant the little one lost all outward aware­ ness and was merged in samadhi.4 The oar fell from her hands. Her eyes were fixed on the sky and oblivious to the surround­ ings, she remained sitting there in complete stillness. Now and then she would call out, 'Krishna, Krishna’ but remained entirely lost to the world. The canoe moved into the current and was carried on a haphazard course. At that moment the sound of a large engined boat was heard. It was heading on a collison course with Sudhamam's tiny boat! The passengers in the big boat shouted in alarm and made a vain effort to arouse the little girl. People standing on the banks of the river shouted and some threw stones in the water around the boat. Just at the last moment the little one partially regained exter­ nal consciousness and somehow managed to steer her boat clear of danger. 4 An advanced state of meditation when one’s consciousness is completely unified with the Supreme Consciousness.Another year passed and Sudhamani was now sent to the house of Damayanthi's eldest brother Anandan.
