With utmost sincerity and enthusiasm Sudhamani discharged her daily chores much to the delight of Anandan and his wife. Anandan's wife even gave the little one a pair of earrings as a reward for whole­ heartedly performing her duties. Compassion for the poor and needy was
24: one of the out­ standing features of the little one's character. Whether she was in her uncle's house, her aunt's house or her own family home, nothing could stop the little one from extending help to those in need. Many Muslim families lived around her uncle's house and most of them were very poor. The little one would pocket various articles from her uncle’s house and se­ cretly give these items to them. At first nobody noticed, but after some time she was discovered. They developed an aver­The Divine Servant 37 sion towards the little one and on several occasions Sudha­ mani received a heating from her auntie. Yet she never felt offended by her aunt’s actions. She thought, ‘Why should I feel offended? Aversion comes only when I take myself to be different from them. I never consider them to be separate. Even when I was in my own house they would beat me. Then what is new if I receive such treatment here as well?’ Although severely beaten on several occasions, the little one was not to be discouraged from showing mercy’ towards the afflicted. She never stopped her habit of giving things to others. Such incidents show the immense patience, compas­ sion and forbearance which were inseparable from Sudha- mani’s very nature. Every event in her life can be taken as a les­ son and she also took it as such. Her future message of love was the continuation of a great and unique sacrifice which she undertook as her way of life. Sudhamani’s sharp intellect would easily penetrate any­ thing and everything to extract the essential spiritual prin­ ciples. All the trials and tribulations which she had to un­ dergo would later be described as rare blessings bestowed by the Supreme to make her understand the ephemeral nature of the world and its relations. Later she told, “From all these experiences I clearly understood that the world is full of sorrow. We have no true relations or relatives as everyone loves us only to fulfill their own selfish needs. Human beings love each other out of desire. Nobody loves us selflessly. Only God has such selfless love.” The little one clearly understood that to continue a close association with these people would be a great obstacle to her supreme goal in life. Finally she created a set of circumstances which would free her from this bondage. One morning she heatedly quarrelled with this family just to break the agree­ ment and left the house. The relatives were so crude that they even took back all the gifts which they had presented to her previously, including the carrings. The little girl was sent home empty handed. Before leaving Sudhamani told, “One day a situation will arise where you will have to come to me begging. Until then I will never enter this house.”38 The Divine Servant Later, after eleven years had passed, financial troubles plagued her uncle's family and they came to Idamannel re­ questing assistance from Sudhamani.
25: Only then did she re­ turn to their house to perform the worship and thereby be­ stow her blessing. On that day, Sudhamani’s aunt repented the past events and said, “Oh, I never imagined that the little one would become so great. How ruthlessly I scolded and beat her!” The Supreme Lord is always keen to fulfill the promise of His true devotee. Even in the Great Epics of India we can find many such incidents which exemplify this. God is truly the servant of His devotees. * * * * * * * * * * * CHAPTER THREE TEARS FOR KRISHNA Having no butter and milk to offer to Thee, I will offer Thee a little of my pain. O Kanna, at Thy Feet I will offer the pearl drops of my tears. -Mata Amritanandamayi-Sri Bhagavan Uvacha: The Blessed Lord said: Those who, fixing their mind on Me, worship Me, ever steadfast, and endowed with supreme sraddha, they in My opinion are the best versed in Yoga. Fix thy mind on Me only, place thy intellect in Me; (then) than shalt no doubt live in Me here­ after. Srimad Bhagavad Gita Chapter 12, Verses 2 & 8 Return to Idamannel Returning to Idamannel from her uncle's house, Sudha­ mani. now aged around sixteen, became fully immersed in her spiritual practices while simultaneously attending to the mountainous burden of household chores. Even in this land of India the little one's tremendous passion for spiritual aus­ terity against the greatest odds stands unique and unparal­ leled. 42 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Her work, as it had always been, was worship to the Su­ preme. If anybody had seen Sudhamani in those days they would have gasped with wonder. How could her little body withstand such a heavy work load? Damayanti became even more hot-tempered and cruel due to her chronic rheumatism which had been aggravated by attending to the housework during Sudhamani’s absence. Moreover, Sudhamani’s com­ passionate nature, which was the basis for her stealing things from the relatives’ houses, had brought her a bad name. This redoubled Damayanthi’s animosity towards Sudhamani. Thus it was that even if the little one faultlessly discharged her household duties, Damayanthi would scold and beat her. Despite such cruel treatment from her own mother, the little one held no grudge against her. In fact, years later Sudhamani would reverentially refer to Damayanthi as her spiritual Guru. Following are Sudhamani’s own words: “Damayanthi Amma was, in a way, my Guru. She incul­ cated diligence, devotion and discipline in me. She observed all of my actions meticulously. If there was even a little rub­ bish left over in the courtyard after sweeping she would beat me. When all the vessels were washed, she would scrutinize them and if there was even a slight trace of dirt, she would scold me. While sweeping the ground, if even a single stick of the broom happened to fall down inadvertently, she would not spare me. If even a speck of dust or
26: ash happened to fall into the cooking pot punishment would follow. Mother, who expected her daughters to do their prayers early in the morn­ ing, would not hesitate to pour a pot of water in our faces, particularly on mine, if we were late to rise due to exhaustion. When I plucked grass for the cows, she would watch me from a distance to see if I indulged in gossip with others. She would even beat me with a wooden pestle used for pounding rice. Witnessing such actions of my mother, even the local people would tell her, 'Please do not punish her like this. She will have to be given in marriage some day!' Bur all the while I realized that such experiences were only for my own good."Tears For Krishna 43 Readers of the Holy Mother’s biography may fee! a bit aghast by Damayanthi’s cruel behaviour towards her own daughter, especially as Damayanthi was previously depicted as a pious lady. Actually, this is easy to understand when seen in the light of devotion which is not based on knowledge. Many devotees of God have strong faith and reverence for the Gods and Goddesses and regularly perform religious rites, but their concept of God does not extend any further. God is not perceived as dwelling in all beings, but rather He is limited to the four walls of the temple. Such devotees perform reli­ gious rites with an aim to fulfill their desires or to satisfy God. Their view of religion and worship has nothing to do with character building or eradication of their own negative ten­ dencies. These devotees have no desire to realize God or Self as their Supreme Goal. They worship God because their fa­ thers have done so or out of fear of sin. However, there is an­ other category’ of devotees who perceive God as all-pervasive and serve Him as such. They renounce everything and surren­ der their whole being at the Lotus Feet of the Lord. Their ul­ timate goal in life is to realise and become one with the Su­ preme Reality. Damayanthi belonged to the first category of devotees and thus there is no wonder if she had a limited vision of God and spirituality'.This was reflected in her harsh actions cowards the little one. Sometimes when Damayanthi was about to beat Sudha­ mani for some reason or other, the little one would catch hold of her hand. Though small, Sudhamani was very strong. Un­ able to release her hand from Sudhamani’s grip, she would then try to kick her. The little one would play her role most effectively and catch hold of Damayanthi’s leg as well. Much more interesting was Damayanthi’s next move. Finding no other way to punish her daughter, the mother would bite her. There are even occasions when Damayanthi would hack Sudhamani with a machete used to open coconuts. The mother would pile vulgar abuse upon the innocent little one without restraint.44 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Sudhamani could be very
27: bold and impertinent at times when it came to dealing with her mother. Sometimes if Damayanthi demanded, “Don’t speak!" she would immedi­ ately retort, “I will speak!" If Damayanthi told, “Don't do it!" Sudhamani would unhesitatingly answer back, “I will do it!" But the more she retaliated, the harsher would be the punish­ ment. Damayanthi would curse her daughter, “Let this defi­ ant girl be damned!” She would continue, “If she grows up like this, she will surely bring a bad reputation to the family. O God, why are You not putting an end to her life?” For her part, Sudhamani was not at all bothered by her mother’s antagonistic way of raising her. In the little one’s eyes all people were seen equally. Even from childhood, she would call elderly men as ‘father’ and all elderly women ‘mother’. This further irritated her parents who considered her peculiar way of addressing others as a disgrace to the fam­ ily. They would scold her saying, “Is it proper for you to call all these dirty people your father and mother?” Sudhamani’s reply would come, “I have never seen my real Father and Mother. Therefore all are my father and mother.” The little one was prohibited from applying sacred ash to her forehead. The family members would taunt her saying, “Hey girl, are you going to become a sannyasin?" Sudhamani was not even permitted to dress like ordinary girls? If she applied Vermillion1 to her forehead or wore a chequered blouse or clean jacket, punishment would quickly follow. Mockingly they would ask her, “Why do you wear these coloured clothes and apply the Vermillion? Who are you mak­ ing a show for?" They would instruct her, “Girls should live with utmost politeness." 1 A sacred red marking worn by Hindus.What was more amazing than their deplorable treatment was Sudhamani’s unwavering tolerance for whatever befell her. Though at times she was saucy, one could never find an iota of hatred behind it. Later when asked the little one would just say, “Damayanthi was not punishing me. It was only dueTears For Krishna 45 to her limited vision that she treated me poorly. All those trials led me along the correct path. Therefore, I have no hatred towards her.” As if this was not enough suffering for poor Sudhamani, God blessed her with an elder brother, Subhagan, who was a veritable terror not only to Sudhamani but to the family and villagers as well. He was an avowed atheist and further held that women should be absolutely quiet and reserved. His hot temper was well known and Sudhamani became his frequent victim. He would not allow Sudhamani to make friends with any girls her own age as he held the strong conviction that this would ruin her character. If Sudhamani went to fetch fresh drinking water for the family she would always go alone. If she happened to meet up with some other girls she could count on receiving a severe beating from Subhagan. As
28: it was, this rule was much to the little one’s liking. She much rather preferred to be alone remembering God without any hindrance. In those days there was only one water tap for the whole village and it ran on wind power. There would always be a long queue for getting water from the pipe and each one would wait their turn. The village women would gather there with their clay pots and Sudhamani was not an exception. Some­ times the villagers would have to stand there for hours to­ gether waiting for the wind to blow. If there was a long queue Sudhamani would have to leave her pots in the queue in order to go and gather grass for the cows. The other ladies, knowing the little one’s piety and hard-working nature, would lov­ ingly fill her pot and keep it aside for her. As mentioned previously, Sudhamani would visit the neighbouring houses to collect vegetable scraps and rice gruel for the cows. If there was any delay she would enter the shrine room and sing a few devotional songs or meditate. Then she would spend some time with the elderly ladies of the house. Sudhamani would lovingly inquire about their well being and listen intently to their tales of woe. It seemed that even their own children would maltreat and neglect them just because of their old age and infirmity. Thus Sudhamani wit-46 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI nessed early in life the transience and selfishness of human relationships. Whenever possible the little one would take these old women home with her and give them a good hot water bath, a nourishing meal and dress them in her family members clothes. If the little one came to know of anyone who was going without food she would at least give them the raw foodstuffs from her own house. Sometimes she would bring home small children who she would find wandering around. They were not being properly attended or nourished by their own par­ ents. Seeing this Sudhamani would extend the necessary care before returning them home. One day Sudhamani was caught red-handed while taking some foodstuffs to be given to a poor man. Though severely beaten, she would not desist from her compassionate activi­ ties. She continued in the same way unknown to her parents whenever she saw that help was needed. On another occasion Sudhamani saw a family starving for want of food. Finding nothing else, she gave them a gold bangle belonging to her mother which they could sell for enough money to acquire the desperately needed food. When her father came to know of this, in a fit of fury he tied her to the trunk of a tree and merci­ lessly beat her until her tender body bled. Though treated like this, the little one remained courageous and forgiving. She would be overheard praying for God to pardon the terrible actions committed out of ignorance by her misguided rela­ tives. Sudhamani would think while sitting in solitude,
29: "O Krishna, what kind of world is this? Even the mother who gives birth treats the child with no kindness. Even she has no pure love for her own family. Where can I find selfless and pure love in this world? Is any love that I see truly genuine? Is it not a mere delusion?’ Sometimes sitting in the family shrine room she would burst into tears thinking of all this and would cry out loud, “Krishna, Krishna! I have none in this world but You. My mind is always running after You yearning to see Your Divine Form. Will You not take me with You? O Krishna, please come running!” 'Tears For Krishna 47 During this period an elderly man who was a distant rela­ tive of the family came to live at Idamannel. He no longer had any family or relatives and was in very poor health. He was unable to even walk. Being completely bedridden his nature calls were done on the bed itself. Without being asked, Sudha­ mani immediately began attending to this old man and took complete responsibility for serving him. The other family members would barely look at the old man let alone take up any of the workload. Thus, in addition to her domestic chores we find the little one sincerely and patiently attending to his needs. She would wash his clothes, give him a hot water bath each day, remove his excreta and urine, and administer the prescribed medicines at the proper time. Though Sudhamani manifested an extraordinary abundance of noble virtues, no one in the family took any notice much less understood or appreciated the little one's all-embracing attitude towards life. It is nothing other than divine paradox that the little one received only heaps of abase for any and all that she did. While she was doing her work it was Sudhamani’s habit to remember Krishna by pretending as though she herself was Krishna, Radha, the Gopis or someone else associated with Krishna’s life. Sometimes while doing the cooking her heart would be filled with the form of Krishna’s mother, Yashoda, who would churn the milk and nurse her babe Krishna. While Sudhamani readied her brothers and sisters for school, she would imagine that she was adorning Krishna, Balarama and the Gopas2 before they went out to graze the cows. Perceiving all this with her inner eye she would shed tears of joy. When the little one went to the market to purchase household goods she recalled the Gopis who used to walk along the streets of Vrindavan selling milk and butter. Instead of calling out, “Milk, butter...” they would call out “Krishna, Madhava, Govinda, Achyuta...!” So intense was their devotion! 2 The cowherd boys of Vrindavan.48 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI The Gopis’ pure love and devotion for Sri Krishna was always a source of great inspiration for Sudhamani. At times she would imagine herself to be Radha, the beloved of Sri Krishna. The mere thought of Radha was enough to steal the
30: little one’s mind and soon her awareness of the outer world ebbed away. She would become totally absorbed in that di­ vine mood and would sing, dance and shed tears in estacy. O dark-coloured One, My eyes are pitifully burning for the sight of Thy Feet. O lotus-eyed One, come running With the cows and the music of the flute. For how many days have I been calling Thee? Hast Thou not even a bit of compassion? What great error have I committed? Art Thou not the Lover of devotees? Before I fall down crying deign to come with Thy Flute, Unable to live as I am without seeing Thee Who art the sole Reality, come, come... Fulfil ler of desires, Cause of all, O dark-coloured One, come, come... Without wasting time and increasing my sor­ row. O Embodiment of Compassion, come, come... While going to fetch water, Sudhamani would remember the Gopis who would go to the river Yamuna carrying pots on their head to collect water. As the little one washed her fam­ ily’s clothes she would sing devotional songs and imagine that she was washing the silken robes of Krishna and the Gopis. After hanging the clothes to dry, Sudhamani would watch them fluttering in the wind and think, ‘O, see how beautifully Krishna’s golden yellow silk robes are dancing in the breeze!' While collecting grass for the cows and feedingTears For Krishna 49 them she would intensely think of Krishna who daily acted as shepherd boy to the cows in the meadows and forests of Vrindavan. Sudhamani would revel in the memory of the sportings of the divine Cowherd Boy and the Gopis. Sudhamani’s favourite time of the day was dusk, when she would wade through the backwaters looking for the domes­ tic animals like ducks, goats and cows which had gone astray during the day. While doing so she would remember Krishna who used to search for the cows and calves which had wan­ dered away from the herd. If she heard any devotional sing­ ing, which is quite common in India during the twilight hour, she would stand motionless, transported to another realm. Occasions were not rare when this would happen and then it would be an irritated family member who would go out searching for the little one. Though one would always find Sudhamani engaged in some work or other, her mind was not at all in the work. The little one’s mind was filled with longing and always chasing after Krishna. His Sacred Names were ever on her lips and the very word ‘Krishna’ would bring tears to her eyes. Because the little one was always carrying water, washing the family's clothes or wading through the backwaters, her simple gar­ ment would remain drenched all the 24 hours. In her own words, “I wanted so badly to see my clothes dry! Even though I had so much work I would pray to God for more so that I could be ever busy in
31: dedicating my actions to Him. Carrying water for cooking and steaming pots of rice gruel on my head, the hair on that spot fell out owing to the weight and heat of the pots.” Whatever Sudhamani was doing, one could notice her lips moving. Nobody understood that this was due to the little one’s incessant repetition of the Lord’s Name. One day her younger brother Satheesh, who had picked up the elder fam­ ily members’ habit of verbally abusing Sudhamani, cuttingly remarked, “Always moving one's lips is a symptom of mad­ ness!” Though she overheard Satheesh’s comment, Sudha­ mani was unperturbed. Nevertheless when Satheesh would 50 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI have a severe asthma attack Sudhamani would carry him on her hip all the way to the hospital, even though there were others among the family who could have more easily done so. Nobody would care about his asthmatic condition except for the innocent girl who was always waiting for a chance to self­ lessly serve and extend help to others. It would be late in the night when Sudhamani finished her chores. Not a single light remained burning in her own house or the neighborhood. Even at this late hour Sudhamani would loudly sing to her Lord in the family shrine room. Damayanti and Subhagan would tonguclash her for singing in the darkness and disturbing their sleep. Her elder brother Subhagan would remark, “Why do you shout and howl like this? Is it so God can hear it in the heavens? Is your God deaf? Though she had to suffer punishment and scoldings, Sudha­ mani was not to be deterred from singing to God in the silent hours of the night. Another night Subhagan came angrily to the shrine room and criticized her for singing in the darkness. Quick came the little one's reply, “You behold only the exter­ nal lamp but within rne burns a lamp which never gets extin­ guished!" Needless to say, the inner significance of her re­ mark was lost on the callous Subhagan. Sudhamani had a fear that God would punish her mother, father and brother for beating her while she chanted devo­ tional songs. Therefore, she would sing softly to make them abstain from doing such evil actions. Deeply dejected by the obstacles created by her family, Sudhamani would weep while she sat in the shrine room. Then their next objection would be voiced. They insisted that it was a sin for her to weep while singing devotional songs and that this would cause them great danger. It made no difference what the little one did, they would find fault. Poor little Sudhamani! She si­ lently endured everything and dissolved all her miseries in the sweet memory of Sri Krishna. Even from her childhood Sudhamani would never tell her sorrows to any human being. The only one who she unbur­ dened herself to was Lord Krishna. Sudhamani was also in the Tears For Krishna 51 habit of talking to the animals and to Nature imagining that
32: Krishna was hearing her every word. Perceiving everything as Krishna she would go on conversing with them. The cows were Krishna attentively listening to her words as far as the little one was concerned. If a cow happened to be lying down resting, the little one would also lie down leaning against its body contentedly thinking that she was lying on Krishna’s lap. Looking at the stars, the moon and the flowering trees Sudhamani would ask, “O my friends, have you seen my Krishna? O gentle breeze, have you ever caressed His en­ chanting form? O glittering stars and silent moon, are you also in search of Him? If you find Him, please tell Him that this poor Sudhamani is also waiting to see Him.” Did anyone of you see my darling Kannan? You can see Him, but He never appears before my eyes... The mark of sandalepaste on the forehead, The beauty of the yellow silk robes. The waving locks of hair with the peacock feather... Oh, when will I behold all this? Of what use is this body and this life of mine? All my good fortune has ended... For how much longer will these sufferings per­ sist? The "Mother Sea' was also one among Sudhamani’s best friends, and she considered the ocean to be her own Mother. Whenever she got the chance the little one would slip off to the seaside and pour out her heart’s miseries while gazing at the vast expanse of water. The dark blue colour would remind her of her blue Beloved and before long her external aware­ ness would be lost.52 MATA AMRTTANANDAMAYI Meanwhile, Sudhamani had observed some of the neigh­ bors earning their living by doing tailoring jobs. Prompted by the idea to help others with the money which she earned from tailoring, the little one now cherished a desire to learn stitch­ ing. In this way, she could avoid the unpleasantness of having to take things from her house in order to help others. Full of hope, Sudhamani expressed her wish to her parents but Damayanthi’s reply was quite discouraging, “You are not going to be sent to learn stitching, but will soon be given in marriage to a coconut tree climber!” The coconut tree climb­ ers were a low class of people in Kerala whose sole source of income was gained only through the plucking of coconuts from trees for people. Sudhamani had often been caught stealing coconuts which Damayanthi thought was, of course, for her to eat but actually it was always given to the needy locals. Damayanthi in her anger was referring to this only. Finally, due to Sudhamani’s persistent demand, her par­ ents allowed her to learn sewing for one hour a day under one condition. She should complete all the domestic chores be­ fore leaving the house to learn stitching. One would be as­ tounded if they would have seen little Sudhamani’s routine on those days. Somehow she would manage to complete the chores which had
33: to be done before noontime and then race off to her stitching class. On some days the other girls who were studying stitching, knowing Sudhamani’s plight, would come and help her finish her chores. In the scorching heat of the midday sun, Sudhamani would walk two or three kilometres to the place where she studied. After one hour she would dash home in time to serve the noon meal. The rest of the day continued as usual with her grueling schedule. The only moments she had for her most important duty’, prayer and meditation, was in the silent hours of the night. Crying and crying the little one would get merged in that God-intoxicated state. Returning to a semi-conscious mood she would drift off to sleep.Tears For Krishna 53 The little one’s patience, endurance and seemingly inex­ haustible energy, which continues to this day, was miracu­ lous. Whatever amount of work was heaped on her she would do happily without murmuring a complaint. Sudhamani took it as her birthright and dharma to extend help to anyone and everyone without being asked. Later she explained, “My joy was in seeing others’ happiness. I never thought of my own comfort and workload. Whenever I got an opportunity to serve and assist others I tried my best to discharge it with utmost sincerity and love.” Initially Sudhamani was learning stitching in two separate places. After some time she chose to attend the classes which were given in a parochial workshop in a nearby chapel. The little one quickly learned the mechanics of sewing and began doing small tailoring jobs for the poor ladies in the neighbor­ hood. At first, she refused to accept money for the services rendered as this was not her way of doing things. But when her parents refused to pay the course fees for her stitching classes she was compelled to accept wages for her labour. Thus she managed to pay the class fee and the remaining amount she used to help the destitute villagers. She was also able to buy a few essential items for doing her tailoring jobs. Sudhamani was adept at tailoring and earned a good wage. Without giv­ ing even a paise3 to her house she would use her earnings to help the poor. 3 Equivalent of a penny.While sewing in the chapel workshop Sudhamani would sing devotional songs. Immersed in that mood she would shed tears which would fall on the sewing machine. The priest of that church was an elderly and pious man and took quick notice of Sudhamani’s remarkable character. While all the other girls gossiped Sudhamani was merged in her devotional singing. The priest was deeply touched by this and Sudha­ mani became very dear to him. This created jealousy in the minds of the other girls yet the little one would show affection to them just as before without a trace of animosity.54 MATA AMRJTANANDAMAYI Sathccsh would always accompany his sister to class and would stay around the church grounds or
34: sit in a corner. One day during prayer time Sudhamani asked, “Why are you not participating in the prayer?” He replied, “are we not Hin­ dus?” Then Sudhamani told him, “You ask the priest if you can also participate in the prayer,” to which the priest happily agreed. From then onwards Sathccsh would always attend the prayer session. When her sewing was completed Sudhamani would go to the church graveyard to do her embroidery work. She loved the solitude there. Sitting in the graveyard she would talk to the departed souls. “How is your life? Where are you living? are you happy there? Do you have any feelings?” She used to feel herself to be in their company and she would console them. A demised friend of Sudhamani’s elder sister Kasturi was buried in that cemetery. This girl had shown boundless love for Sudhamani even when the family members were terri­ bly abusing the little one. Perhaps this was another reason why Sudhamani would visit the graveyard. Sensing the mis­ ery of the departed ones, she would shed tears. She would talk to the souls drifting along with their subtle bodies and would sing for their peaceful rest in a heart rending tune. Sometimes Sudhamani would sit in meditation and enter into samadhi while sitting in the silence and stillness of the Christian grave- yard. If there was any time remaining after completing her em­ broidery work, Sudhamani would return to the chapel. The chapel had an inner apartment which seemed like a cave. In that dim light, she would gaze upon the crucified form of Je­ sus Christ. Seeing the form of Jesus oh the cross, she felt that it was her beloved Krishna. Immediately, she would become enraptured. Coming down to the plane of normal conscious­ ness. she would shed tears thinking of the love and sacrifice of Jesus Christ and Krishna. She would think, "O how They have sacrificed everything for the world. People turned against Them but still They loved them. If They have done it, then why can't I? There is nothing new in it.’Tea:* For Krishna 55 Sudhamani was acutely aware of the dire poverty of the villagers. Seeing their sorrows and sufferings, the little one would weep in the silent hours she spent in the shrine room. She would pray, “O God, is this life? People are toiling day in and day out just for a bit of food to satisfy their hunger. O Krishna, why do you allow them to starve? Why do they be­ come afflicted with diseases? Everywhere I turn I come face to face with selfishness and the people’s sufferings caused by it. Youngsters pray for a long life and children pray for an early death for their aged father and mother. Nobody is at all inter­ ested to care for the elderly. O Lord, what kind of a world is this? What is the purpose of creating such a world? O Krishna, what is the solution for all this?”
35: Such were the prayers of the innocent girl. Thus, three years had passed when Sudhamani decided to discontinue her tailoring classes considering them to be a hindrance for her spiritual practices which she wanted to in­ tensify. Within a few weeks, she stopped attending classes. The priest was also transferred at that time to another parish. Before he left, he sent a few of the girls from the class to Idamannel to express his wish to bid farewell to Sudhamani. Accompanied by Satheesh, she went to visit the priest for the last time. Gazing at the little one, the priest burst into tears and wept like a small child. Sudhamani stood with subdued emotions. The priest said, “Daughter, I am going to give up this job now. I have decided to lead the life of a sannyasin. ” As Sudhamani and Satheesh were about to leave, the priest said to Satheesh, “See, Sudhamani will become great in the fu­ ture. ” Perhaps the virtuous priest had already envisioned the shining divinity within the little one. Having now mastered stitching, Sudhamani expressed her wish to have a machine of her own. Damayanthi scolded her for being ambitious but Sugunanandan promised several times that he would get a machine for her. However, this sewing machine never materialized. Sudhamani decided “I won’t ask for a machine again. Only if God gives one shall I use it.” After several years, when devotees began flocking to56 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Idamannel, one among her Dutch devotees named Peter pur­ chased a sewing machine for her and she was reminded of her vow. God cares for each and every need of His true devotee. All the children except Sudhamani were studying either in secondary school or college. They all were light in complex­ ion and fair looking. Sudhamani’s dark blue complexion and hard working nature made them all look down upon the little one as a mere servant. She was not even given enough cloth­ ing. SRIMAD BHAGAVATAM SKANDA X, CANTO XIV, VERSE 24 Spiritual glory and the related behaviour of a God-Real- ized Soul are far beyond the realm or recognition of an ordi­ nary human being's consciousness. Whatever the case may be, the Great Souls remain unper­ turbed. They never heed the senseless remarks of skeptics and critics who are not to be blamed for their limited perception ofthe subtle realms of consciousness. A rainbow without colours, a flower without fragrance, when such is my heart, why cry for compassion? Life has become full of coldness without even a trace of warmth like a veena which has no sweet melody,but sorrowful silence alone... Can the lotus flowers in a small rivulet deep within the forest blossom where the rays of the Sun cannot reach? Seeing the clouds in the sky, the peacock spreadsits wings to dance, but in vain, and a chatakabird1 waits for drops of water... Who is taking birth? Who can harass a true devotee of the Lord?' This strong conviction completely changed her mind. Though externally she looked like the same ordinary village girl, internally she was one with the Lord Krishna, the natural state of abiding as the One Reality’. * * * * *CHAPTER FIVE FOR THE GOOD OF THE WORLD All the Deities of the Hindu Pantheon, who rep­ resent the infinite aspects of the One Supreme Being, exist within us as well. A Divine Incar­ nation can manifest any one of them for the good of the world by mere will. The Divine Mood of Krishna (Krishna Bhava) is the manifestation of the Purusha or Pure Consciousness aspect of the Absolute... -Mata Amritanandamayi-I know no Reality other than Sri Krishna whose hands hold the flute, Who is beautiful like a fresh rain cloud, Who wears yellow robes, Whose lips are red like an aruna bimba fruit, Whose face is charming like the full moon and Whose eyes are elongated like lotus petals... MADHUSUDANA SARASWATI Advent of Krishna Bhava Young Sudhamani, whose entire being had found eternal rest in the Supreme, now struggled to carry on with the house­ hold chores as before. She was then hard to discharge her dutieswithout fail but as we shall see, this was not what the Divinehad in store for her. It was during a Wednesday evening in the month of Sep­ tember 1975 that certain events occurred which would later mark the beginning of a fresh chapter in the annals of India's spiritual history’. The little one had just finished collecting grass for the cows and was returning home accompanied by her younger brother Satheesh. She was carrying a big bundle of grass on her head and the time was nearing 5 o'clock in the afternoon. She was in her usual sublime mood and the melodi­ ous tune of a devotional song was heard on her lips. As
44: the two 74 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI passed the entrance gate of the neighbouring house situated on the northern side of Idamannel, Sudhamani came to an abrupt halt. She had overheard the final verses of the Srimad Bhagavatam being read out in the courtyard of this house.1 The reading had come to an end and the devotional singing was just beginning. Sudhamani stood there captured by the moment and ap­ peared to be intently listening to the singing. Suddenly her mood changed dramatically. The bundle of grass fell from her head as the little one ran to the spot and stood in the midst of the devotees who were gathered there. Overwhelmed with divine bliss, her inner identification with the Lord over­ flowed into her external being transforming her features and movements into that of Sri Krishna Himself? The devotees, for the most part, believed that Sri Krishna had come there temporarily in the form of this village girl in order to bless them. Sudhamani asked one amongst the devo­ tees to bring some water and sprinkled it on everyone as sacred water. News about Sudhamani's Divine Manifestation spread quickly and soon a big crowd had gathered. There were skeptics in the crowd who raised an objection to the little one's sudden Divine Mood saying, “If you are really Lord Krishna, then you should be able to show us some proof of it through a miracle. Otherwise, how is it possible for us to be­ lieve?” Straight away came the reply, “An object which does not already exist cannot be brought into existence. All things are really only the projection of the mind. Having the Real Gem within you, why do you crave for an imitation? Even though the Pure Being is within you, igno­ rance is veiling It!” 1.Even- month this family would conduct the reading of this great epic whichhighlights the life and sportings of Lord Krishna. One Sri Narayanan fromthe next village would conduct the discourse each month.For The Good Of The World 75 Unable to grasp this sublime truth uttered by one estab­ lished in that Pure Being, they pressed her again and again to show a miracle. Sudhamani replied, “I am least interested to make somebody a believer by showing a miracle. It is not my intention to show miracles. My goal is to inspire people with the desire for Liberation through Realisation of their Eternal Self. Miracles are illu­ sory. That is not the essential principle behind spirituality. Not only that, once a miracle is shown you will desire and demand to see it again and again. I am not here to create desire, but to remove it.” The skeptics insisted, “No, we won’t demand again, show a miracle once, we won’t insist again!” At last, conceding to their insistent demand, Sudhmani told, “In order to inculcate faith in you I will do it once, bur never again approach me with such desires. Those who doubt, let them come to this same place on the day of
45: the next Srimad Bhagavatam discourse.” When the next Bhagavatam discourse was held, a huge crowd gathered inside and outside the house. The non-be­ lievers even climbed the trees and perched on the rooftops hoping to expose any fraud. Revealing her oneness with Krishna, Sudhamani called one of the doubting Toms to bring a pitcher of water. As before, this was sprinkled on the devotees as sacred water. Then Sudhamani asked the same man to dip his fingers in the remaining water. To everyone's amazement the water had become pure milk! This was then distrubuted among the crowd as a holy offering from God. Then Sudhamani called another skeptic and asked him to dip his fingers in the pitcher. The milk in the pot had turned into a sweet and fragrant pudding (panchamritam) made of milk, bananas, raw sugar, raisins, rock sugar. All present raised a cry, “O God! O God!” and believed themselves to indeed be in the Divine Presence of Lord Krishna. The panchamritam was distributed among more than a thousand people who had gathered, yet the pot remained full to the brim. Some people76 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI who were sitting at a distance near a small banyan tree at the seaside also received the sweet pudding, but still the vessel’s contents did not diminish. A few skeptics were not satisfied and they declared the whole incident a feat of mesmerism in­ sisting that the panchamritam would vanish within a few see­ onds. Much to their disappointment it did not vanish and the sweet scent remained on everyones’ hands for several days. This event greatly increased the faith of the villagers and they all firmly believed in Sudhamani’s divinity. Referring to the advent of Krishna Bhava Sudhamani ex­ plained, “In the early days I used to dance in bliss and move about alone, persisting in Krishna Bhava, but no one knew. One day I strongly felt the urge to get absorbed in that Supreme Being once and for all. Then I heard a voice from within, ‘Thousands and thousands of people in the world are steeped in misery. I have a lot to be done by you who are one with Me.’” It was after hearing this voice that Sudhamani manifested her inner identity with Lord Krishna to the villagers. Sudha­ mani further continued, “I was able to know everything concerning everyone. I was fully conscious that I myself was Krishna, not only during that particular time of manifestation, but at all other times as well. I did not feel, ‘I am great’. When I saw the people and knew their sufferings, I felt- immense pity’ for them. I was con­ scious of devotees offering salutations to me and addressing me as ‘Lord’. I could understand the grievances of the devo­ tees even without being told.” From that time onwards, Sudhamani regularly revealed Krishna Bhava near a small banyan tree growing on the west­ ern side of Idamannel near the seashore path. Around the tree there were quite a few flowering
46: plants as well. On the whole it looked like a bush. A few years earlier the villagers hadFor The Good Of The World 77 planned to construct a temple there. To inaugurate the temple site, some of the village youth had united and planted another banyan tree as well as lighting a sacred oil lamp. Sugunanandan had encouraged the youths and actively participated in their efforts. His elderly mother Madhavi of­ ten times accompanied by Sudhamani would come each eve­ ning, light the oil lamp and sing a few devotional songs. A tiny hut thatched with woven coconut leaves had been built right in front of this banyan tree and a portrait of Lord Krishna and Mother Kali had been installed inside.2 Now several years later, this became the spot where Sudha­ mani would reveal her identity with Lord Krishna. As the piece of land was public property, it was a convenient location for the people to gather and participate in the auspicious Krishna Bhava. Lying horizontally on a slender branch of the banyan tree, Sudhamani would assume the posture of Anantasayana, that is Lord Vishnu reclining on the thou­ sand-headed serpent Ananta. The little one's body would become as light as air at this time by self-will. This was a won­ derful sight for the devotees. This sacred spot became a veritable Vrindavan, the Abode of Sri Krishna and the atmosphere was saturated with devo­ tional songs in praise of the Lord. Devotees began to Hock to this place not only for the auspicious darshan of Sri Krishna but also to get relief from their problems. The devotees' dis­ tress would mysteriously vanish after unburdening their troubles to Sudhamani during the Krishna Bhava. In those days when someone prayed for a solution to their problems, Sudhamani as Krishna would instruct them to light a piece of camphor and place the burning camphor on her protruding tongue. She would then swallow it along with the fire! After the Bhava had ended, nobody could find a trace of a bum on her tongue. This practice also increased the faith of the people. News about Krishna Bhava quickly spread and people from Kerala and different parts of India started flocking to 78 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Parayakadavu. This marked the beginning of a pilgrimage to this holy place which has continued unabated to this day. Some people came seeking relief from disease, some sought relief from material difficulties, others came out of curiosity, and some came out of devotion. But one thing they all discov­ ered was that after coming to Sudhamani, a solution was found for their problems. One group of local skeptics began coming just to pass time hoping to expose the fraud which is how they perceived Sudhamani’s Divine Mood. But the little one remained equi­ poised under all circumstances. She would later explain, “During the Bhava, different kinds of people come to see me, some out of devotion, others for getting a solution to their worldly problems, and for relief from
47: diseases. I discard none. Can I reject them? are they different from me? Are we not all beads strung on the one life-thread? According to each one’s level of thinking they see me. Both those who love me and those who hate me are the same to me.” During the first two Krishna Bhavas, Sugunanandan had been out of town on business. When he came to hear of the mysterious transformation of his daughter, he suspected it to be some undiagnosed disease. However, he decided to see the Krishna Bhava for himself before making any final decision. Thus, he arranged a Bhagavatam reading at Idamannel and on that day Sudhamani revealed her oneness with Sri Krishna. Having seen the Divine Mood of his daughter who had been since birth full of surprises, Sugunanandan was wonder- struck and could not utter a word. From that time onwards Sugunanandan, being an ardent devotee of Lord Krishna, was found partaking in all the Bhava Darshans which had become a regular feature of this hallowed coastal fishing area. At this point, the parents still believed that Sudhamani’s Divine Moods were only possessions of Lord Krishna and her devotional practices were temporary’ aberrations that would stop one day. They waited for that day to come so that theyFor The Ckxxi Of 1110 World 79 could give her in marriage. They cannot be blamed for their misconception as they were not at all aware of Great Souls and their behaviour. Theirs was a simple view of God and His manifestations on earth were, in their mind, limited strictly to the Gods and Goddesses in the shrine rooms and temples. God was not to be found elsewhere, least of all in their eccen­ tric daughter! Having forgotten their previous experiences, the parents once again made arrangements for Sudhamani’s marriage. But each time Sudhamani blatantly threatened any prospec­ tive groom who was unfortunate enough to arrive at Idaman- nel. Finally, Sudhamani angrily warned her parents, "If you succeed in giving me in marriage, I will kill him and then come back to Idamannel.’” The parents, having bitterly failed to marry off Sudha­ mani, decided to seek the advice of a well-known astrologer3 who lived in a distant place and had never heard of Idamannel, Sudhamani or her Divine Mood. The parents were full of hope that at last they would circumvent this stubborn obstacle. After consulting Sudhamani’s horoscope, the astrologer turned to Sugunanandan and said in a solemn tone, "This girl is a Mahatma (Great Soul or Saint). If the marriage is not yet arranged, please do not make any more effort to do so. If the marriage has already taken place, then call her back immedi­ ately. Otherwise you will have to face a great calamity’; it will be the cause of great sorrow for you. ” The dejected father re­ turned home with a heavy heart and all future plans for Sudha- mani’s marriage were dropped. When people understood that Sudhamani’s manifesta­ tion of Krishna Bhava was
48: genuine, more and more of them came to receive her blessings. At the same time, some people came hoping to take advantage of Sudhamani’s divine be­ nevolence to further their selfish motives and to make money. One night, some people approached Sudhamani thinking 3.In India, marriages are traditionally arranged by the parents after an astrologerconsults the son or daughter's horoscope.80 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI that the little one could be tempted by money. They offered her a large sum of money if she would show some miracles. Sudhamani laughed out loud and affectionately told them, “I have nothing to gain by showing miracles. My goal is not to gain name and fame and material prosperity by exhibiting miracles. There is an immense and inexhaustible wealth of divinity within us. Discarding that, why should one chase after the perishable and trivial wealth of the world? Selfless service to God and ailing humanity is my life’s goal. I am here not to cam anything, but to renounce everything for the hap­ piness of others." Day by day the number of devotees increased as the won­ derful experiences of those who had come to Sudhamani in Krishna Bhava inspired others to also take refuge there. The seashore around the banyan tree came alive with devotional singing and the villagers, forgetting all their differences, would gather together to sing and receive her blessings. On one such occasion, a huge crowd of devotees had gath­ ered at the sacred banyan shrine during Krishna Bhava. Sud­ denly, dark,menacing storm clouds gathered overhead and there was a downpour. As there was no shelter available nearby, the devotees simply stood there waiting for the inevi­ table drenching. But to the amazement of all, no rain fell at that particular spot where the devotees were standing, al­ though it fell in torrents all around them! During this period, a poisonous cobra continuously dis­ turbed the pedestrians, particularly at night. The people of the village often saw the cobra moving feely about and every­ one became fearful to walk along the seashore at night. Some of the villagers approached Sudhamani during Krishna Bhava and prayed for a solution. Then one evening during the Divine Mood the dreaded serpent made an appearance. The crowd scattered and stood cowering at some distance. Sudhamani fearlessly caught hold of the cobra and touched its flicking tongue with her own! After doing so she released For The Good Of The World 8 1 it. Never again were the villagers troubled by the venomous snake and once more they moved freely about the oceanside. During one period the ‘Children of the Mother Sea’, as the fisherfolk were known, were starving because they had been unable to catch any fish for many days. They approached Sudhamani during Krishna Bhava and confided their tale ofwoe. Giving them a tulasi leaf,4 she instructed them to have ayoung boy drop it into the sea at a particular spot and then tofish there. In order to test her, the fishermen did not act uponher
49: advice and approached her again during the next Darshan(audience). Before they could say a word, Sudhamanipointed out their mischief and once again offered them a tu­lasi leaf. Surprised and remorseful, they accepted the leaf andproceeded to the sea, but somehow they failed to drop it in thedesignated spot. Taking pity on them during the nextDarshan, Sudhamani blissfully danced on the seashore and inthat way bestowed her blessing. Much to the fishermen'shappiness and satisfaction, the next day a vast school of fishswam right up to the shore. Never before in the history of thevillage had there been such a bountiful catch. On two otheroccasions Sudhamani did the same in response to the sincereprayer and request of the fisherfolk. But this self-centred anddesire-prompted devotion of the villagers did not win muchfavour or encouragement from Sudhamani. 4 Tulasi is a variety of basil which is considered to be holy and sacred to Sri Krishna.Though Krishna Bhava was the external manifestation of Sudhamani’s infinite spiritual power which she expressed through the form of Krishna, her parents and most of the vil­ lagers believed that she was being possessed temporarily by Lord Krishna during Bhava time. Her elder brother and par­ ents also had the idea that she was suffering from schizophre­ nia or some other mental delusion. For her part Sudhamani was content to leave it at that. At this point, apparently it was enough that the people were getting devotion to God and re­ lief from their worldly sufferings as a result of the Bhava82 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Darshan. This divine drama was to unfold in its various stages according to the needs of the people and at the proper mo­ ment. Conducting the Bhava Darshan near the seaside had its drawbacks although it was true that the devotees could gather there freely. While some came out of reverence and devotion, there was a persistent group that came to abuse and harass Sudhamani. Not only that, the fast increase in the number of devotees had given rise to small scale develop­ ments around the banyan tree. One self-motivated group among the villagers had even banded together and formed an administrative board which promptly took a decision to in­ stall a locked box for monetary offerings for themselves. This was the forewarning of the formation of a troublesome alli­ ance. These occurances made Sugunanandan very sad. One night he approached Sudhamani during the Krishna Bhava and expressed his concern, “It is painful to see You perform­ ing Bhava Darshan on this roadside. Moreover it is intoler­ able to hear the non-believers poking fun al You. Besides all this, the little one is my daughter and it is heart-breaking to see her surrounded by all sorts of people in a public place." Saying this, he burst into tears. Sudhamani replied, “In that case, give Me another place to receive My devotees. If there is no other place, that cowshed will suffice." Sugunanandan readily agreed and arrange­ ments were made to repair the cowshed. It was cemented and
50: a half wall was made in the middle which divided it into two sections. One side remained a cowshed and the other side was prepared for conducting the Bhava Darshan. All four sides were covered with woven palm leaves. Soon, the Bhava Darshan was shifted from the seaside ban­ yan tree shrine to Idamannel where it continues to the present day. During the Krishna Bhava, Sudhamani would stand in the newly built shrine. While leaning on the half-wall, she would now and then reach over and put her hand on one of the cows which were standing on the other side.For The Good Of The World 83 One night during Krishna Bhava, Sudhamani called her father and told him, “My devotees will come from far and wide. Many of the devotees will come and settle here permanently. You will have to face many obstacles, but do not be afraid. Endure eve­ rything. Do not take revenge against anyone. Do not be envi­ ous. Do not seek anything from anyone. Everything you need will come to you unasked. Part of what you receive should always be given in charity. In due course, this place will be­ come a great spiritual centre. The little one will travel around the world many times. Though you may have to suffer a lot in the near future, God will always bless you and provide for your needs. Your kith and kin and even the villagers will hate you and abuse you but in due course of time they will become your friends. Thousands of My devotees will become like your own children. From this day onwards the little one is ever pure." Once more, Sugunanandan was wonderstruck! His dark blue daughter who had suffered countless beatings at his own hands was going around the world?! Why, she had never been as far as Cape Comorin.Thousands of people were coming to Idamannel? Where on earth would they all stay? It was only a tiny house. And what was the meaning of saying that the little one would be ever pure?6 Though these words left a deep impression in Sugunanandan's mind, for the time being he dismissed them as crazy utterances. Only after years had passed did he realize the absolute truth of what had been spo­ ken to him that day. Some of the local people found that their own interests had suffered due to the relocation of Krishna Bhava and loudly protested saying, "We don't want a God who follows her fa­ ther's wishes!’' The devotees who used to sing at the banyan 4.Cape Comorin is the southern tip of India, 200 kilometres to the south of Parayakadavu. 5.After Krishna Bhava began, Sudhamani never had her monthly period.84 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI tree shrine now split into two groups. One group declared their opposition in the form of non-cooperation and the other group came to Idamannel to sing bhajans (devotional songs) during the Bhava Darshan as before. Provoked by the loyal group of devotees, a group of villagers
51: came to Idaman­ nel with the sole intent to quarrel and fight. They proceeded to openly abuse the bhajan singers while they sang during the Bhava Darshan. This antagonistic group, consisting of both men and women, persisted until one day Sugunanandan be­ came completely fed up. He and some of the other devotees chased them out of Idamannel, but this was only the begin­ ning of the trouble. The Rationalist Movement Some among the truculent group were sons of the village landlords. They banded together and formed an organisation which they named 'The Committee to Remove Blind Beliefs’ otherwise known as "The Rationalist Movement’. From thir­ teen coastal villages they managed to round up a thousand youths who began their campaign to put an end to Sudha­ mani’s Divine Mood. The villagers loved the virtuous and noble minded little one since her very childhood when she would greet the new day with her enchanting singing to Krishna. Furthermore, they had faith in her Divine Mood and unwavering devotion but Sugunanandan’s unyielding nature served to increase any feeling of envy or enmity that they might have had. At the outset of Krishna Bhava, Sudhamani had warned her father during one Darshan not to quarrel or take revenge on anyone who opposed her. Without heeding this divine advice, Sugunanandan took certain steps against the committee. This only redoubled the enmity of the so-called rationalists. They began by making slogans against Sudhamani. They then proceeded to publish notices filled with baseless and ir­ rational criticism of the little one. This vindictive campaign did not end there; in fact, their effort to defame SudhamaniFor The Good Of The World 85 and bring an end to Krishna Bhava had just begun. Their next step was to lodge a false petition with the police against Sudhamani stating that she was cheating people in the name of devotion? As a result of the petition, some police officers came to Idamannel to interrogate the little one. Sudhamani, courageous under all circumstances, told the officers, “Please arrest me if you like and take me to jail. Here, the fam­ ily members and villagers are not allowing me to meditate. At least in jail I would get solitude so I could always meditate on God. If it is God's Will, let it be done." Having spoken these words, she stretched out her hands. The police officers were very much impressed to see her bold yet innocent way of fac­ ing the situation and addressing them. A few thought she must be crazy, but the others were charmed by her personality and felt sorry that such a Great Soul was being so rudely scan­ dalized and persecuted for nothing. These officers paid their homage and left Idamannel. The following song was com­ posed by Sudhamani during this time of the false petition and subsequent police inquiry: Thou art compassionate to the devotees... O Pure Consciousness! Destroyer of all transgressions! Are there only sinners in this world? O Bhagavan! O Bhagavan!7 7.O Lord!
52: O Lord! 8 This refers to the eternal spiritual truths stated in the Vedic Scriptures.Who is there to instruct in the righteous path? The Essential Principles8 are only found Printed on the pages of books... O Bhagavan! O Bhagavan! What is seen is just False costume and trumpery. O Kanna, please protect and Restore righteousness!86 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI One evening at twilight while the devotional singing was going on, another police officer, who was not satisfied with the first investigation, came to Idamannel on the basis of some new complaint. To his surprise the atmosphere had a soothing effect on him and unable to find anything offensive or amiss, he also left the place without uttering a word. The miscreants still continued their efforts to put an end to Sudhamani’s Divine Mood. They now turned to much more aggressive and direct tactics to achieve their desired end. Their next plan was to go to Idamannel in small groups during the Bhava Darshan and catch hold of Sudhamani during the Divine Mood, thus dishonoring her and at the same time making a sham of the Darshan. After catching hold of the little one, they intended to rough her up. These hooligans were convinced that their straightforward plan would succeed as they prided themselves on their courage and strength. How­ ever, before the night was over they left Idamannel full of shame as, for some inexplicable reason, not one among them dared to approach Sudhamani during the Divine Mood. Undeterred, they now hired a black magician infamous for his deadly sorcery. He himself came to Idamannel and offered Sudhamani some so-called ‘sacred ash' in which he had in­ voked malevolent forces. This ash was prepared from the charred body of a cobra and was known to be so powerful that its evil effects would cause death to the particular person towards whom it had been directed. Even to receive such ash in one's hand was considered a bad omen which would bring about a great disaster. Fully aware of the supposed conse­ quences, Sudhamani took the ash and rubbed it on her body right in front of the misguided man. She thought to herself, uIf the body is to perish by this, let it be so. If it is God’s Will, can anyone escape it?” The unscrupulous man waited a long time to witness the agonizing effects of his sorcery which he had inflicted on numerous other victims. Finally, he had to quit the place admitting utter defeat as nothing untoward had occurred even after hours and hours had passed.For The Ckxxi (X The World 87 Now the miscreants were really desperate to finish off Sudhamani and her Divine Mood and they launched their most treacherous plan of all. During the Krishna Bhava they entered the temple and offered Sudhamani a glass of deadly, poison-laced milk. Sudhamani impeccably played her role and graciously smiling, drank the whole glass of milk without hesitation. The murderous enemies anxiously waited to see Sudhamani collapse in convulsions inside
53: the shrine and breathe her last. To their bitter dismay, after a few moments Sudhamani turned in their direction, vomited the poisonous milk right in front of them, and continued to receive the devo­ tees as if nothing had happened. The rationalists fled from the place and temporarily gave up their campaign against the little one. The other obstacle which Sudhamani continually faced was the attitude of her own family. Despite the incessant har­ assment which she received from her own kith and kin, Sudha- mani's mind never wavered in its equanimity, tolerance and compassionate resolve to uplift suffering humanity whether friend or foe, family or foreigner. The formation of the antagonistic organization and its evil intention to harm the innocent little one created great mental distress for Sugunanandan. During this period, it was Sudha­ mani’s habit to spend her nights outdoors meditating under the star-filled heavens. Since her childhood she had always held sacred the solitude and silence of the night when she could commune with the Divine and dance blissfully in a God- intoxicated mood without interference. Sugunanandan’s fear for his daughter multiplied when he thought of her enemies who may sneak up to attack her while she sat alone. Therefore, one day he demanded, “Daughter, come and sleep in the house!” Sudhamani firmly reassured her father, “See, I have no house of my own. I prefer to sleep outside. God is omnipresent. He is everywhere, outside and inside. Then why should I worry? If somebody comes to harm me, God will protect me.”88 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI As for Damayanthi, when the Krishna Bhava began she had faith in Krishna but her abusive attitude towards the little one once the Bhava had ended persisted as before. This was due to her false perception that Sudhamani was possessed by Krishna only during the time of the Bhava Darshan and other­ wise she remained as ever her lowly servant and eccentric daughter. After the advent of Krishna Bhava, Damayanthi had no choice but to exempt Sudhamani from her household responsibilities as, at any moment, her mind may soar off into samadhi regardless of time or place. If she happened to be cooking or wading through the backwaters this total absorp­ tion would be a genuine hazard. As previously described, Damayanthi was always very orthodox when it came to her daughters conduct. She prohib­ ited Sudhamani from talking to any of the devotees after the Darshan had ended, particularly to young men. If she did so, Damayanthi would severely chastise and beat Sudhamani without hesitation. She still feared that Sudhamani’s unusual behaviour would bring bad reputation and dishonour to the family name! Although Sudhamani was beyond all likes and dislikes, the parents mistook her to be an ordinary’ girl with all human feelings, attractions, and weaknesses except during the Bhava Darshan. We can only wonder how it is possible that the people who were closest to her were least able to rec­ ognize her constant abidance in the Supreme. The most intractable member
54: of the family was Subhagan, Sudhamani’s elder brother. He could not digest his sister’s way of receiving the devotees and was completely intolerant of her ecstatic way of devotional singing and dancing. Sudha­ mani, who was beyond all duality, would receive men and women, children and the aged, in the same equal manner. This infuriated Subhagan who, besides being an atheist, held the belief that women were subservient to men and should remain silent and unseen. He considered his sister to be a schizo­ phrenic and tried his utmost to create obstacles for her. One day, he intentionally broke the oil lamp which the devotees kept lit in the temple during Krishna Bhava. The For the Good Of The World 89 devotees who arrived that night for the Darshan felt very de­ jected seeing the broken lamp as there was no other lamp to replace it. Seeing their unhappy faces, Sudhamani asked some of the devotees to fetch a few seashells. When they were brought she then asked them to place wicks in the shells and to light them without oil as none was available. Then the impossible happened. Not only did the wicks light but they burned throughout the night until the Krishna Bhava was over, even without a drop of oil! When asked how this could happen, Sudhamani simply said, “The lamps burnt like that because of the sankalpa9 of the devotees.” On the next Bhava Darshan day, a devotee who was unaware of the broken oil lamp and the subsequent incident, made an offering of oil lamps for the shrine. Responding to the queries of the devo­ tees he explained that he had had a dream in which he was in­ structed to bring them. 9 A pure resolve.Some of the villagers who arrogantly ridiculed Sudhamani had to face great disasters in life. The following is one such incident. One day Sudhamani was returning home after visiting a nearby house. By the side of the path stood a group of villag­ ers. As she passed them, Sudhamani overheard one amongst them teasing her. A wealthy villager was telling another man in a loud voice, “See this girl, she is crazy. She is always sing­ ing, dancing and pretending to be Krishna. What nonsense! This is a case of emotional hysteria. If her father would just give her in marriage, this disease would be cured.” Hearing his critical remarks, his friends burst into laughter. He con­ tinued egotistically, “If her father needs a dowry, I am ready to give him credit for two thousand rupees to marry’ this girl off. I must tell him today!” Sudhamani didn't utter a word. Reaching home, she ran to the family shrine room and locked herself inside. Sitting there, she began pouring her heart out to Krishna, “O Krishna, don't You hear what they are saying? They call me90 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI crazy! They know nothing of Your beauty and instead would bind me to their own self-centered way of life. O Krishna, Protector
55: of those who seek Your refuge, have You too aban­ doned me? If that is so, then who will ever worship You seeing my desperate condition? Is this the reward for the tears I have shed thinking only of You? Is this love and devotion of mine merely the raving of a crazy' girl? All these days You are my only comfort. In the blue sky I see Your smiling face, in the waves I see Y’our dancing form. The morning doves’ song is Your di­ vine flute playing only! O Krishna, Krishna...” Thus praying she began sobbing and collapsed on the floor. Meanwhile, the wealthy man who had teased Sudhamani was busy readying his fishing boats and nets, of which he was the proud owner, for the days fishing. Gathering his workers, they pushed off into the sea. That day they had an exception­ ally good catch and everyone was rejoicing as they rowed back to shore. On the way back, some among the workers who loved and worshipped Sudhamani remarked to the boat owner, “Any­ how, it was not a right action to tease the innocent little one the way you did today.” The boat owner snccringly retorted, “And so what if I did? see what happened because I teased her; we got a bigger catch than on most days?” The devotees were tongue tied and hung their heads. The boat was nearing the shore when suddenly the boat owner said, “Hey, why don’t we go to Neendakara?13 There we will get a good price for our catch. In Parayakadavu the prices are very low.” That decided,they steered the boat to Neen- dakara. They had almost reached the destination when sud­ denly the sea started to become rough. Within no time, huge waves rose up and dashed either side of the wooden boat. The boat full of fish, nets and fishermen was tossed up and down by the gigantic waves. All efforts put forth by the boatmen to control the boat were of no avail. As the waves dumped water into the boat it began sinking. The next instant, as if grabbed 13.A town in Kerala well known for its fish markets.For The Good Of The World 91 by an enormous wave, the boat was dashed on the rocks and broke into pieces. The day’s catch was lost, one of the rich man’s best boats was destroyed and his fishing nets lay tom on the rocks. Only their lives had been spared. With great diffi­ culty, the workers and the boat owner managed to swim to the shore. A most unexpected disaster had befallen the arrogant boat owner. Now it was his turn to walk along the shore with his head hanging down. Broken-heartedly he collapsed in the sand unable to bear his great loss. The workers who were ad­ mirers of Sudhamani whispered to each other, “See the result of God’s fury. He was just boasting about his good fortune af­ ter he teased the little one. Now
56: look and see what has hap­ pened.'” Another worker who was a devotee remarked, “He left the shores of Parayakadavu after saying that the little one was suffering from hysteria and even boasted that he would give 2000 rupees to marry her off. Now let as see from where he will borrow that money!” Altogether including the boat, fishing nets and the day’s catch he had lost 75,000 rupees. The workers took a bus home and the news flashed everywhere. In those days, sometimes Sudhamani would suddenly be­ come playful like a three year old child and would make mis­ chief with the devotees who had come for Krishna Bhava. When the Darshan was over, she would silently sneak about like a thief and approach the sleeping devotees. Sometimes she would tic the end of one woman's san to the hair of another woman. Other times, Sudhamani would pour a handful of sand on the men devotees who used to sleep with wide open mouths. Harshan, Sudhamani’s elder cousin, was lame. He had great respect and love for her. If he happened to be sleep­ ing somewhere after Darshan finished, Sudhamani would search and find him. Catching hold of him by the legs, she would drag him all around. It was a big joke for him as well and all the devotees would burst into laughter seeing Sudha- mani's childlike sports. Subhagan never liked such actions of his sister and would scold her for her unusual behaviour. How could he, who never even studied the first lessons of spiritual­92 MATA AM R IT AN AND AM A YI ity, understand the exalted state of Sudhamani? Regarding her strange behaviour, Sudhamani said, “My mind is always shooting up to merge with the Abso­ lute. I am always trying to bring it down; only then can I serve ailing humanity and mingle with the devotees. That is why I play such games...to keep the mind engaged in the world of the devotees, though without attachment.” * * * * *CHAPTER SIX AS THE CHILD OF THE DIVINE MOTHER As noble aspirations clearly manifested them­ selves in my mind, the Divine Mother, with bright, gentle hands, carressed my head. With bowed head, I told Mother that my life is dedi­ cated to Her... -Mata Amritanandamayi-O Devi, verily You are Siva, You alone are the Teacher,You alone are the Supreme Energy, You alone are the Mother, You alone are Fa­ ther. For me, knowledge, relatives, sustenance and Intelligence, everything is You. DEVI BHUJANGAM Devotion Bhakti (devotion) and its unique charm is indescribable. A true devotee's sincere desire is to be a devotee forever. He wishes to attain neither heaven nor liberation. For him, devo­ tion is his life and the Lord is his Supreme All-in-all. The devo­ tee always finds delight in singing the glory of the Supreme. That is why the Bhakti Shastras1 declare: 1 Devotional scriptures like "Narada Bhakti Sutras'.Devotion alone is the fruit of devotion. In its intrinsic nature this divine love
57: Is Immortal Bliss... 96 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Even the great saint Suka, though he was thoroughly es­ tablished in the Supreme Consciousness, took great delight in singing the glories of the Lord. Such is the bliss derived from pure devotion. Sudhamani, who was fully established in the conscious­ ness of Krishna, still had an unquenchable thirst to enjoy the bliss of Supreme Devotion--Para Bhakti. But her complete identification with Lord Krishna made it impossible for her either to meditate on His Form or get immersed in His thought. Then and there her prayers stopped flowing to Krishna and her Krishna sadhana came to an end. One day, Sudhamani had a vision which brought about a vast change in her manifestation of the Supreme and her serv­ ice to the world. This unexpected vision paved the way for Sudhamani's Devi sadhana, her passionate quest to realize the Supreme as the Mother of the Universe. Sudhamani was sitting alone in a room in the house. Her eyes were open but her mind was turned inwards and she was absorbed in the Self. All of a sudden, right in front of her there appeared a globe of brilliant light. It was reddish as the setting sun and at the same time soothing as the moon. The globe of light neither rested on the earth nor hung in the sky. It was rotating. Against the background of this radiant, yet cooling disc of light, emerged the most enchanting form of Devi, the Divine Mother with a beautiful crown adorning Her head. Excited by this heart­ capturing vision of Devi, Sudhamani cried out, “O Krishna, Mother has come! Please take me to Her, I want to embrace Her!” At once, Sudhamani perceived Krishna lifting her. He ascended with Sudhamani up above the clouds where she beheld strange sights-lofty hills, huge thick forests, blue snakes and terrifying caves. But the little one could not find Devi anywhere. Like a small child, Sudhamani called out, “I want to see my Mother! Where is my Mother?” Saying thus, she began crying. This exquisite vision of the Great Enchantress disap­ peared from her sight but became firmly fixed in Sudhamani's heart forever. The little one remained immersed in that enrap­As The Child Of The Divine Mother 97 tured state for a long time. From that moment onwards she cherished a desire to again behold the benign smile and com­ passionate face of the Divine Mother. Sudhamani, who be­ held the Divine Form of Sri Krishna countless times, was as­ tonished by the tremendous radiance of Devi. Her heart rushed after Devi and her only desire now became to embrace the Mother, sit on the Mother’s lap and give a kiss on the Mother’s check. Thus Sudhamani, who had never meditated on any other form than Sri Krishna, who had strongly believed that there was no Deity higher than Sri Krishna, now dedicated her en­ tire being to realize the Supreme as the Universal Mother, Adi Parashakti.2 Except for the time spent
58: in Krishna Bhava, she would be found in deep contemplation of the resplendent form of the Divine Mother. The fire of longing for Her Vision burned in Sudhamani’s heart day and night. Previously, the little one's household duties had demanded that she remain functional in the mundane world, but now this fetter had been broken and she became completely lost to this gross plane of existence. It became a struggle for her to even main­ tain the most minimal care of her body. For months together she subsisted only on tulasi leaves and water. 2 The Primal Supreme Energy, the Creatrix, the feminine counterpart of the masculine ‘Siva' or Pure Consciousness.Sometimes when Sudhamani came out of deep meditation she would be heard crying aloud, Amma! Amma! Where have You gone? Did You come that day only to abandon me? Please have mercy on this child of Yours and again reveal Your unsurpassingly gracious Form! O Mother, if I am deserving, make me one with You. I cannot bear this agony of separation? O Mother of the Uni­ verse, why are You indifferent to the heartbroken call of thischild? Please embrace me, do take me on Your lap!98 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI I shall wash Thy Feet with my tears. O Katyayani, forsake me not. How many days are wanted, my Mother, To grant me the Vison of Thy Form? Though Thou delayest in giving what I want, My mind sits satisfied because of Thy Maya. Will Thou allow me to offer A red flower at Thy Feet? Through this forlorn path I wander in the hope of finding Thee. Is there any kindness in Thy hard heart, Tell me, O Beloved of Siva? Just as Sudhamani had perceived everything to be per­ vaded by Krishna at the end of her Krishna sadhana, she now felt the Divine Presence of Devi in everything. Even the breeze was the breath of Devi. The innocent one used to roam about talking to plants, trees, birds and animals. She felt the earth to be her Mother and she would roll in the sand calling, “Amma, Amma! Where are You? Where are You not?” One day after finishing her meditation, Sudhamani came out of the small shrine room. She was suddenly overwhelmed with the feeling that she was a small child and that Nature was the Divine Mother. In that mood she crawled like a baby on all fours and reached a coconut tree. The little one sat there shed­ ding tears and pleading, “Mother...my Mother...why are You hiding from my sight? I know that You are hiding in this tree. You are in these plants. You are residing in these animals, these birds! The earth is nothing but You. O Mother, how You conceal Yourself in the ocean waves and in the cooling breeze! O Mother, my elusive Mother!...” The next moment she embraced the coconut tree feeling that it was the Divine Mother. Sometimes Sudhamani would lie down, but not to rest or relax. She had
