59: developed a strong aversion for the material world and never liked to see it around her with all its attractiveAs The Child Of The Divine Mother 99 objects and people who were totally immersed in sensuous pleasures. Thus, while lying down she would gaze at the infi­ nite sky, the silvery clouds, the glittering stars, the shining sun or the soothing moon depending on the time of day. When dark storm clouds gathered overhead, Sudhamani no longer saw Sri Krishna there, but visualized the long, cascad­ ing, curly hair of the Divine Mother. Each object suspended in the infinitude of the heavens became a means for Sudha­ mani to remember Devi. As she lay there, she would never sleep. What may have appeared to be sleep to the onlooker was in fact an unconscious state which resulted from her continu­ ous tear-filled supplications to the Supreme Mother of the Universe. About those days Sudhamani later related, “While walking I would repeat the Divine Name with each and every step. Always the next step was made only after chanting the mantra. If ever I forgot to chant the mantra while taking a step, immediately I would step backwards. Having withdrawn the step, I would repeat the mantra. Only then would I proceed. If I happened to be engaged in any external activity, I would decide beforehand to chant the mantra a cer­ tain number of times before finishing the work. While bath­ ing in the river before plunging into the water, I would resolve to chant the mantra a set number of times before coming to the surface of the water. I never had a Guru, nor was I ever initi­ ated by anyone and given a particular mantra. The mantra which I used to chant was ‘Amma, Amma” There is a scriptural injunction which states, “Actions will naturally subside in the supreme state of Devotion..” This can be clearly seen in the case of Sudhamani. In the morning, she used to brush her teeth, but the next moment her mind would become abstracted in the thought of the Divine Mother. This state would deepen and persist sometimes for hours to­ gether. Her attempts at taking bath usually met with even less success. First she would enter the bathroom and then realize 100 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI she had forgotten to bring a towel. After fetching the towel she would notice that she had also come without soap. Then she would think, 'O Mother, how much time is wasted just trying to take a bath! Instead, let my mind always be fixed on You! Even a moment not thinking of You creates agonizing pain in my heart..? Disregarding her original idea to take a bath, she would sit in the bathroom and merge in samadhi. Hours would pass before somebody who had been in search of her would discover her sitting in the bathroom deeply ab­ sorbed in meditation. In order to rouse her, they would pour a bucket of water over her head
60: and in this way the little one finally got her bath! If this failed to bring her back, they would violently shake her. Sometimes they had no choice but to carry her to the house. In this coastal area there were no proper toilets. Each fam­ ily would erect a small scaffold over the backwaters and en­ close the frame with woven palm leaves. As there was no floor­ ing, one would perch on a board in order to go for one's nature calls. Many times it happened that while Sudhamani sat in such a latrine she would tumble into the backwaters having lost her external awareness. Sudhamani would sit for long hours immersed in medita­ tion on the Divine Mother. Before she would sit for medita­ tion, she would make an inner resolve, "I must sit for this much of time? Then she would give a command to’the body, “Sit here, body. ” She would tell Devi, “Don’t play Your tricks on me. Keep them to Yourself. If You do not allow me to sit and meditate, then I won't let You go! ” If any external distur­ bance occurred, the little one would bite Devi and pull Her hair until she realized that she was biting her own body and ripping out her own hair. One day, Sudhamani was unable to sit for the predeter­ mined length of time as she felt someone violently shaking her body and this sensation disturbed her. Sudhamani thought, 'This is Her trick! Why is She not allowing me to sit?' Sud­ denly she opened her eyes, ran from the shrine room and re­ turned a moment later with a wooden pestle thinking to As The Child Of The Divine Mother 101 threaten and beat Devi. Raising the pestle, the little one shouted at Devi, “Today I will...” and the next moment she realized her own senselessness. She thought, ‘What! To beat Devi? Is it right? Is it possible?' She dropped the pestle and resumed her meditation. Sudhamani would not waste even a second without re­ membering the Divine Mother. If somebody happened to talk to her, she would imagine them to be Devi. The person would go on talking until they realized that the little one had mysteriously slipped off to another world. If a moment hap­ pened to pass without remembering Devi, Sudhamani would be very distressed and confess, “O Mother, this much time was wasted.” To make up for the lost time, she would increase the length of her meditation that day. If she happened to miss a meditation then she would pace the ground the entire night repeating the mantra and sincerely praying, “O Mother, what is the use of this life if I am unable to meditate on You? Other­ wise, there is only Maya waiting to devour me. O Mother, please give me strength! Grant me Your Vision! Dissolve me in Your eternally auspicious Self!” Most of all, Sudhamani liked to meditate by the oceanside in the silent
61: hours of the night. For her, the crashing waves resounded with the sacred syllable 'Aum'. The vast, deep blue heavens sparkling with countless stars reflected the limitless divinity of the Mother. Within a moment, the little one's mind would turn inward and spontaneously abide in the Self. If Sugunanandan happened to search for his daughter on those nights he would become very agitated when he was unable to find her in the house or yard. Eventually, his search would take him to the sea. There he would find the little one absorbed in deep meditation sitting like an immovable rock. Some of the villagers, not knowing the purpose of Sudha- mani's late night visits to the seaside, began spreading ru­ mours about her. Eventually this reached the ears of Sugun­ anandan who thenceforth strictly forbade his daughter from going to the sea at night.102 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI The above-mentioned incidents, which characterize the beginning phase of the little one’s Devi sadhana, only served to more fully convince her family of her insanity. These high states of Pure Devotion were beyond the realm of an ordinary person’s imagination. Sometimes Sudhamani would sob like a small child and call out to an imperceptible Being; at other times she would clap her hands and laugh aloud, then she would roll on the ground or try to kiss the ripples on the water calling out “Amma, Amma'. It is no wonder then that the little one’s flight of the alone to the Alone was mistaken for mad­ ness. Even the devotees who visited her during Krishna Bhava utterly failed to comprehend this passionate quest of Sudhamani’s for attaining oneness with the Divine Mother. It is ironic that although her family considered her as one gone mad, they never tried to discover the cause or cure. In­ stead, they persisted in their habit of taunting and torment­ ing the little one, especially her elder brother Subhagan. Due to their inhumane treatment, Sudhamani at last resolved to put an end to her life by jumping into the ocean. She cried and called to the Divine Mother, “.Am I such a wicked girl? Why do the family members persist in their cruelty? People love only those who are charming. I cannot see pure love anywhere in this world. O darling Mother, I feel everything to be an illu­ sion. O Mother, are You not the Protector of Your devotees? Am I not Your child? Have You also forsaken me? If so, then why should I carry this body? It is a burden for me as well as others. Accept Your child, O Mother Sea!” With a deter­ mined mind Sudhamani ran towards the ocean. Reaching there the little one was about to jump when she saw the vast sea to be Devi Herself. Unable to keep her mind on the physical plane, she entered into samadhi and fell unconscious on the sand. Harshan, Sudhamani’s cousin, was a well-wisher and devotee of the little one. He had overheard her parting
62: prayer as she ran from Idamannel. He followed her in a haste having somehow understood her intention. Finding her uncon­ scious on the oceanside he reverently carried her back to Idamannel thanking God that he had found her still alive.As The Child Of The Divine Mother 103 The villagers would sympathize seeing Sudhamani’s con­ dition although many of them also considered her as mad. They would remark to one another, “See, what a pitiful condi­ tion it is. Poor girl! Nobody is there to look after her; even the parents have discarded her. When she was healthy and normal she toiled day and night for them, but now they are not at all interested to care for her. Is she not their daughter?” Some of the women who lived in the neighbourhood took pity on the little one and began to lovingly serve her These women had deep admiration for this extraordinary fishergirl since her very childhood. They were now devotees of the Krishna Bhava and adored Sudhamani’s spiritual splendour and all-embracing love at those times. Somehow, they had a vague understanding of her sublime spiritual states. When­ ever they got a chance, they would extend assistance to the little one or rescue her from danger. Chellamma and her daughter Vatsala lived on the plot of land directly in front of Idamannel. Vatsala considered Sudhamani as her close friend and had boundless love for the little one. Due to their close proximity to Idamannel, it was they who often times noticed the little one's unconscious tumbles into the backwaters. Immediately, they would fish her out of the water, dry her off and dress her in a fresh set of clothes. Pushpavathi was the wife of Bhaskaran and both were ardent devotees. She considered Sudhamani as her own daughter and felt sad to see how the family tormented her. Two sisters. Rema and Rati, were living beside Idamannel and held the little one very dear to their hearts. Aisha was Sudhamani’s cousin on her aunt's side and had fond memories of the kind and affectionate Sudhamani. It was these ladies who were fortunate enough to serve Sudhamani during her days of intense tapas.3 As the little one's family completely neglected her, these women tried to render their assistance whenever they could. Oftentimes when the little one became oblivious to the surroundings, one among these ladies would find her lying in muddy water or in a dirty place. If they could 3 Severe austerities.104 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI not revive her, they' would carry her in their arms to their own house. As if she was a small child, they would brush her teeth, give her a hot water bath, dress her in good clothes and feed her with their own hands. As before, Subhagan remained at odds with Sudhamani and her Divine Mood. Several times he pressed her to stop the Krishna Bhava as he still considered it to be a shameful thing which would bring a bad reputation to the family name. See­
63: ing that he was getting nowhere with his demands, he decided to take more drastic steps. One day after the Bhava Darshan, Sudhamani was about to enter the house when she found her way obstructed by Subhagan who stood menacingly in the doorway. He shouted at her, “Do not enter this house! You will be permit­ ted to come inside only after you stop this shameful singing and dancing!" Taking his words to be the Divine Command, Sudhamani walked out of the house without murmuring a word and sat in the front yard. Subhagan ordered her not to sit there either. Hearing this the little one took a handful of sand and gave it to Subhagan saying, "If it is yours, please count this sand!" Thenceforth, she lived outdoors which was much to her liking. The sky overhead became her roof, the earth her bed, the moon her lamp and the sea breeze her fan. These austere conditions only served to intensify the little one’s renuncia­ tion and determination to realize the Divine Mother. Raising her hands above her head with tears rolling down her checks like a small child appealing to its mother, Sudhamani would ay out aloud, “Amma, Amma...have You left me here to die with longing for Your Vision? The days are passing one byone. Still I have no peace of mind without beholding Yourenchanting Form. All my hopes are pinned on Y'ou. Will Youalso forsake me? Do You not see my desperate condition?"During this period, Sudhamani wrote the following songs: O Devi, O Ambika, Beauty Personified, O Thou Who art affectionate towards devotees,As The Child Of The Divine .Mother 105 May Thou dwell here in order to end The sufferings of the devotees... Thou art everything, powerful enough to End my misery, the Tap Root of all... Thou standest as the Empress of all beings, Thou art everything and its Protector as well... Believing this lam praising Thee with Devo­ tion. O Goddess of the Universe, I desire to See Thee... Since how many days have I been desiring to see Thee...I am praising Thee without even losing a Moment... Did some mistake happen on my part or is it That Thou hast no mind to end my sorrow? Or perhaps Thou wishes t that my inner Self gets Burnt to ash. I am getting confused; I know Nothing... Will the truth that I keep in my heart that all The children are equal to the Mother become False? In order to end my misery I will request a Little of the nectar of Thy Grace pouring From the glance of Thy holy eyes... I will fall at Thy Feet in order to see ThyGracious face and beg for the boon of the fulfillment of Life... ♦ ♦ ♦ ♦ ♦106 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI O Mother, for the satisfaction of my life Give a drop of Thy Love to my dry, burning heart. Why O why dost Thou put burning fire As fertilizer to this scorched creeper? Bursting
64: out crying, how many Hot tears have I offered before Thee? Hear Thou not my heart throbbing And agony coming out as suppressed sighs? Let not the fire enter and dance Through the forest of sandalwood trees. Let not this fire of sorrow show its strength And burst firth like shattering tiles... O Devi, chanting the Name ‘Durga, Durga' My mind has forgotten all other paths. O my Durga, I want neither heaven nor Libera­ tion. I want only pure devotion to Thee... Due to her intense tapas, Sudhamani’s body would be­ come extremely hot as if she were standing in the midst of burning coals. It became so unbearable that she could hardly wear clothes. In order to get relief from the burning sensa­ tion, the little one would roll in the muddy sand of the backwa­ ters. On certain occasions, she was seen standing submerged in the backwaters for hours together in deep meditation. Sincere and ardent devotees of Sudhamani used to invite her to their homes during times of special worships. They believed that her presence would bestow spiritual splendour and power to the occasion. These families would come to Idamannel to take Sudhamani by bus to their house. Some­ times it would happen that while waiting at the bus station, Sudhamani would become God-intoxicated. Forgetting the external world, she would roll on the ground and burst into blissful peals of laughter. Of course, the people there could not comprehend the meaning of this state and would gatherAs The Child Of The Divine Mother 107 around the little one and watch her in astonishment Others would tease and rebuke her calling her a crazy girl. Children used to stand around and pooh-pooh her, but such treatment was lost on Sudhamani. What taunting words could reach the world where she was revelling? What harassment could taint this innocent girl’s intoxicated mood of Divine Bliss? In her deep anguish of separation from the Divine Mother sometime Sudhamani would cry and scream aloud. Small children would gather around her on such occasions and used to ask, "Elder sister, don't cry! Are you suffering from a head­ ache?” Later, they also came to understand that she was crying to see Devi. During these uncontrollable outbursts, one among her younger sisters would stand before the little one assuming the pose of Devi wearing a sari and cascading hair. Sudhamani in great joy would run up to her and embrace her. If the little one happened to see a beautiful girl when she was in this mood, she would run to them. Embracing and kiss­ ing them, she saw only Devi. Seeing his daughter's utter neglect of the body and taking pity on her, Sugunanandan tried several times to build a thatched shelter to protect her from the rain and sun. The little one would be lying or sitting somewhere merged in meditation. The parents would take this opportunity to con­ struct a shade above her. Coming back to the state of normal awareness
65: and seeing what her parents were doing she would move away from that place saying, “This also will become a cause for sorrow. For how many day’s will you be able to keep it here? If you go away somewhere who will maintain it? Un­ affected by anything, let me endure heat, cold and rain and thereby transcend them.” In those days of intense yearning for the Divine Mother, Sudhamani assumed the nature of a two year old child, the child of the Divine Mother. Her identification with this atti­ tude of child to Mother was so complete that many of her ac­ tions can be understood only in the light of this fact. One day, coming out of meditation, Sudhamani felt very hungry and thirsty. Just then, she saw Pushpavathi of the neighbouring108 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI house breastfeeding her baby. Straightaway Sudhamani went there, displaced the suckling baby and herself laid in the woman’s lap for nourishment. Instead of feeling awkward by this unexpected act of Sudhamani’s, Pushpavathi over­ flowed with motherly feelings for the little one. This incident repeated itself several times until Pushpavathi realized it was safer to feed her baby out of sight of the innocent-minded Sudhamani. One day, Sudhamani was lying unconscious in the mud and sand near the backwaters. Some devotees happened to find her and felt heartbroken at seeing the little one lying there with her nostrils, eyes, cars and hair full of dirt and sand. The incessant stream of tears had left visible streaks on her dark blue checks. The devotees went and informed Sugunan­ andan about her pitiful condition but this plea fell on deaf ears. Greatly afflicted by the situation, they decided to carry the little one to the house as they were unable to arouse her. They cleaned the dirt from her body and unwittingly laid her on the cot belonging to her elder brother. They left her there so that she could rest comfortably. Returning home, Subhagan found Sudhamani lying on his cot. He flew into a rage and began shaking the cot as if to break it into pieces. He became like a madman himself scream­ ing, “Who put this wretch on my bed? Who put this wretch on my bed?” The bed broke into several pieces but Sudhamani lay there in the middle of the wreckage lost to the world. Later, when she came to know about the incident and her close brush with danger, she simply said, “Whatever happens is God’s Will for the best.” The very next Darshan, much to everyone’s amazement, one devotee who was a carpenter and who was totally unaware of the previous day’s incident, presented a cot, a table and chairs to Sudhamani. When he was asked about this, he told that he had a dream in which Krishna ap­ peared and told him to bring all those things for the little one. *****************ChapterSeven FAR BETTER THAN MAN “Human beings are not the only ones with the capacity for speech. Animals, birds
66: and plants have this power but we do not have the capability to understand. One who has had the Vision of the Self knows all these things.” -Mata Amritanandamayi-On being established in harmlessness, all beings coming near him cease to be hostile. PATANJALI YOGA SUTRAS SADHANA PADAM, VERSE 35 While Sudhamani was living outdoors, dogs, cats, cows, goats, snakes, squirrels, pigeons, parrots and eagles all sought her company and became her intimate friends. This phase of her sadhana exemplifies the power of love, untainted by attraction and aversion, to bring harmony among animals who were otherwise natural enemies. At a time when her own kith and kin had abandoned her and were staunchly opposed to her spiritual life, it was these animals who stood by her in rain or shine and rendered service. If one closely observed the behaviour of these animals, it would certainly seem that they understood Sudhamani far better than the so-called human beings. In those day's, Sudhamani would not eat anything from her own house as she was extremely sensitive to food which had been prepared bv worldly people. The only food that she could eat was that which had been prepared while chanting mantras. One day, as she came out of the temple after medita­ tion, she felt very hungry and thirsty’. Lying right in front of the temple was a cow from her house. The cow kept its rear legs in a convenient position so that one could suck milk from its udders. Taking this as an arrangement made by God, this is exactly what the little one did! Playing the role of a calf, she drank directly from its udders thereby quenching her thirst and hunger to her full satisfaction.112 MATA MARITANANDAMAYI We cannot call this a mere coincidence as, from that day onwards, it became the daily habit of that particular cow to lay down in front of the temple until Sudhamani emerged from her meditation. Before feeding the little one, this cow would refuse to go to graze or feed its own calf! Annoyed, Sugunan­ andan and other family members tried several times to force the cow to move away from the spot where it would wait for the little one. They would pull the cow's tail and pour buckets of water on it, but try as they might, it would not budge an inch from in front of the temple. On some days, milk would be brought from the neig- hourhood for the little one to drink. However, it was not pure milk; it had been mixed with water. If Sudhamani ever drank this milk, she would vomit. It happened on these occasions that the people who had sent the adulterated milk for Sudha­ mani would also have to suffer. For that reason, Sudhamani decided that she would thenceforth only drink or eat what was provided by God. The very next day, an extraordinary incident occurred which shows how mysteriously the Divine Plan works. The reader might remember the village Bhandaraturuttu
67: where Sudhamani’s grandmother lived some 6 kilometres south of Parayakadavu. In that village Sudhamani’s uncle Ratnadasan as usual untied the cows from the cowshed to bring them to the front yard where he fed and bathed them. All of a sudden one of the cows wriggled away and began running towards the ocean where it took a sharp turn to the north. The cow was running at such a speed that Ratnadasan had to struggle to catch up with her. Finally the cow turned into Sudhamani’s village, where it had never been, and ran directly to Idaman­ nel. Ratnadasan's amazement knew no bounds as he observed the subsequent events. The cow proceeded straightaway to where Sudhamani was sitting in meditation and began gently nuzzling and licking the little one as if to express its love to her old friend. As Sudhamani still remained deeply immersed, the cow laid down nearby intently watching the litrlc one as ifFar Bctier Than Man 113 waiting for her to come out of meditation. After some time Sudhamani opened her eyes and noticing the vaguely familiar cow, got up and walked over to it. At that moment the cow raised one of its back legs inviting her to dnnk its milk. Sudha­ mani, for her part, drank from the udder to her full satisfac­ tion. The little one’s uncle standing a short distance away shook his head in disbelief. What mysterious power had inspired the cow to visit this unknown place where Sudhamani stayed? Clearly, the cow had a purpose behind its wild ramble along the coast. Though Sudhamani had tended this cow during her brief stay at her grandmother's house many years ago, does this explain the logic behind this animal’s unprecedented behaviour? Here our intellect must bow down to the wisdom of the heart. When Sudhamani sat outdoors in meditation on various occasions, snakes were observed coiling around her body as if to bing her awareness back to the external world. Some read­ ers may feel this to be mere coincidence but the following in­ cident is more difficult to dismiss as happenstance. One day, due to harrassment from her family, Sudhamani left the grounds of Idamannel. One lady from the neighbor­ hood met up with her, consoled her, and led the little one to the peace of her own house. Reaching there, Sudhamani en­ tered the family shrine room and began pouring her heart out to the Divine Mother. It was then that she composed the fol­ lowing song: Through my mind, speech and actions I am Remembering Yon incessantly. Why then are You delaying to show Your mercy to me, beloved Mother? Years have passed but still my mind has no peace. O darling Mother, please grant me a little relief...114 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI My mind sways like a boat caught in a storm. O Mother, give me a little peace of mind Lest I become a lunatic... I am tired Mother; it is unbearable. I don't want such a lift. I can't
68: stand Your tests. O Mother, I can't endure it! I am a miserable destitute. I have none but You Mother. Please stop Your tests, Extend Your hand and pull me up... Suddenly, her mood changed and she fell into a fit of divine madness. Crying and rolling on the ground, she began tearing at her clothes. The next moment she would burst into laugh­ ter still rolling uncontrollably. The family watched in con­ cerned amazement and had no idea of how to calm the little one. At this juncture, a large snake appeared in the doorway and crawled directly onto Sudhamani's body. The family members stared horrified as the snake began licking the un­ conscious girl’s face with its flicking tongue. This went on for some minutes and immediately had a soothing effect on Sudhamani. After a few moments, the little one's mind came down to the normal plane of consciousness. The snake crawled off her body and disappeared. It seemed to the on­ lookers that the snake knew the remedy to restore Sudha- mani’s external awareness and deftly administered to her needs. Any visitor to Idamannel will soon notice that birds of all types are an ever present feature of the natural surrounding. Sudhamani especially loved the parrots as they were dear to the Goddess. Sometimes when she would pray, "O Mother, won’t You come near me?” a flock of parrots would fly to her and settle on the ground nearby. One day a devotee presented her with a parrot which would always be seen playing around Sudhamani. The little one would never cage it. One day Sudhamani was contemplating "Oh, what a terrible, cruelFar Better Than Man 115 world it is! Not even an iota of truth or righteousness is to be found anywhere. All are deceitful and the world has become filled with sinners. It seems there is nobody to show the cor­ rect path to humanity.’ Thinking this, tears began rolling down her checks and she remained in this introspect!vc mood for a long time. At one point, Sudhamani took notice of her parrot which was standing right in front of her. She found that it was also shedding tears as if it were suffering from some pain. Her intense agony had moved the bird as well. Besides this parrot, there were two pigeons who would be seen spending time near the little one. Whenever she sang songs to the Divine Mother, the three birds would stand be­ fore her and dance gleefully to the tune spreading their wings and hopping about. In a big tree on the Idamannel property there was an eagle's nest.1 Somehow, the nest was disturbed and fell to the ground. The nest was destroyed and the two fledglings which were in the nest lay on the ground dazed and vulnerable. Some mischievous children began throwing stones at the tiny birds and tried to kill them, but soon Sudhamani arrived on the scene and saved them. She gave them shelter and carefully nursed
69: them. Within a few weeks they were strong enough to be able to fly. At this time, Sudhamani set them free. These two Garudas always came at the beginning of each Krishna Bhava and would sit on the top of the shrine for a long time. They were a great source of attraction for the devotees as the Garuda bird was the vehicle of Lord Vishnu. 'This mysterious connection which these two birds maintained with Sudha­ mani not only added visual splendour to the Darshan but also increased the devotees' faith in the little one’s divine nature. 1 The bald eagle is called Garuda in India. Garuda is the vehicle of Lord Vishnu of whom Sri Krishna is an Incarnation. During the period of Devi sadhana, whenever Sudhamani became unconscious shedding copious tears to see the Divine Mother, these two birds would fly from the sky and sit near her as if to protect her. Some of the ladies from the neighborhood116 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI would watch with wide open mouths as these two birds, gaz­ ing at Sudhamani’s face, also began shedding tears seeing her agony. One day soon after meditation, Sudhamani felt terribly hungry. One amongst the two Garudas immediately flew to­ wards the ocean and returned a few minutes later with a fish in its beak. The eagle put the fish on Sudhamani’s lap who cooly took it and ate it raw. Somehow, Damayanthi came to know of this and she used to wait for the Garuda to come and make its daily offering. As soon as the bird dropped the fish, Damayan­ thi would swoop in and grab the fish so that she could cook it for her daughter. During the sadhana period when Sudha­ mani was intent to realize Krishna, she stopped eating fish as even the smell would make her vomit. But now, as far as she was concerned, the fish brought by the Garuda was the food sent by God and so she would eat it. This daily practice of the Garuda continued for quite some time. Another animal who closely moved with the little one was a cat. This cat would enter the temple during the Bhava Darshan and go around Sudhamani as if doing pradakshina.2 It would then sit near her for a long time with its eyes dosed and seemed to the devotees to be doing meditation. One time, somebody tried to get rid of the cat by taking it across the river but the very’ next day it returned and stayed by Sudhamani’s side. Faithfulness is a common characteristic of dogs but the conduct of one black and white dog went far beyond dogged loyalty' as can be seen by the following occurrences. If Sudha­ mani cried too much to Devi and fell unconscious in her prayers, the dog would rub against her and lick her face and limbs in order to revive her. If Sudhamani was going to quit the Idamannel property, the dog would hug at her skirt andbark
70: in protest to prevent her departure. At other times hewould bring a food packet in his mouth and lay it before her toeat. The dog wouldn't eat even a grain of rice from whatever hehad brought as an offering. In the night time the dog wouldsleep near the little one and Sudhamani would use him as apillow if she ever happened to lie down to gaze at the sky.Far Better Than Man 117 One night the little one was meditating while sitting on the banks of the backwaters. She was steeped in samadhi and there was a thick blanket of mosquitoes all over her body. Sugunanandan happened to come there at that moment and began calling her. No reply came. Then he began roughly shaking her body as was the family's usual custom. He found that she had become so light in weight that it seemed as if she was a twig. “The whole body seemed like a corpse. But it wasn’t the first time we found her in that condition, so I wasn’t worried,” Sugunanandan later explained when de­ scribing the incident. He sat by his daughter but the next moment the black and white dog appeared on the scene and began fiercely barking. Within a few minutes, Sudhamani opened her eyes and came back to her normal awareness. It seemed that the animals had a greater capacity to capture Sudhamani's attention regardless of which world she hap­ pened to be revelling in. Sometimes owing to the intense love of the dog, Sudha­ mani would think that it was the Divine Mother Herself. The next moment, forgetting everything she would embrace and kiss the dog calling aloud, “My Mother, my Mother....’” One day while meditating, Sudhamani felt extremely agi­ tated. Immediately she got up and began walking towards the village in a haste. Meanwhile, the black and white dog had fallen into the hands of a dog catcher. It was howling and crying piteously, but it was not acting viciously in the least. Unable to escape from the dog catcher's sling, it was dragging its paws as its captor struggled to haul it away. Some of the village girls who were friends and admirers of Sudhamani, recognized the dog as her faithful companion and requested the dog catcher to free the dog saying that it was Sudhamani's pet. As the dog’s release was not forthcoming, they even promised a bribe if he would only let the dog go. At this mo­ ment, Sudhamani arrived. The dog looked pitifully at Sudha­ mani and began shedding tears! This was too much for the dog catcher. Looking at Sudhamani and the dog’s unquestion­ able love for her, he had no other choice but to set the dog free.118 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI In this manner the dog was saved several times from the hands of dog catchers. Another dog from the neighbourhood also had intense love for Sudhamani. One day during the dog’s pregnancy, it came to the side of the temple and stood expectantly
71: waiting. When Sudhamani came out of the shrine after meditation, she found the dog standing on the temple verandah. It did not step in, but placed its forelegs on the edge of the temple floor. It began howling in a peculiar way as if it were crying. Sudha­ mani embraced and kissed her asking, “What happened daughter, what happened?” As soon as Sudhamani did this, the dog stepped down from the temple verandah and laid in the sand not far from where Sudhamani stood. Within a few seconds, it breathed its last. Whenever somebody prostrated before Sudhamani, the black and white dog would stretch its forelegs and bow its head down before the little one. When the little one danced in devotional ecstacy, the dog would jump around her as if par­ taking in the ecstatic dance. When the sacred conch was blown during the twilight worship each evening, the dog would howl in a peculiar way closely imitating the sound pro­ duced by the conch. One day, Sudhamani had an intense feeling that her friend, the black and white dog, would die afflicted by rabies. The same happened after a couple of days yet he died without much suffering. When Sudhamani was asked if she was de­ jected to lose her steadfast companion, she told “I am not at all sad about his death. Even though he died, he will come to me. Therefore why should I am sad?” Later, she told that the dog’s soul had reincarnated nearby Idamannel but would reveal no further details. About a goat which had great love for her, Sudhamani said, “On account of a disease in its udders, the goat was struggling for life. It was about to die when I returned from some place. Seeing its agony, I sat. at a little distance and got lost in prayer and meditation. When I opened my eyes I beheld the poor animal approaching me crawling on its knees. Reaching me Fir Better Than Man 119 and placing its head on my lap, it quietly breathed its last gaz­ ing at my face. Such indeed is pure love.’” Some years later recollecting all these incidents she ex­ pressed the following: “How blissful indeed were those days! Strangely enough, those animals could understand my feelings and act accord­ ingly. If I cried, they also would join me in crying. If I sang, they would dance in front of me. When I lost my external con­ sciousness they would crawl over my body. All the traits of various animals can be found in human beings. When one gets rid of all attachment and aversion and attains equal vision, then even hostile animals will become friendly in one's pres­ ence.” * * * CHAPTER EIGHT DAZZLING LIKE A MILLION SUNS Smiling, She (Devi) became a Divine Efful­ gence and merged in me. My mind blossomed, bathed in the many-hued Light of Divinity, and the events of millions of years gone by rose up within me. Thenceforth, seeing nothing
72: as apart from my own Self a single Unity, and merging in the Universal Mother, I renounced all sense of enjoyment. -Mata Amritanandamayi-O Spouse of Siva! may Thou graciously bathe even me, who stands helpless at a far off distance, with Thy far-reaching glance, beautiful like the slightly blossomed blue lily. This mortal will derive the summum bonum of existence from such action. By such action, no loss is, after all, sustained by Thee. The snow-beamed Moon sheds the selfsame lustre on a farcst as well as on a mansion. . SAUNDARYA LAHARI VERSE 57 Placing her absolute faith in the Divine Mother, Sudha­ mani, the fisherfolk girl, swam in the Ocean of Immortal Love. For her, the whole atmosphere above, below, right and left was surcharged with Her Divine Presence. The breeze was the loving caress of the Mother. The trees, creepers and flowers were all Devi and hence worthy of Sudhamani's un­ differentiated worship. Gazing at the sky, seeing what we know not, the little one would be overcome with uncontrol­ lable fits of tears and laughter which subsided only when she fell unconscious to the sand. The heartbroken prayers of this orphaned child to her vanished Mother echoed in the air of Idamannel day and night. It was at this level of Realisation, of seeing all of Nature as being the Divine Mother only, that she wrote the following song:124 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Creation and Creator art Thou, Thou art Energy and Truth, O Goddess, O Goddess, O Goddess! Creator of the Cosmos art Thou, And Thou art the beginning and end... The Essence of the individual soul art Thou, and Thou art the five elements as well... Attended mostly by the now familiar clan of animals, Sudhamani was neither seen sleeping nor eating. She never made contact with other people unless they first approached her and even a basic task such as brushing the teeth was beyond the capability of her high-flying mind. It was noticed that if she did eat, it might be used and discarded tea leaves, cow dung, glass pieces or human excreta; she was unable to find any difference between delicious food and all of these. What words are there to describe that State which our mind and in­ tellect are incapable of grasping? No longer could the little one contain her agony and her prayers could be heard incessantly pouring forth to the Di­ vine Mother, Mother, my heart is being tom by this pain of separa­ tion’ Why does Your heart not melt seeing this endless stream of tears? O Mother, many Great Souls have adored You and thereby attained Your Vision and became eternally one with You. O Darling Mother.' Please open the doors of Your com­ passionate heart for this humble servant of Yours! I am suffo­ cating like one who is drowning. If You are not willing to come to me then please put an end to my life. Let that sword with which You behead the cruel and
73: unrighteous fall on my head as well. At least, let me be blessed by the touch of Your sword! What sense is there in keeping this useless body which is a heavy burden for me?"Dazzling Like A Million Suns 125 Sudhamani's anguish reached a pinnacle. Her prayers had been said. In her own words, “Each and every pore of my body was wide-open with yearning, each atom of my body was vibrating with the sacred mantra, my entire being was rushing towards the Divine Mother in a torrential stream...” In unspeakable agony she cried out, "O Mother...here is Your child about to die drowning in unfathomable distress...this heart is breaking...these limbs are faltering...! am convulsing like a fish thrown on shore... O Mother...You have no kindness towards me...I have nothing left to offer You except the last breath of my life...” Her voice became choked. Her breathing completely stopped. Sudhamani fell unconscious. The Will of the Mother designates the moment The Divine Enchantress of the Universe, the Omniscient, the Omnipresent, the Om­ nipotent Being, the Ancient, Primal Creatrix, the Divine Mother, appeared before Sudhamani in a living form dazzling like a million suns. Sudhamani's heart overflowed in a tidal wave of unspeakable Love and Bliss. The Divine Mother benignly smiled and, becoming a Pure Effulgence, merged in Sudhamani. What followed is best described in Sudhamani's own com­ position ‘Ananda Veethi' or ‘The Path of Bliss' wherein she has tried to make intelligible that mystical union which is beyond words. Once upon a time, my soul was dancing In delight through the Path of Bliss. At that time, all the inner foes such as Attraction and aversion ran away hiding Themselves in the innermost recesses of my mind.126 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Forgetting myself I merged in a golden dream Which arose within me. As noble aspirations Clearly manifested themselves in my mind, The Divine Mother, with bright, gentle hands, Carressed my head. With bowed head, I told Mother that my life is dedicated to Her. Smiling, She became a Divine Effulgence And merged in me. My mind blossomed. Bathed in the many-hued Light of Divinity And the events of miliums of years gone by Rose up within me. Thenceforth, Seeing nothing as apart from my own Self A single Unity, and merging tn the Divine Mother, I renounced all sense of enjoyment. Mother told me to ask the people To fulfill their human birth. Therefore, I proclaim to the whole world The sublime Truth that She uttered, uOh man, merge in your Self' Thousands and thousands of yogis Have taken birth in India and Lived the principles visualized by the Great Sages of the unknown past. To remove the sorrow of humanity. How many naked truths are there! Today I tremble with bliss Recollecting Mother's words, “Oh my darling, come to Me Leaving all other works. You are always Mine." O Pure Consciousness, O Embodiment of Truth,Dazzling Like A Million Suns 127 I will heed Your words. O Mother, why are You late
74: in coming? Why did You’give this birth? I know nothing, O Mother, Please forgive my mistakes. At this point Sudhamani developed a strong aversion towards everthing. She would dig big holes to hide herself in so as to escape from the diverse world and sensuous-minded people. She spent her days and nights enjoying the pcrrenial Bliss of God-Realisation and avoided all human company. If anyone had considered her mad before, they would stand firmly convinced of her insanity' now. Who among these fish­ erfolk could conceive of the plane of consciousness in which the little one was established? Though internally Sudhamani had crossed the threshold into the Absolute, externally she was the same crazy Sudhamani who was possessed three nights a week by Krishna as far as the family and villagers were concerned. The only recent change, if they had noticed any at all, was that instead of rolling in the sand, she was now digging big holes. Advent of the Devi Bhava One day Sudhamani heard a voice from within her say, “My child, I dwell in the heart of all beings and have no fixed abode. Your birth is not for merely enjoying the unal­ loyed Bliss of the Self but for comforting suffering humanity. Henceforth, worship Me in the hearts of all beings and relieve them of the sufferings of worldly existence...” It was after this inner call that Sudhamani started mani­ festing Devi Bhava, the Mood of the Divine Mother, in addi­ tion to the Krishna Bhava. At these times, she revealed her oneness with the Divine Mother though, for the devotees, in addition to Krishna, .she had now become possessed by Devi 128 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI as well. The following incident marks the advent of Devi Bhava. Only six months had passed since the beginning of Krishna Bhava Darshan. It was now late in 1975. One night as the Krishna Bhava was going on and the devotees were entering the temple one by one, an unexpected incident changed the whole atmosphere. As usual, a group of devotees were singing devotional songs outside the shrine room on the verandah. Sudhamani was manifesting her inner identification with that aspect of the Supreme as Sri Krishna and was receiving the devotee? joyfully. A pleasant and enchanting smile lit up His bright face and the devotees in great delight were enjoying the Di­ vine Presence. At that moment, a devotee entered the small temple completely distraught. Apparently, he had been se­ verely harassed by one of the antagonistic villagers. 1 Unable to bear the sharp remarks, he burst into tears and appealed to Krishna to find some remedy for the situation. Without warning, the gracious smile on her face vanished. Sudhamani's entire facial expression changed and became fierce as if the final dissolution had come. Her eyes looked like two smouldering iron balls. Burning with anger, it seemed as if they were emitting shooting flames all around. Her fingers were holding Devi mudra.2 All present both inside and out­ side the
75: temple were shocked to hear a boisterous laughter issuing from her whole being, They had never heard such laughter in their lives. Seeing the sudden change in Sudha­ mani, those standing in the temple began trembling out of tear not knowing what to do. Some scholars who were present began loudly chanting peace mantras and devotional songs in praise of the Divine Mother while some others showed 1. Die miscreants were still in operation and would stand along the roadsidewherethe devotees would pass and make cutting remarks. Not only the villagerswereengaged in such base conduct but Sudhamani's own father and brotherwouldindulge in such activity. They would even try to discourage the devotees fromremaining until the end of the Darshan. 2 A divine gesture associated with the Divine Mother.Dazzling Like A Million Suns 129 Aarathi.3 After much prayer and chanting of various man­ tras, she became calm and peaceful, but the Bhava had been transformed from that of Krishna to that of Devi. 3 The waving of burning camphor before the Deity. 4 Kali MataSudhamani later told, “Seeing the distress of that devotee, I felt like destroying all the unrighteous people who persist in ridiculing the devotees. Unknowingly, the Devi of fierce na­ ture4 manifested to grant refuge to the persecuted." Thence­ forth, in addition to Krishna Bhava, the Holy Mother as we will henceforth address her, regularly gave Darshan to the devotees as Devi. The Holy Mother was the embodiment of Universal Love. Those virtues which had been prevalent since her early child­ hood such as the urge to love, help and serve the people, fully unfolded. The Mother accepted the most worldly and the most spiritual, the illiterate and the well-educated, the rich­ est and the poorest, the sick and the healthy with the same equal tenderness and compassion. Patiently listening to all their problems which they came to unburden, she would adapt to the nature and maturity of each one. According to each one's need, she would guide and comfort them in all their difficulties. Soon after the beginning of Devi Bhava Darshan, there occurred certain changes in Mother. During her Devi sadhaana, she was generally aloof and uncommunicative. All her time was devoted to prayer and meditation on the form of the Divine Mother. If her parents or brother abused her physi­ cally or verbally, she would keep silent. Now she became more daring and even her facial expression changed. Her nature be­ came fearless and unyielding when it came to dealing with her parents and brother over the issues of the Bhava Darshan and her association with the devotees in particular. She now be­ gan mingling more with the devotees and instructing them spiritually. This marked the beginning of the Mother’s spiri­ tual mission.130 .MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI My Own Formless Self “From that day onwards* I could see nothing as different front my own Formless Self wherein the entire universe exists as a tiny bubble...” 5 Referring io her experience of the Divine Mother. 6 A description of
76: Kali Mata 7. A stringed instrument always seen held in the lap of Saraswati, the Goddess of Knowled ge.In this pithy utterance, the Holy Mother conveys a wealth of insight. Although established in the ultimate state of God- Realisation, the Mother performed additional sadhana to prove that all the different forms of Gods and Goddesses are facets of the same non-dual Reality. Having attained perfect control over the mind, she found that she could identify her­ self with any aspect of the Divine which she chose by her own will. Mother has narrated various experiences which she had while performing these sadhanas: "At the end of (one) sadhana one day I felt that a large ca­ nine tooth was coming out from my mouth. Simultaneously, I heard a terrific humming sound. I perceived the form of Devi with large canine teeth, a long protruding tongue, thick black curly hair, reddish bulging eyes and dark blue in colour.5 6 I thought, "Quick! Escape! Devi is coming to kill me!' I was about to run away. Suddenly I realized that I myself am Devi. The humming sound also was being produced by me. The next moment I found that I was holding Devi's veena.7 I had Her crown on my head and I was wearing the Mother's nose ring. After a couple of minutes I thought, "What is this? How have I become Devi? Maybe this is a trick played by the Divine Mother to obstruct my sadhana.' (So I thought) "Let me meditate on Siva and see what happens,' bur the moment I be­ gan meditating on Lord Siva's form, I became Him, matted hair, snakes on my neck and coiled on my upper arms. IDazzling Like A Million Suns 131 thought, ‘Maybe Siva is also testing me,’ so I stopped meditat­ ing on His form. Now I fixed my heart and soul on Lord Ganesha, the re­ mover of obstacles. Immediately my being changed to that of Ganesha, an elephant’s face with a long trunk, a pair of tusks with one half broken, and so on. Whichever form of God or Goddess I contemplated on, I became that. Then I heard a voice from within, ‘You are not different from them. They have all merged in you long ago. Then why should you call all these Gods and Goddesses?” Thenceforth, the Holy Mother’s meditation on God with form naturally subsided. The all-pervasive sacred syllable ‘OM’ sprang forth from within the Holy Mother and her whole being was forever merged in That. Even then, to set an example, she would sit and meditate. When asked about this, the Holy Mother told, “During meditation. Mother will go near all the children, especially to those who are intensely thinking of Mother or to those who are suffering.” A similar incident occurs in the great epic, Srimad Bhagavatam. One day, when the renowned sage Narada vis­ ited Dwaraka, the Abode of Sri Krishna, he found the Lord sitting in deep meditation. Narada reverentially bowed to
77: the Lord and asked, “O Lord, whom are You meditating on?” The Lord smilingly replied, “I am meditating upon my devotees.” It should be kept in mind that although the little one had become ‘Mother’ in the eyes of many, she remained Sudha­ mani as far as her family was concerned. Her natural abidance in the Supreme Self was far too subtle for her parents and elder brother to understand even if they had tried. They continued to doubt and misinterpret her behaviour as schizophrenia. They feared that her contact with the devotees would result in her deviating from the path of morality and thus bring disre­ pute to the family. Her brother Subhagan was especially fa­ natical in his aggression against the Mother, and his behavi­ our towards her had gone from bad to worse as shown by the following incident.132 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI One day, Subhagan and a few of his cousins called the Mother to a relative’s house under some false pretext. When she arrived they locked her in a room of the house. One of her cousins began threatening her and suddenly pulled out a long knife which he had hidden in his clothing. Subhagan spoke, “This behaviour of yours has gone too far. You are bound to spoil the family name. Since you cannot stop mixing freely with all sorts of people and persist in your singing and danc­ ing, it is better that you die.” He was enraged to hear the Mother laugh and retort, “I am not at all afraid of death. The body must meet its end sooner or later, but it is impossible for you to kill the Self. Now that you are determined to put an end to my physical existence, I will express a last wish of mine. It is obligatory’ that you fulfill it. Let me meditate for a while and then you are free to kill me while I am in meditation." Hearing her daring answer, they became more infuriated. One among them exclaimed, “Who are you to command us? are we here to kill you or not kill you according to your wish?" Mother smiled and boldly retorted, “It seems that nobody but God alone can put an end to my life!" Another of the cous­ ins shouted, “God! Who is your God!" Though they verbally threatened the Holy Mother, none among them was coura­ geous enough to do anything against her after hearing her bold reply and seeing her unperturbed nature. Suddenly the cousin who had brandished the knife jumped forward and pressed the knife against her chest as if to stab her. But he could not make another move as he was immedi­ ately afflicted by an excruciating pain in his own chest at the exact point where he had pressed the knife against the Holy Mother. He himself fell to the floor in agony. Seeing this, the others in the room were filled with dread. At this moment, Damayanthi, who had been fearful seeing Sudhamani
78: leaving alone with Subhagan and his cousins, arrived on the scene. Entering the house, she heard the uproar and guessed that something very wrong was going on. She began pounding on the door and crying for them to open it. The door was un­ locked and opened. Damayanthi took the Holy Mother by the hand and quickly left the house. 'Dazzling Like A Million Suns 133 On the way back to Idamannel, they walked along the sea­ shore. The Mother told Damayanthi, “Your people are dis­ honoured by me. This ocean is my Mother as well. She will accept me happily with outstretched arms. I am going to Her lap.” Hearing her words, Damayanthi became mentally un­ balanced. She began screaming, “Don't say that, daughter! Don’t say like that, daughter! During Krishna Bhava, Bhagavan told me that if you happen to commit suicide, all my children will go insane...” Somehow she succeeded in dis­ suading the Holy Mother and took her back to Idamannel. This episode does not end here. The cousin who had raised the knife against the Mother to stab her was later hospital­ ized. Though he was provided with excellent medical treat­ ment, he finally died continuously vomiting blood. When his disease was in its acute stages, the Holy Mother went to see him in the hospital. She lovingly consoled him and fed him with her own hands. He was deeply repentent towards the Holy Mother for his grave mistake and burst into tears having experienced the compassion and forgiveness of the Holy Mother for himself. Here the readers should notice that the Holy Mother had no aversion towards her cousin who had attempted to murder her, nor had she made any resolve for the ill-fate that he met. He had suffered the fruit of his action. The Mother explained, “Just as human beings have intense love for Mother, there ae numerous subtle beings who love her as well. If somebody attempts to harm Mother, Mother will not react. Mother will face it without any excitement and will not even think any­ thing against him who acts out of ignorance. But these subtle beings will get angry and take revenge. Do you understand how this is? Suppose someone's mother is attacked by a man. Do the children sit back and not care about it? Even if their mother tries to stop them, they will find that man and take revenge.”134 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Transcending the limitations of worldly existence, the Holy Mother would receive the devotees without distinction of caste, creed, class or gender. In the eyes of the ignorant non-believers, the equal vision and broad-mindedness of the Holy Mother was just the manifest symptom of a mental aber­ ration. The miscreants continued to enter the temple during the Bhava Darshan and unnecessarily question the Mother. Though the Mother remained unaffected and calm, Sugun- anandan became very depressed due to their insolent re­ marks. Furthermore, although all his attempts to arrange the marriage of his daughter had ended in
79: failure, he still could not completely let go of this idea. He now began to feel the Bhava Darshan was a great impediment to fulfill his wish. He joined Subhagan in his firm belief that the Darshan was a shameful thing. Something else also worried him. His daugh­ ter’s body would become stone-hard after the Bhava and only hours of strong massage would return it to normal. Such was the state of Sugunanandan’s mind when he made a firm resolve with Subhagan to somehow put an end to the Bhava Darshan. With this thought in mind, Sugunanandan entered the temple during the next Devi Bhava. He told the Holy Mother, “Devi should go away from the little one’s body. Here we don't need this Bhava Darshan anymore. We want to give her in marriage. I want my daughter back!"8 8 It should be remembered that as far as her family was concerned, the Holy Mother was possessed three nights a week by Krishna and Devi and the rest of the time she was a crazy girl 9 Since her young age she had accepted only God as her real Mother and Father and thus everybody else was her stepmother and stepfatherThe Holy Mother addressed him as stepfather9 and asked, “Is it your daughter?" You will have to bury her, not marry her.” Not Dazzling IJkc A Million Suns 135 being in the mood to listen, Sugunanandan demanded, “Let the Goddess go back to Her own place! I want my child back!” The Mother said, “If so, here is your daughter. Take her!” Instantly the Mother fell down on the spot. Within a few moments her body became stiff and her heartbeat stopped. Though her eyes were wide open, there was no sign of life to be seen. She was dead. A hue and cry arose. All those who had come for the Darshan became dumb with grief. Damayanthi and her other daughters fainted on the ground. The news flashed that Devi had taken away Sudhamani’s life due to some error commit­ ted by Sugunanandan. Everyone accused him of being the cause for the untimely death of the Mother. Oil lamps were lit all around the body. Even Nature had become silent in that moment. Some of the devotees burst into tears, others were blabbering like idiots due to sudden and uncontrollable emotional upset. Others sat solemnly near the body testing to see if there was any breath coming from the Mother by holding their hand near her nostrils. Nothing. A doctor who was present checked her pulse. Noth­ ing. It was a terrible moment. Coming to realize the horror of the situation caused by his indiscriminant
80: action and unable to bear the excruciating pain in his heart, Sugunanandan also fell unconscious. A mournful silence reigned ail around. Thinking that the im­ possible had truly happened, all hope of her revival was given up. Eight long hours dragged by. Regaining his conscious­ ness only to confront the dreadful scene, Sugunanandan cried aloud his prayer to the Divine Mother, Devi! I beg of You to please forgive me for speaking such words which I ut­ tered out of extreme ignorance! Please bring my daughter back to life! Forgive my fault! Never again will I repeat such a despicable action!” Imploring thusly, he fell flat on the ground weeping uncontrollably in full prostration before the temple. Minutes passed like hours. Suddenly one among the devo­ tees noticed faint signs of movement in the Holy Mother's 136 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI body. With soaring hopes, the devotees stood by and their rears of sorrow were transformed into tears of joy. The Mother came back to life, but in Krishna Bhava! Addressing Sugunanandan, who was an ardent devotee of Krishna, she told him, “Without Shakti10 there can be no Krishna!” 10 The feminine aspect of the Cosmic Energy personified in the form of DeviThis incident brought about a great change in the father’s attitude towards God and his daughter. Thenceforth, he left her to do more or less as she liked and never again attempted to arrange a marriage for her. Later, the Mother remarked about this particular incident, “He was adamant about getting his daughter back from Devi. But if this is really their daughter, then they should have the power to bring her back to life as well. That they could not do. At the most this body is theirs. When he demanded his child back, this body was given.” * * * * *CHAPTER NINE THE SWORD OF TRUTH Children, even when a man is cutting a tree down from its root, it gives him shade. A spiritual aspi­ rant should also be like this. Only he who prays even for the welfare of those who torment him can become a spiritual person. A spiritual aspirant's greatest weapon is the sword of Truth. -Mata Amritanandamayi-O Devi, Your nature is to subdue the conduct of the wicked; this Your peerless beauty is incon­ ceivable for others; Your power destroys those who have robbed the devas of their prowess, and You have thus manifested Your compassion even towards the enemies. DEVI MAHATMYAM CHAPTER 4, VERSE 21 The Ill-Fated Subhagan It seems that all Great Souls must endure the persecution of the mediocre minded. However, the Great Ones seem to thrive on this as even’ obstacle thrown in their path adds addi­ tional brilliance to their glory. We need only look at the life of Sri Krishna, Sri Rama, Jesus Christ and Buddha to find ample evidence of this fact. The Holy Mother's life is also a shining example. Three years had now passed. It was the middle of the year 1978. The number
81: of devotees continued to increase day by day as people flocked from all parts of India for the auspi­ cious Darshan of the Holy Mother. As the number of admir­ ers and well-wishers increased, the miscreants also stepped up their evil campaign. But no worldly power could obstruct the predestined spiritual mission of the Holy Mother. 140 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI At this time, certain bad omens were seen which presaged an impending catastrophe at Idamannel. Subhagan was not at all put off by the ill consequences of his attempt to have his sister murdered in cold blood. He now became more openly arrogant and hostile towards the Holy Mother. He tried to impose his own selfish ideas on the whole family. Even they were afraid to oppose him because of his volatile, hot-tem­ pered nature. The increasing number of devotees and the unabated slander of the rationalists set his mind on fire with restlessness. He began accosting devotees coming for the Bhava Darshan and severely abusing them hoping to dissuade them from partaking in the Darshan. At this juncture, either due to fate or as a result of his vile actions bearing fruit, Subhagan became afflicted with the terrible disease elephantiasis. Symptoms of this disease ap­ peared on both his hands and legs. Although he underwent various medical treatments, none had a curative effect. The thought that he had become terminally ill plagued him con­ stantly. He became severely depressed and developed suici­ dal tendencies. Several times he expressed his mental anguish to his close friends. He developed insomnia as a result and resorted to taking sleeping pills. The cumulative effect of his physical and emotional afflictions took its toll and gradually Subhagan lost his mental balance. One day the Holy Mother called Damayanthi and told her, “It seems that brother Subhagan is nearing the end of his life span. As a solution you can take a vow of silence, but certain obstacles can be seen which will try to break your vow.
