152: first time that I was going to be away from her and that too, for one month. My heart was very’ heavy. In the train I sat in a comer to hide my tears. All the passengers were happily engaged in chatting but my mind was full of sorrow because of my separation from Mother. All the way I thought of her. The following morning I reached Tirupati. My days were filled with excruciating pangs of separation. I felt like a fish taken out of water. I tried to concentrate on my studies but failed. Each minute moved like a snail. I was even unable to look at Mother’s picture. Each object brought from the Ashram reminded me of Mother and her gracious form. I forgot to eat and sleep. Each day was like a year for me. Now and then I collapsed. Unable to bear the pain of separation, I burst into tears. By the time the final year exams began I somehow managed to write the papers. There was nobody to share my sorrow with. At that time, I received a letter from Mother. Several times I read and reread it. Soaked by my teardrops, the letter turned wet. The following is the translation of Mother's letter to me: Darling Son, Your Mother is always with you. Son, Mother doesn’t feel that you are away from her. My child. Mother can see your yearning heart. Mother can hear your cries. My son, this world is so beautiful. The flowers, the vast ocean, the chirp­ ing birds, the expanse of the sky, trees, bushes, forests, moun­ tains and valleys are all here. God made this earth beautiful. See Him in everything. Love Him through all beings. CutExperiences Of Devotees 251 open the bund separating you from God. Let your mind inces­ santly flow towards Him. Son, nothing is bad in this world. Everything is good. See the good and virtuous pan. Let your mental flower blossom and spread its fragrance all around... “That night I was sitting outside the room. The trees and plants danced in the cool gentle breeze. The sky was full of glittering stars and the silver rays of the moon lighted the earth with all its splendour. I thought, ‘This breeze may go to my Mother and it may be fortunate enough to carrying my Mother’s body. Yes, no doubt this is carrying my dear Mother’s divine fragrance. If I had a pair of wings I would fly to my Mother. ’ The following piece of poetry was written by me on the same night. O stars, can't you please come down? Mother is here to sing a lullaby to you. She is the stream oj never-ending love and She is the shade-giving tree for seeking minds. O cool gentle breeze who comes slowly humming Silent songs in the night, what did you whisper so Sweetly in my ears? Is it the sweet stories of my Mother? The sun and moon slowly rise and set Up
153: in the blue sky everyday. Don't you have the desire to see my Mother who has given this divine splendour to You? Trees and creepers grow in abundance in The silent lonely valleys and hill sides. As if to console me their tender branches dance In The wind. “I was in a completely different mood. I roamed about in the room like one gone mad. Somehow I controlled myself and decided to leave the next day itself. One more subject remained among the final year papers. I decided not to appear for the first year exams which were to start after four days. I 252 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI thought, ‘Mother has asked me to attend all the exams but now I am going to act against her advice? “Finally I decided to seek Mother’s permission in a pecu­ liar way. I took three pieces of paper of equal measurements. On the first piece I wrote, ‘Son, you come back'. On the see­ ond one I wrote ‘You write all the papers and come’ and on the third I wrote ‘As my son likes’. I rolled all the three papers in the same manner, shook them and took Mother’s picture from the bag and placed it in a comer. With this prayer I hum­ bly offered the papers in front of her, ‘O Mother, I am going to take one of these papers from here. Let me know your will whatever it be? “With closed eyes, I took one of the papers with trembling hands. I opened it. Alas’ it was the one on which I had written ‘You write all the papers and come; Not satisfied with the first attempt, I tried my lot again with three more pieces of paper but again I picked up the same note. My mind however, was yearning to see Mother and so I finally decided to leave the place the next day itself. “The next day, after attending the last subject of the final year exams, I rushed to the room and packed all my things and was about to leave the room. Suddenly, I noticed certain things lying in the corner of the room. They were some useless newspapers which I had used as a wrapper to bring things from the Ashram and also a broken piece of a soap box. I thought, ‘How intense was my pain when I was separated from Mother. These things also may have the same pain as myself. If I leave them here it will be a sin? I carefully packed all those things and put them in my bag. “The following day I reached the Ashram. On the way to Mother’s room I saw my brother Venu. In an astonished tone he said, ‘Yesterday night Mother told me that you are getting very restless and that you will come today? I entered Mother's room and fell at her feet crying. Mother lifted me up and con­ soled me saying ‘Son, I know your heart, this love is
154: good but try to acquire more mental strength. A sadhak should be soft like a flower and hard like a diamond. You must go and writeExperiences Of Devotees 253 the other papers. Even if you fail. Mother won’t mind. To­ morrow itself you go and come back when the exams are over.’ "The next morning I went back to Tirupati. After one week when the last exam was over, I returned to the Ashram. I was not satisfied with my answers and even feared that I may fail. Mother calmly said, ‘Forget about it. Don’t doubt. You will succeed.’ When the results were published I was surprised to see that I had passed with a high second class. “To be in the presence of the Holy Mother itself is a tapas. There is always something new and fresh in it. Every moment reveals a novel experience enlightening the devotee in a dif­ ferent realm of spirituality, evolving him from one plane to the other. In the beginning stages of my spiritual life I some­ times used to feel that I had understood the Holy Mother. Later I realised that I had understood nothing about Mother at all.” ***** Venu is the brother of Balu (Amritatma Chaitanya.) Both of them were infants when their mother died. After her death, Balu grew up in his father's house and Venn was raised by his aunt (mother's eldest sister) Saraswathy Amma. The family atmosphere was quite religious and spiritual. Venu was the pet of the family and never experienced the absence of a mother’s love and affection. After completing his schooling at the age of 15, Venu came to his father to continue his college education. Though he manifested an inclination towards spirituality at an early age, during his college years all such qualities faded into the background and he led a worldly life. Even then, whenever he would see a devotional movie or a monk in ochre robes he would feel again the stirrings of his dormant spiritual impulse. By this time, his brother Balu had already met the Holy Mother and had dedicated himself to spirituality’. Though Balu told his brother about the Holy Mother several times, Venu paid little attention to it. Moreover, he openly abused 254 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI the Holy Mother and asserted, “I won’t come near that fisher­ girl.” However, even before seeing Venu, Amma predicted to Balu, “Your brother also is my son. He will come here.’1 Hear­ ing this, Balu became a bit worried because there was already an uproar in his family over his own decision to leave home and the worldly life. What would happen if Venu also followed in his footsteps? However, the Divine Will is supreme and be­ yond the realm of ordinary mortal vision and calculation. What was fated would inevitably come to pass. While Venu was studying for the final year of his Bachelor of Science degree, the Holy Mother visited his aunt’s house one day. When Venu returned home from college,
155: the Holy Mother was standing on the verandah of the house. Even without looking at her, Venu strode past Mother into his room where Sreekumar, Pai and some other residents of Mother’s Ashram were seated. Suddenly and unexpectedly, the Holy Mother ap­ proached Venu and holding his hands like a loving mother said, “Aren’t you my son Balu’s brother? Mother has been yearning to see you.” Venu’s heart melted and he realized in aflash that Mother was not an ordinary personality, but rathera fountain of motherly love and tenderness. Venu felt at­tracted to her as a piece of iron to a magnet. In the afternoonwhen Amma fed everyone, Venu also got a ball of rice. It wasan unforgettable experience for him. He felt enchanted to seeher infinite love, equal vision and childlike innocence. Herface was radiant and overflowing with spiritual splendour.Her lucid way of explaining spiritual mysteries, ecstatic andbewitching bhajans, and above all her absolute humility im­pressed him deeply. Within a short period of time, Venufound himself being drawn to the Mother. Even though theHoly Mother was talking to others it seemed to Venu that shewas actually responding to the doubts which had cropped upin his mind. The first meeting with the Holy Mother left a deep impres­ sion in Vcnu’s mind and all the prejudices which he had about the Holy Mother and spiritual life vanished. Day by day hisExperiences Of Devocecs 255 wish to see the Mother increased. At last in February, 1980 he came to Vallickavu. When Venu saw the Mother he burst into tears. The Holy Mother caught hold of him and made him sit near her. That night, when Venu went inside the temple dur­ ing Krishna Bhava, he felt that he was standing before Lord Sri Krishna Himself. His mind overflowed with joy and he could neither cry nor laugh. He prayed to Mother to bless him by bestowing pure devotion and knowledge on him. Mother said, uSon, you will get what you are seeking?’ Mother gave him a mantra written on a piece of paper and a garland of tulasi leaves. After Venu's first meeting with the Holy Mother, he lost all desire to continue his studies, his only wish being to lead a spiritual life. Due to the Mother’s insistance, Venu prepared for his college examinations which were one month away. Professors and students were amazed to see Venu coming to the college with a completely shaven head and sacred ashes on his forehead. They thought that he had gone mad. His mind was entirely immersed in the thought of the Holy Mother. So much so, he inadvertantly prepared for the following day's examination and not for that day’s paper. Somehow or other he did complete the exams and came to stay with the Holy Mother in September, 1980. One day in connection with a festival, sweet pudding was prepared in the Ashram. It was customary to offer it to God before distributing it among the devotees. Venu took a tum­ bler full
156: and placed it in the small shrine before the temple. Finding nothing else to cover the tumbler with and looking around to make sure that Mother was not nearby, he plucked a tender leaf off a small plant growing near the shrine. Mother saw it and from a distance called aloud ‘Hey Venu!' Hearing Mother’s voice, he tried to hide the tender leaf but in his haste he tipped the tumbler spilling over the entire contents upon the sand. By this time, Venu was completely undone and, hoping to escape further notice, he scooped the pudding from the sand and stuffed it back into the tumbler even though knowing that it was wrong to put it back in the shrine.256 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Mother, who was observing the whole scene from a dis­ tance, came near him and said in a serious tone, “Son, even a dog won’t eat this. What to say of human beings? Then how could you offer it to God? Son, will even you eat it? No! This isa real sin. God will accept anything that is offered to Him withpure love and devotion not caring about what it is. He onlysees what is the attitude behind the offering. If you were reallyignorant about your deed, I would not have minded it, butwith full knowledge that what you were doing was wrong,you proceeded to do it. Not only that. You have committedanother mistake by plucking a tender leaf from that smallplant. How merciless you are! I can see the small plant cryingout of pain. If someone pinches you, how much pain you willsuffer. Son, even though you don’t feel its pain. Motherdoes.” Venu realised his mistake and repented. He prayed for forgiveness. Mother said, “Son, whatever errors you com­ mit, I take to be due to some fault of my own. Mother is not at all angry’ with you but in order to lead you on the path to Per­ fection she has to pretend to be so.” Venu says, “Nothing can be concealed from the Holy Mother. She knows everything. About 5 years ago I had an experience which exemplifies this fact. One night during supper while everyone was drinking kanji (rice gruel), I sud­ denly had a strong desire to have some mango pickles as a side dish. I had seen them in the Ashram kitchen earlier in the day but, as they were meant for the labourers and visiting devo­ tees, we residents were not supposed to take them. Also, Mother had told us that as spiritual aspirants, we should not eat things which were very spicy, sour, salty or sweet. She would often come into the kitchen unannounced to see whether her instructions were being followed. Although I was fully aware of this, the desire for the pickles got the better of me. Without making any noise, I entered the kitchen and stealthily stole two big slices of mango pickle and was about to leave when suddenly I heard Mother’s voice, ‘Venu, what is
157: inExperiences Of Devotees 257 your hand?’ I was shocked and to avoid being caught red- handed, I threw the mango slices away. Mother then searched and found the slices and approaching me, caught hold of my hands and tied them to a post. I was ashamed and full of fear.” Seeing his childlike fear and innocence. Mother burst into laughter. Actually, the Holy Mother was enjoying looking upon Venu as the child Krishna who was tied to a mortar by His mother Yashoda for stealing butter and milk from the Gopis' houses. After a couple of seconds. Mother untied him and lovingly served him with some mango pickles. She said, “Son, only if the taste of the tongue is controlled can one en­ joy the taste of the heart.” Mother has her own ways to exhaust the negative tenden­ cies of her spiritual children. Sometimes she will say, “I am a crazy girl who knows nothing.” She will pretend as if she is an ignorant and innocent village girl but her eyes will be pene­ trating to the truth of everything. Once the mistake is de­ tected, then one can see the Great Teacher in her rising up and instructing the student befittingly while concealing her Motherhood temporarily. ***** Sreekumar, a resident in the Holy Mother’s Ashram, was an electronic engineer before coming to her. While studying for his B.A. in 1979, he heard about a woman who could as­ sume divine moods and bless devotees in various ways accord­ ing to their particular problems. Though believing in God, he doubted that Divinity could manifest through a human being. Observing the nature of this world where a few are happy and the majority suffer, his faith in an all-good God waned. It was at this juncture that he came to know about the Holy Mother and decided to go and see for himself whether or not she possessed divine power. With a skeptical mind, he came to the Ashram in March, 1979 and entered the shrine room drawing dose to the Holy Mother. Her loving and compassionate glance entered deep258 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI into Sreekumar’s heart. Her mere presence transported him to another world where God, His Holy Name and himself alone existed and he became oblivious to his surroundings. This experience bound him to Mother and his mind became filled with the thought of her alone. About his second meeting with the Holy Mother Sreekumar says, “I heard some people calling her ‘child’ (kunju) while others called her ‘Mother’ (Amma). After Bhava Darshan, she talked with the devotees. All of a sudden, she would behave like a small and innocent child. She would play with the devotees and seeing her innocent sports, their hearts would rejoice forgetting everything else. Sometimes she would sing and dance and the next moment, while hearing a song, she would shed tears and sit motionless as if lost to the world. Some bowed down before her and others kissed her hand and still others sang devotional
158: songs. Then, like one gone mad she would roll on the ground and laugh." In the beginning, Sreekumar felt that Mother was being temporarily possessed by the Divine Mother Kali and Krishna but gradually, due to close association with her, he came to understand that she was actually manifesting her inner identity with the Supreme Reality. Sreekumar's relationship with the Holy Mother strength­ ened day by day. It became very difficult for him to be away from her. Whenever he could get time, he would avail it by spending some moments near the Holy Mother. Sometimes the Holy Mother would feed him with her own hands and would give him spiritual instructions at the same time. One day she asked him, “Has Mother given you a mantra to re­ peat?” He replied, “Yes, it was written on a small piece of paper and given for the improvement of my studies." Mother then told him, “Son, during Devi Bhava, Mother will initiate you.” That night, Sreekumar was initiated with a mantra after which he decided to dedicate his life to spirituality under the Holy Mother's guidance. Although Sreekumar's parents were devoted to the Holy Mother, they disagreed with him about his becoming a monk.Experiences Of Devotees 259 Their objection was mainly due to the fact that his father was retired and his sister still remained to be married. They there­ fore arranged a job for him in Bangalore about 600 kilometres away. On certain days when his breaking heart would yearn too much for her presence, he would get a vision of her. In order to console him. Mother used to send him occasional let­ ters. It was at this time that he wrote the following song: O Mother, even though You are near I am wandering unable to know You. Even though I have eyes I am searching unable to see You. Are You the beautiful moon That blooms forth in the blue winter night? I am the wave that, unable to reach the sky Beats its head against the short. When I came to understand the truth That all worldly comforts are worthless I longed to know You Shedding tears day and night. Won't You come to comfort me Who am weary of the burden of sorrow? With the desire that You will come I am waiting always. Owing to his intense desire to see the Holy Mother and to stay with her, Sreekumar returned home even before the completion of one month in Bangalore. Laid up with fever, he was hospitalised immediately after arriving home. His long­ ing to see the Mother went on increasing until one morning at 4 a.m., he had a wonderful experience. ttMy father had gone out to get some coffee for me. I was alone in the room when all of a sudden my hands and legs be­ came as if paralysed. A cool and gentle breeze carressed me and to my great surprise I saw Mother entering the room.260 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI With a
159: benign smile on her face, she walked towards me. Like a small child, I started crying. She then sat near me and put my head on her lap. She didn't speak anything. I was overcome with emotion. Words stuck in my throat. An effulgence from Mother's body pervaded the room and she herself was sur­ rounded by a divine light. At that moment, the door opened and my father walked in. The Holy Mother immediately van­ ished.” A few days later, Mother visited Sreekumar's house. It was in the morning and she was sitting in front of the house play­ ing with some children. All of a sudden, she got up and walked through the fields towards the cast with her hands forming a mudra. After walking a short distance, she entered a forest where a certain family had been worshipping snakes. In a semi-conscious mood and with half-closed eyes, she cast a bewitching smile and sat on the small shrine built for the snake worship. Several people gathered there to see this unusual sight. However, no one would enter the forest which was full of poisonous snakes. Hearing about the news, the owners of the forest also came there and stood before Mother with joined palms. They asked, “Mother, we are doing the worship without any break. Do we have to perform anything more?” Mother replied, “Keep a glass of fresh water here daily. That will suf­ fice.” When the Holy Mother came back to the house, they asked her, “Mother, what made you go there?” She replied, “Worship of the snakes has been going on there since a very long time. Mother went there to satisfy the desire of the pre­ siding deities which have been installed in that forest. From the moment that I arrived here, I have been having the feeling that they are beckoning me.” Shortly after this, Sreekumar's parents found him a job in Bombay. Their insistence was so great that finally Sreekumar had no other choice but to go there. With great reluctance he proceeded to Bombay, once more separated from the Holy Mother. While travelling in the train, he intensely felt the presence of the Holy Mother. In a half-waking, half-sleepingExperiences Of Devotees 261 state he incessantly enjoyed visions of her and the bliss of her Divine Presence. Finally, after eight months he was unable to bear the separation from her any longer and submitted his res­ ignation to his employer. During his stay in Bombay, Sreekumar wrote this piece of poetry which reveals his aching heart: The sun has set in the western ocean And the day has started its lament... It is but the play of the Universal Architect. So why should you, O closing lotuses, be dejected? This world, full of misery and sorrow Is but a drama of God and I, the onlooker, Am but a wooden puppet in His hands Having no tears to shed. Like a flame, my mind is burning up in Separation from You in
160: this ocean of grief. lam getting tossed about Unable to find the shore. Even before coming to Mother or taking up to spiritual life seriously, Sreekumar used to have certain experiences in the astral plane of existence. While lying down, he would feel his subtle body coming out of his gross body and travelling around. During such moments, even though his eyes were closed, he would clearly see the objective world. During his stay in Bombay, he had a thrilling experience. It was daytime and he was relaxing with his eyes closed after meditation. Suddenly, his body became stiff. He felt his subtle form become separated from the gross one and imme­ diately heard a thundering sound followed by an outpouring of billows of smoke into the atmosphere in the midst of which he beheld the figure of the Holy Mother dressed in the colourful costume which she wears during Devi Bhava. The magnificent stature of the Holy Mother created awe and rev-262 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI erence in his mind. A few minutes passed while beholding this sublime vision, himself unable to move or open his eyes. On the evening of the 28th of January, 1980 Srcckumar was about to go to his house to visit his parents when Mother stopped him saying, “Stay here; don't go anywhere today.” In his own words, “I was happy to hear Mother’s words and cancelled my plans to go out. At around 6 o’clock in the eve­ ning I was standing outside talking to some people when sud­ denly something bit me on the leg. I cried out in pain hearing which Mother came running to the spot. Immediately find­ ing the wound. Mother sucked out the blood and poison and then spat it out. Inspite of this, the pain became unbearable. Seeing me rolling in agony due to the excruciating pain. Mother tried to comfort me. Finally, due to the insistence of others, she allowed me to be taken to a doctor specializing in snake bites. The doctor told, 'The snake which has bitten you was an extremely poisonous one but strangely enough the poison does not seem to have affected your body or blood.’ By Mother's loving and affectionate care, I at last fell asleep at 3 a.m. only after which Mother herself retired. The next morning Mother told me, 'Son, wherever you might have been, you were destined to be bitten by a snake. However, as it happened in Mother's presence, nothing seri­ ous has come of it. That is why Mother prevented you from going away from here yesterday.’ Later, after reaching my house, I went through my horoscope and was surprised to find that something had been written therein concerning that would-be fatal incident. It said, 'At the age of 22 there is the probability of poisoning. Therefore, special worship and offerings in the temple should be made for good health’.” By the Grace of the Holy Mother, Sreekumar had several spiritual experiences which always served as a source of
161: inspi­ ration for him to continue his sadhana with more and more enthusiasm. After making some provision for his parents and sister, he came to settle permanently at the Ashram.Experiences Of Devotees 263 Pai and Rao were relatives who were also close friends. They were like one soul in two bodies. Pai was a devotee of the Mother Kali. Hearing about the Holy Mother from his friend Rao, Pai came to see her in 1979. Then he was 21 years old. Getting off at a nearby bus stand, he asked a bystander to tell him the way to the Ashram. Instead of answering Pai’s ques­ tion, the man abused Mother and even tried to prevent him from going there saying, “Don't go there, it is a place where all kinds of evil things go on.” “Let me go and see for myself,” Pai responded and then he proceeded towards the Ashram. It was a Bhava Darshan day and there were a large number of people present. A soulful song ‘O Mother, the knower of the beginning, the middle and the end (of Creation), the Eter­ nal Virgin’ sung by the Holy Mother, welcomed Pai to the Ashram. Shortly after this. Mother revealed her identity with the Divine Mother of the Universe during the Bhava Darshan. When his turn came, Pai entered the temple and approached the Holy Mother. When he gazed upon her face overflowing with motherly’ love, Pai burst into tears. He tried to control them, but could not do so. His heart was full of bliss and within a few minutes he experienced a rare calm and peace of mind. The Holy Mother told him, “Son, I am your Mother.” The moment the ^Mother uttered these words, Pai again cried aloud like a small child. For all practical purposes, Pai was an orphan. Though both his father and mother were alive, it was as if they had died, since they had separated and he had no contact with them, being raised by his grandmother and grandfather. Now, for the first time in his life, he heard a true mother's sincere words of love. Deep within him, Pai felt that his relationship with the Holy Mother was not a new one but had existed since time immemorial. That first meeting with Mother created a very strong im­ pact on Pai's mind. The Mother's form became deeply im­ printed on his heart. Many things would vividly remind him of Mother. If, while walking on the road, he happened to hear the tinkling of anklets, he would suddenly turn and look264 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI thinking that it was Mother who had come to him (Amma used to wear anklets with small bells attached in those days). If he happened to see a girl with a nose ring, he would feel as if it were the Holy Mother herself. With each passing day his devotion intensified. His tender feelings knew no bounds. At that time he was employed in a shop, but as he
162: could not concentrate on the work he soon resigned. However, not being able to stay permanently at the Ashram due to lack of facilities, Pai obtained a job as a clerk in another shop only to resign once more six months later after realizing that the company and atmosphere were not condu­ cive to spirituality. It was a place where one could easily fall a prey to intoxicants and women. After meeting the Holy Mother, a strong aversion developed in Pai’s mind towards all worldly enjoyments. Pai's new way of life did not win the: favour of his relatives, friends or the villagers. Some tried to advise him against going to the Holy Mother, but Pai utterly ignored them. They spread rumours that it was Pai who took Rao to the Holy Mother and that it was he who was responsible for Rao's sud­ den change. This reached the cars of Rao's parents and other family members. They too turned against Pai. Rao's friends also joined with them. They’ threatened Pai saying that they would chop off his legs if he stepped into Haripad. His un­ shakeable faith in the Holy Mother gave him the courage and mental strength to confront these challenges. In those days, the Mother's parents would not allow most of her spiritual children to visit the Ashram except on Darshan days. Having left his house and relatives, Pai would spend his nights on shop verandahs, in bus stations and temple compounds. As one gone mad, he would wander around. Sometimes he would get something to eat, at other times he would starve for days together. A soul which thirsts to attain eternal union with God won't care about all these things. Days, weeks and months passed. Still Pai would come to see the Holy Mother on all the Darshan nights and at other times when she would visit devotees' houses.Experiences Of Devotees 265 At last, when an Ashram was formed, Pai also came and stayed with the Holy Mother. Instructed by her, Pai contin­ ued his spiritual practices. Pai always meditated on the Holy Mother in one particular pose. He cherished a wish to get such a photo of her but was afraid to speak out his desire to the Holy Mother. How could one ask her to sit in a particular pose in order to take a snap shot? He did nor open his heart to anyone concerning this. One day, Pai felt very sad about this matter and wept for a long time. Suddenly, Mother walked towards him and smil­ ingly said, “Son, Mother knows your desire. Mother will ful­ fill it.” After a couple of days. Mother called Pai and went to the temple. She sat on the peetham (stool) where she would usually sit during the Devi Bhava. Immediately upon sitting down, the Holy Mother became absorbed in samadhi. It was exactly this pose which Pai had held dear. Calling Sreekumar, a photo was taken and Pai became full of joy. Though one
163: is not well versed in the scriptures; by the Gum's Grace one can become an expert speaker. One of Pai’s experiences illustrates this fact. Once a devotee from Trivandrum came to see the Holy Mother. He wanted one amongst her children to give a seven day discourse on the great epic Srimad Ramayana. He prayed to Mother to kindly send one of her spiritual children to fulfill his wish. The Holy Mother told him, “None of the children have learnt Ramayana. Therefore, your request is difficult to fulfill. ” The devotee then said, “Mother, please don’t say so. I have promised the people that I will bring someone. Please save me! ” At last. Mother suggested Pai as the one to give the discourses. When he came to know about it he was shocked. He thought, 'Without having read or learnt Ramayana, how can I give a discourse? ’ Mother called Pai aside and asked him to bring the Ramay­ ana from the library. When it was brought, she took the Sun- darakanda (one chapter in the book) and asked him to read it. Hardly had he read ten lines when Mother asked him to stop and said, “That is enough. Now you go.” Having full faith in266 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Mother’s words, Pai went to Trivandrum. He stayed there for seven days and delivered speeches on Ramayana. It was a great success. Everyone appreciated Pai’s deep insight into the subject. At the end of the seven days, Pai frankly told everyone that in truth, he had not even studied the Ramayana and that all had happened by the Holy Mother’s Grace. Yet no one would believe him. Nothing is equal to the boundless com­ passion and Grace of a Perfect Teacher. It is that Grace which makes man into God. ***** Ramakrishnan is the son of brahmana parents belonging to Palghat in Kerala. In 1978, when he was an employee of the State Bank of Travancore, he heard about the Holy Mother from one of his friends. One evening he came to see her accom­ panied by the same friend. Though bom and brought up in an orthodox family, Ramaknshnan was heading for trouble due to the influence of college studies and bad company. On the verv first day itself when he saw the Holy Mother he burst into tears. All his inner coarseness melted and softened until eve­ rything was washed away in those purifying tears. After this, he would come for almost all the Darshans in order to see Mother in the Divine Mood. He would cry like a small child and pray to her to give him the vision of Madurai Meenakshi, his beloved Deity’. He would even fast on certain days in deep sorrow for not having gained that Vision. On such occasions, the Holy Mother would feed him with sweet pudding with­ out even making a mention of his fast. Shedding tears of in­ tense yearning while lying on Mother’s lap during Devi Bhava, he would
164: ask her, “Mother, will you come to me to­ morrow? At least let me hear the jingling of your anklets. ” Due to his sincere supplications, he was fortunate to have many such visions of his beloved Deity. On some days he would hear the jingling of Mother’s anklets and would some­ times get the vision of the Divine Mother or at other times he would smell a divine fragrance pervading the atmosphere around him.Experiences Of Devotees 267 There were two significant events which inspired Ramakrishnan to abandon worldly life and take up to a life of renunciation and spirituality. The first was on the day that he received initiation from the Holy Mother. On that auspicious occasion he felt an inexpressible power transmitted from her to him which totally changed his conception about the mean­ ing and goal of life. The second event was as follows: One day, while showing a picture of Sri Ramakrishna Par- amahamsa to Ramakrishnan, Mother said, “Both of you have the same name yet you have become like this.” These words of the Holy Mother pierced into the inner recesses of Ramakrishnan’s heart like a lightning bolt and strengthened his desire to become a real spiritual aspirant. One summer evening Ramakrishnan came to have Darshan of the Mother during Devi Bhava. It was extremely hot inside the temple where Mother was sitting. Mother asked him to fan her. He, however, hesitated as there was a group of young ladies standing outside near the temple. He thought to himself, *If a young man like me who is an em­ ployee in the State Bank fans a woman, they may laugh at me.’ Thinking thus, he didn't fan the Mother. But while coming out of the temple after Darshan, he hit his head severely on the wooden lintel over the door. Seeing that, all the young ladies standing there burst into laughter. Ramakrishnan turned pale and felted abashed. The next day, when Ramakrishnan went for Darshan Mother called him and said, “Yesterday you wouldn’t fan me even though I asked you to. Therefore I thought it would be for your good to make you a laughing stock in front of the young ladies whose laugh you feared!” From the next Darshan onwards, Ramakrishnan regularly fanned Mother even without being asked. At one time, Ramakrishnan was transferred to a branch of the bank about 100 kilometers away from the Ashram. His duty was to keep the key of the safe with him and arrive promptly at 10 a.m. every morning. Leaving the Ashram the following morning after the Sunday night Darshan, 268 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Ramakrishnan boarded the bus and reached a bus stop about 13 kilometres from his office. Enquiring there, he came to know that there would not be any bus going to his destination before 10 a.m. He then tried to get a taxi but none were avail­ able. Worried and upset, he called out “Amma!” Within a few moments, a man came along on a
165: motorcycle and stopped in front of him. He was a complete stranger. Turning to Ramakrishnan he said, “I am going to Pampakuda (the same village where Ramakrishnan was working). There are no buses until after ten so if you are willing I will drop you there.” Ramakrishnan agreed and sitting on the back seat reached the place and walked into the bank at exactly 10 o’clock! Replying to Ramakrishnan’s query. Mother told him, “One call is enough if done with concentration. God will reach there.” In 1981, Ramakrishnan had an experience which taught him a good lesson in obedience to the spiritual Master. Out of fear that Ramakrishnan would become a monk if he were to live in the Ashram too long, his parents tried to get him transferred to a branch bank in his home town near to them. Owing to their constant pressure, he at last submitted an application for transfer without seeking Mother's advice or permission. After some days, he changed his mind and sent a letter to the bank authorities requesting them to ignore his previous application. One day Mother said to him, “Better enquire about the second letter sent to the bank. It seems that it did not reach there.” Ramakrishnan replied, “That is not necessary. Mother. They must have received and accepted it.” Mother insisted several times that he enquire about the second letter but Ramakrishnan did not take her words seriously. Before long, Ramakrishnan received his transfer orders from the authorities in Trivandrum where the head office of the bank was located. Rushing there, he met the officers in charge but it was too late. As Mother had said, they had not received any letter asking them to ignore the previous appli­ cation. It had somehow got lost. In this way, Ramakrishnan learnt the bitter lesson that even seemingly insignificant statements of one's Guru should not be ignored.Experiences Of Devotees 269 One day while in the middle of a conversation, Mother turned to Ramakrishnan with a frown on her face and said, “There are some who still look at girls even after taking up to a life of renunciation.” Ramakrishnan asked, “Who is that. Mother?” “You!” Mother replied. Ramakrishnan was shocked. “What, me? I never look at women. Mother is scold­ ing me for no fault of my own,” he pleaded. The next moment Mother uttered the name of a woman well-known to Ramakrishnan and continued to narrate all the details relating to her husband's name, the names of the children and other family members, etc. Ramakrishnan stood motionless with his mouth wide open. Hearing the exact description, whereabouts and other details about a woman utterly unknown to the Holy Mother, he stood dumbfounded. Mother again asked him, “Hey, Ramakrish­ nan, tell the truth. Won't you look at her everyday?” Ramakrishnan kept silent. It was true that he would look at that woman everyday, but do you know why? The woman’s external appearance closely resembled the Holy Mother. Seeing her, he felt as if
166: he were seeing the Holy Mother her­ self. When Mother saw him standing there without speaking and with his head hanging down, she burst into laughter. It goes without saying that after this, Ramakrishnan never again looked at that woman. The above incident clearly shows how the Holy Mother closely observes the external and internal actions of her spiri­ tual children and instructs them accordingly. Before the Ashram was officially registered as a charitable institution, only a few persons were allowed to stay there. To look after the needs of many people was impossible due to the lack of finances at that time. Having left their jobs, some of the brahmacharins looked up to Ramakrishnan for food and clothing. As he was still employed, he would happily meet their needs even unasked. In the beginning days of the Ashram, Ramakrishnan felt that Mother was two distinct beings, her usual self and the Divine Being during the Bhava Darshan. This idea gave rise to270 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI a lot of confusion in his mind and he would often feel miser­ able while thinking in this way. He at last requested Mother to bless him and clear his erroneous way of thinking. One night he had a vision of the Holy Mother in her usual mood dressed in white clothes. This happened in the days before Mother was even dressing in white. After this vision, Ramakrishnan believed that Mother was essentially the same person what­ ever be her mood. Ramakrishnan’s faith in Mother deepened and his mind gradually became fixed on her divine form and name. This state created many troublesome situations at his work place. Sometimes he would make a mistake while counting out money or commit errors in the bank's accounts. In 1982, he came and settled in the Ashram and at the same time somehow continued his office work as well. Later, in 1984 he resigned his job and came to live in the Ashram pcrmanantly. ***** Ramesh Rao was born in a wealthy brahmana family in Hanpad, Kerala. He grew up as a moden youth enjoying and indulging in worldly pleasures, following a wayward and misguided way of life. Though immersed in mundane life, he would go to a nearby Devi temple to pray and repent for his loose ways. Before undertaking any act, good or bad, he would go and pray in the temple seeking the blessings of the Divine Mother. Once a friend of Ramesh’s invited him to the Holy Mother's Ashram but he declined the invitation. Later, when he was trying to go abroad for employment, he decided to visit the Ashram in order to know about his future as he had heard that the Holy Mother was endowed with divine powers and could foresee one's future. Thus, in June 1979 he entered the temple and approached the Holy Mother during Krishna Bhava. Before he could say anything, the Holy Mother ac­ costed him saying, “Son, you are trying to go across the ocean. Mother will make
167: it possible if you wish. Don't worry.”Experiences Of Devotees 271 At the very first meeting Ramesh was convinced of the Holy Mother’s divinity and felt bound to her by a strong feel­ ing of divine love. Returning home, he tried to concentrate his mind on the textile business which he had inherited from his father but he could not due to his mind being occupied with thoughts of the Holy Mother. On some days, his yearn­ ing to see her would be so intense that he would close the shop and rush to the Ashram. One day, while taking leave of the Holy Mother to return to his house, she told him, “Son, where to go? You are meant to be here.” One night Ramesh had a dream. The final dissolution of the Universe had arrived and balls of fire were raining down everywhere. The ocean’s waves rose up to the sky and threat­ ened to inundate the earth. Gathering all his strength, Ra­ mesh called out, “Amma. '”. Immediately a radiant splendour rose up from the turbulent ocean and started to pervade all the quarters. From that effulgence emerged the enchanting form of Goddess Durga dressed in a red silk saree and seated on a ferocious lion. She held divine weapons in each of Her eight hands. Ramesh was full of wonder to find that the compas­ sionate face of the Goddess was the same as that of the Holy Mother. She consoled him saying, “Why fear when I am with you? You are My son. Don’t worry. ” After this Ramesh had many visions of the Holv Mother in dreams. Due to close association with the Holy Mother, Ramesh’s inner urge to realise God and to live in the presence of the Mother intensified. One day while sitting in her presence, he had an experience which fanned the flames of his aspiration. It was 4 p.m. and Ramesh had come as usual to see Mother who was sitting in the shrine. He entered the temple and after pros­ trating before the Holy Mother he sat down near her. While sitting and gazing at her radiant face, the whole atmosphere in the temple suddenly changed. The world of plurality van­ ished from his sight and only Mother was visible. He realised her to lx his own Mother and found himself to be a 2 year old child. Intoxicated with divine love, Ramesh forgot the world. The Holy Mother lovingly placed his head on her lap.272 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI Mother, realising that Rao had become immersed in the inner bliss, gently lifted his head and had some devotees lay him on the floor of the temple. At 9 p. m.. Mother re-entered the temple and found him still lying in that state. Only upon hear­ ing Mother call “Son” did he return to normal consciousness. Following this incident, Ramesh’s life changed drasti­ cally. His longing to see Mother increased. He hardly cared for worldly matters. He stopped going to the shop. His
168: visits to the Holy Mother became frequent. He spent days and weeks at the Holy Mother’s place. This sudden change in Ramesh created an uproar in his family. They put all their ef­ forts into bringing him back to worldly life and persuaded him to get married. But all their attempts ended in failure. One day Mother told Ramesh, “Son, your parents are longing to see you. Go to your house and get their permission to be here.” Ramesh said, “Mother, are you abandoning me? They will make trouble for me” Mother replied, “A courageous man is one who can overcome all such difficulties.” The Mother sent Rao to his house with another resident ofthe Ashram. The family members kept Ramesh in their cus­tody by using force. They thought that the Holy Mother hadinfluenced him by using some malevolent force. In order toinfluence their son to return to worldly life, they performedspecial rituals. The parents insisted that Ramesh eat a specialtype of ghee which had been prepared by a priest using par­ticular mantras to make him leave the Ashram and lead aworldly life. Ramesh sought the Holy Mother’s advice aboutthe ghee. The Mother said, “Son, you eat it. If there is any­thing evil, let it be. Because of your spiritual tendencies youhave come here. Nothing will happen to such a person even ifthe ghee is eaten.” Obeying her words, Ramesh ate the ghee but nothing happened. His thirst for spiritual life did not wane. Now the family changed their tactics which became more harsh and inhuman. They presumed that their son’s sudden change was caused by some mental abnormality due to his disappoint­ ment at not getting a job overseas. With the assistance of hisExperiences Of Devotees 273 friends who also disapproved of his new ways, they forcefully took him to a psychiatrist for undergoing treatment. Ramesh told the doctor, “I am not crazy. I will strictly abide by the words of my Guru. It is you who are crazy for this world. Therefore, you try to impose your madness on others also.” At the bidding of his relatives the doctor, treated Rao for ten days. Their aim was to somehow create a taste in their son for worldly life. Therefore, immediately after the psychi­ atric treatment was over they decided to send him to Bhilai where his relatives lived thinking that a change in environ­ ment would help him to regain his old way of life. In addition to this, they tried to find a suitable bride for Ramesh. He was in a mental predicament and wrote to Mother, 'Mother, up to this moment I have not given into their trivial temptations. Even now if Mother doesn’t save me I will merge with the Mother in heaven. I will commit suicide.’ After a months stay at Bhilai, Rao was brought back to his house. His family was convinced by this time that he had given up his spiritual way of thinking and living. They prompted him to continue his textile
169: business. One day, without any­ body’s knowledge, he visited the Holy Mother and said, “Mother, if you abandon me, I will die." Without waiting for her reply, Ramesh again started staying in the Ashram. Dur­ ing his short stay of 3 days. Mother warned him several times about his relatives' intentions to create obstacles in his path again and again. The Holy Mother even advised him to go back to his house and wait till they gave consent to take up spiritual life but Rao paid no heed saying, “If I return home they won't let me continue my spiritual practices.” By this time, Rao's father had lodged a petition against the Holy Mother requesting for police intervention to regain his son who, he asserted, was being forcibly kept in the custody of the Holy Mother. On the third day, Rao's father and some other relatives came to the Ashram with a van full of police­ men. Rao boldly said to the police officer, “I am mature enough to choose my way of living and am free to decide on my place of residence.” They didn’t pay any attention to his 274 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI words and with the help of the police, his relatives decided to admit him into a mental hospitial in Trivandrum. On their way, all of them got down at Quilon to have their lunch. Rao refused to eat and sat in the car. Suddenly he heard a voice from within saying, “If you escape now you will be saved. Otherwise you will be destroyed.” The very next moment an autorickshaw came and stopped right in front of him. Without a moments hesitation he jumped into it. He told the driver the destination and asked him to drive fast. There was not even a penny in his pocket. At that time, one of the Ashram residents was staying in Quilon preparing for his Master’s degree in philosophy. Rao told him all that had happened. That night itself with the help of some devotees, Rao left Kerala and proceeded to Chinmaya Mis­ sion in Bombay. Coming to know that he was in Bombay, the relatives again tried to disturb him. To save his life, Rao de­ parted for the Himalayas. There was hardly any money with him for his train fare and food. Nor did he have any warm clothes to protect him from the freezing cold there. Somehow reaching the Himalayas, he wandered here and there. His clothes became torn and tattered. This fair looking youth became like a wandering mendicant begging his food and meditating under a tree or in a eave. Days and months passed. Finally, one day he received a letter from the Holy Mother at an address which he had given her which said, "Son, you come. No more problems.’ Rao returned to the Holy Mother’s Ashram. She then sent him to visit his parents who had learnt a good lesson. There was an apparent change in their adamant nature. They were happy to
170: see their son back home yet they still tried to tempt him. When they realised that their antagonistic approach was fruitless, they attempted to change him in a loving manner. But all their efforts were burnt to ashes in the fire of Rao’s intense dispassion. On 27 August, 1982 Rao joined the Ashram as a permanent resident and continued his spiritual practices undisturbed. *****Experiences Of Devotees 275 The following is Shakti Prasad’s (Subhir) account of his renunciation of worldly life achieved by the grace of the Holy- Mother in spite of tremendous odds. “It was at the end of 1983 when I met Amma after being di­ rected to her by the late Swami Vimalananda of Sri Ramakrishna Math, Nettayam, Trivandrum in Kerala. Dur­ ing the first year after meeting Amma, she gave me a number of chances to confirm her status as a Sadguru. She imparted many spiritual experiences which established a deep bond of love and surrender. Thus, one day I mentioned to her my wish to join her Ashram as a resident in order to get her teachings This meant resigning my position as Project Engineer in the Electronics Research and Development Centre, Trivan­ drum, a wing of Keltron. The Mother agreed yet warned me of a strong reaction from my relatives who belonged to the Muslim community. On the 4th of May, 1985 I joined the Ashram and many obstacles followed which were overcome solely due to Amma’s grace and directions. On the 11th of May my father and his people came to the Ashram demanding that I accom­ pany them to my family home saying that my mother was seri­ ously ill. I was obliged to leave the Ashram in their company. The father and others seriously believed that I was mentally afflicted and could be made okay if subjected to psychiatric treatment. I had no choice but to accompany them to many specialists in that field who unfortunately, could not grasp the charm and greatness of the study and exploration of the Inner Reality through the ancient tradition of Ashram life. On the night of the 16th I escaped from their custody and spent two weeks in an Ashram in Trivandrum where the people have great affection and devotion for Amma. 'The par­ ents then came to meet Amma and assured her that they would not disturb me again. I returned to Vallickavu at which time Amma told me that she did not believe their words. 5 June 1985: Father came to our Ashram and wanted me toaccompany him saying my mother had been hospitalized. Idiscussed the matter with Amma and she commented, “Their276 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI plan is to take you to a remote place for psychiatric treat­ ment.” Since the father was not prepared to go back empty handed, I requested Amma to permit me to go just to avoid any trouble to the Ashram. While bowing down at her feet before leaving, she whispered in my car, “You will be safe, I am
171: with you.” At the main road, the car was waiting for me with six people inside. Soon, the course of the journey’ was redirected to Ban­ galore and not Edappally as I had originally been informed. We spent that night in a tourist bungalow in Mettur. I was well guarded so as not to escape as before. At this time I was able to collect a few records revealing their intention to subject me to brainwashing and electric shock treatment. I carried this documentation carefully which would be produced as evi­ dence before the Kerala High Court after one year. Bangalore, 6 June: A room in a hotel near Subbaiah Circle was taken and seeing that I was indifferent to make any at­ tempt to escape, father and two others left the hotel for mak­ ing arrangements for admitting me. Finding no way to es­ cape, I meditated for a while and then sought a suggestion from Amma. Immediately flashed within my mind's screen the whole operation for escape. I requested those guarding me to allow me to go to the ve­ randah to make a phone call to an old friend. Permission was denied. Again I repeated my request and finally made a com­ mand. Almost mechanically they unlocked the room and while I was taken outside, in a brisk move I sprang ahead of them and pulled the door with all my force bolting it from the outside, thus locking them all inside. I rushed to the main road for some vehicle. An autorickshaw sped by with a passenger inside. After 50 yards it stopped abruptly. The driver was trying to restart it with the cranking lever. Thanking Amma- chi, I ran in that direction and dashed into the auto. That same moment it started and left the place. I could see through the back window that my captors were running aimlessly in search of me.Experiences Of Devotees 277 At Majestic Circle, a travel agent arranged a ticket for me to Calicut1 by a tourist bus service that same night. I gave him mv name for booking the ticket which I felt afterwards to be alittle unwise. Reaching the Mother’s Ashram the next day, itwas surprising to note that the money Amma had arranged tobe given to me while leaving the Ashram was exactly sufficientfor my return. Seeing me, Amma commented, “Very' good,but why should you give your name to those bus people?!” Vallickavu, 17 July 1985 : That morning Amma warned meto expect something to happen and suggested that I shouldnot be sentimental in any way just for the peace of the Ashram.She told me that she was dedicated to the sadhaks (spiritualseekers resident in the Ashram) and will act along those linesalone when facing any situation At 11 a.m., a big group led by the father entered the Ashram and demanded that I accompany them. As I refused,they threatened me with forced removal. Amma remainedcool and said to them, “We will give him protection if hewants; that
172: is our Ashram dharma.” The atmosphere becamea bit tense as the residents also entered into the scene. As a fewlocal devotees also started gathering, the group left the placegiving me two weeks notice. At 3 p.m. Amma came to me and ordered me to immedi­ately leave the Ashram premises. I left the place at once, not­ing my departure in the office Register. Within minutes aftermy departure, the father and his people were back with a few policemen from Karunagappally, accusing the Ashram of unlawful custody, etc. I spent those days in various institutions and friends’ houses in hiding. After seeking permission from Amma, I proceeded to Cochin Arya Samaj and had the necessary ceremonies performed so that I could embrace Hinduism as perthe Vedic rites. The details were then published in the Ga­zette.2 1 Eight hours journey north of Vallickavu. 2 A government publication which contains all legal notices, change ofnames, etc.278 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI The Governor and Inspector General of Kerala, after hear­ ing of my situation, took some action and warned the opposi­ tion. They assured me that I could stay in the Ashram and so I went back there. After one month. Mother gave me mantra diksha (initiation into the sacred mantra) during Devi Bhava. This, accelerated the progress in sadhana greatly. 10 July 1986. Amma was leaving for a reception and bhajanprogramme at a temple in Vypin accompanied by the resi­dents including myself. On the way we visited our Ashrambranch in Quilon and spent an hour there. It is our (the dis­ciples’) experience that as a Great Master, Amma first giveshints for right action from within, then from without, andorders will be given only if we miss the hint. Eight months hadpassed since the last trouble with my family and we never an­ticipated any action from them. But a strange feeling withinprompted me to consider halting my journey here in Quilon.This idea became stronger and put me in suspense to make adecision. I looked to Amma for her command; "Yes, remainhere,” she nodded meaningfully and proceeded to Vypinleaving me in Quilon. After 2 hours or so had passed, Srinivasan, a brother dis­ ciple, came running into the Quilon Ashram. He asked me to immediately leave the place. The Ashram van had been stopped at Karunagappally, 30 kilometres north of Quilon, by the police with a search warrant for my arrest based on a lunacy act issued by the Quilon Chief Judicial Magistrate Court. In vain they had searched all through the van for me.I spent the following days secretly staying here and there. Many other situations and timely help also occurred by Di­ vine Grace. At this stage of complete uncertainty, I had a thought to leave Kerala as a search warrant was still pending due to its reissue by the Court at my father’s and his peoples' persistent request. Also, I felt guilty in creating difficulties for Amma and the Ashram. That same day, Amma sent a message to me not to have any concern and thus
173: assured me that a direction favourable for a solution would come soon.Experiences Of Devotees 279 Two days later, a news article appeared in the Malayalam daily, ‘Maturbhumi’ that my parents had filed a Habias Cor­ pus petition in the Kerala High Court naming the respondent as Amma. They charged her with illegal custody and stated the need of expert psychiatric treatment for their son’s men­ tal illness. After getting permission and instructions from Amma, I immediately proceeded to Cochin. Through Advocate Gov- inda Bharathan of the Kerala High Court, I filed an Affidavit­ counter staring all the facts. On 27th August, 1987 I ap­ peared before the Kerala High Court with police protection. After a detailed discussion, the panel of Judges dismissed the original petition filed by the father and issued orders for my protection. The presiding Justice also issued a warning to cer­ tain persons who had been deeply involved with my harass­ ment during the last two years. Bombay, Feb. 1987: I had accompanied Amma and the brah­macharins to Bombay for her programmes there. It was thenthat we received the message that an appeal was being filed inthe Supreme Court of India questioning the ruling of theKerala High Court. They strongly argued that my sanitycould be proved only after a twenty-eight day observation bymedical experts. This claim had already been dismissed by theHigh Court’s ruling after they had examined all the docu­ments which I had produced, particularly the testimonialsfrom my previous employer and also after a detailed discus­sion with me regarding the causes of my embracing Hinduismand accepting Amma as my Master for spiritual life. Wishing me all the best, Amma instructed me to proceed to New Delhi. By her Grace, in the first hearing and argument itself, the appeal was dismissed.3 I was relieved from any addi­ tional complexities and risks.4 3 This is a Supreme Court landmark ruling in India which rules in favour of the individual's right to freely choose his religion. 4 “We do not see any grounds for releasing a notice to Mata Amritanandamayi, nor do we find anything defective in the judgement of the Kerala High Court in concluding the state of normalcy of the petitioner's son and offering him the right to pursue the spiritual life in her Ashram." (13th March, 1987)280 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI The expert in Amma is now fathoming deep into our being preparing us also to contain the Infinite within. Let us con­ tinue our journey to the Goal, experiencing and overcoming all natural hurdles, in the soothing care of Divine Love.” ***** This was the final comment of Justice M.P.Thakker and Justice B.C.Ray of the Supreme Court of India after handling a hearing of the special leave petition by Shakti Prasad's parents. The petition was duly dismissed. Shakti Prasad left New Delhi and returned to the Mother’s Ashram in Vallickavu.PART 4 'The Embodiment of Spiritual Perfection: Mother as the Spiritual Master'Mother As The Spiritual Master Who is a perfect person? If this question is asked to a mod­
174: ern youth he would say that an ideal person is a handsome, highly influential multi-millionaire or perhaps a high rank­ ing political leader; otherwise he would tell out the names of some romantic looking movie stars or cricket players. It is a pity that today the youngsters cannot conceive of a society without movies, politics and romance. It is a vital force for them. But do these have anything to do with our life and char­ acter building? What makes a person beautiful and perfect? What adds sweetness and attractiveness to one’s actions? What is that factor which makes one immortal and adorable? Is it any of the aforesaid things? A mature person endowed with discrimination will undoubtedly say, “No, absolutely not!” Then what is it? To put it in a sentence, it is the integra­ tion of inner virtues which in turn will manifest as eternal vir­ tues through one's whole being. This is what one can experi­ ence in the presence of the Holy Mother Amritanandamayi, the beautiful blending of unconditional love and bliss. People from different walks of life, each according to their own level of understanding and mental maturity speak differ­ ently about the Holy Mother. For example, if you ask who Mata Amritanandamayi is to a person whose intellect always dwells at a gross level, he would say, “She is an extraordinary woman who can cure terrible and incurable diseases by a mere touch or look.” He may also say, “She can also solve your worldly problems and can easily fulfill all your desires.” If the same question is asked to one who has a more subtle intellect he would say, “O, the Holy Mother is really incredible. She can bestow on you many psychic powers. She is a master in te­ lepathy and clairvoyance. It is nothing for her to transform water into panchamrnam and milk. All the eight mystic pow­ ers are under her sway,” and so on. A true spiritual seeker's answer to the same question would be, “The Mother is the ultimate Goal to be realised by an aspirant. She is the source and support of true seekers and helps them to cross the ever­284 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI changing Ocean of Transmigration. Her very nature is love and compassion; she is a veritable witness to the truths ex­ pressed in the Vedas and all other religious texts of the world. If you take refuge at her feet, definitely the Goal is at hand. She is a Perfect Master and a Great Mother as well. From the standpoint of a person who is following the Path of Devotion (Bhakti Yoga), the Holy Mother is a true devotee par excellence. In her one can see the different aspects of Su­ preme Devotion manifesting unimpeded. When one who pursues the Path of Knowledge (Gnana Yoga) observes the Mother, he can perceive a perfect Knower of the Self in her words and deeds. For a person who is sincerely following the Path of Action (Karma Yoga), the
175: Holy Mother is unsur­ passed among Karma Yogins. These are all partial views bom of each one’s limited experience and understanding. But through close association and observation devoid of preju­ dice and presumption, one could clearly comprehend that the Holy Mother is an integration of all these. There is a proverb in Malayalam, ‘As patient as the Earth.’ The Mother Earth bears everything. People kick her, spit on her, till her with a plough, dig into her, and cut open her breast using a pick axe for cultivation and other purposes, They even construct hundred story buildings on her but she patiently bears everything. She has no complaints. She despises none but serves and nourishes all in the best way. Likewise, the Holy Mother shows an immense amount of patience in re­ building the character of her children. She patiently waits until the disciples become mature enough to be disciplined. Until then, she bathes them in her selfless love forgiving all the errors which they may commit. If one closely goes through the great lineage of the ancient saints and sages of India and observes the ways which they adopted to teach and enlighten their disciples, it is not diffi­ cult to understand the unique nature of the Guru-disciple re­ lationship which cannot be seen anywhere else in the world. The Holy Mother would say, “In the beginning a Sadguru (Perfect Master) won’t give strict instructions to his disciple. Mother As The Spiritual Master 285 He will simply love him. He will bind the disciple with his un­ conditional love. The strong impact created by the Guru’s love will make the disciple fit for the Guru to work on his vas- anas or mental tendencies. Slowly the Guru, with strict vet loving instructions, will discipline and remould the disciple’s personality. In a real Guru-disciple relationship, it will be dif­ ficult to distinguish who is the Guru and who is the disciple because the Guru will be humbler than the disciple and the dis­ ciple will be humbler than the Guru.”In the beginning the Guru, in addition to showing great love for the disciple, may even act according to the disciple's whims and fancies to some extent, but when he finds that the disciple is mature enough to earnestly start spiritual practice then the Guru will slowly start disciplining him. Once the disciplining starts however, though full of love for his disciple who is verily like a son or daughter to him or her, he will not express his love much. His sole aim will be to make the disciple aware of his own Pure Self. In other words, his disciplining itself is another way of expressing his love. That is real love, transforming the dis­ ciple into a pure jewel. Pointing out and correcting the drawbacks of her children the Holy Mother would say, “I am like a gardener. Tlx garden is full of colourful flowers. I was not asked to look after the beautiful flowers which are in no way
176: defective but I have been asked to remove the insects and worms from the pest- ridden flowers and plants. To remove the insects I may have to pinch the petals and leaves which is painful no doubt but it is to save the plants and flowers from destruction. Likewise, Mother will always work with the children's weaknesses. The process of elimination is painful but it is for your good. The virtuous aspects need no attention but if your weaknesses are not removed they will destroy your virtues as well. My chil­ dren, you may think that Mother is angry with you. Not at all. Mother loves you more than anyone else and that is why she does all these things. Mother expects nothing except your spiritual progress." One could never see the Holy Mother sitting on a royal seat286 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI commanding her spiritual children and devotees to do this and that. She instructs and at the same time sets an example by her actions. Humility and simplicity are the trademarks of greatness. The Holy Mother is a living example of this. She is humbler than the humblest and simpler than the simplest. She says about herself, “I am the servant of servants. This life is for others. Her children’s happiness is Mother’s wealth and health." The Holy Mother’s method of exhausting the ego and other negative tendencies of her children is wonderful. Al­ ways she is the invincible warrior. The Mother herself pre­ pares the field to test her children’s mental maturity' and spiri­ tual progress by creating a suitable situation. Without caus­ ing the least suspicion she leads one to the field. By the time one realises the seriousness of the situation all the inner foes would have risen up and the discriminating intellect gives way to the emotional mind. At this moment the Holy Mother properly uses the opportunity to remove the egoism of her children. Her mighty weapons hit the target without fail and in due course the negative tendencies of those who seek her guidance become weaker and weaker. The following is one such incident. Once, some years ago Brahmachan Nealu brought a port­ able typewriter from Tiruvannamalai where he had previ­ ously resided. Although Balu (Amritatma Chaitanya) had not learned typing, he took a piece of paper and just for the fun of it typed, ‘Mother, make me your slave.' Mother, who was sitting nearby talking to Nealu suddenly turned towards Balu and asked, “Son, what are you typing?” Balu thereupon trans­ lated the meaning of the sentence into Malayalam for her. Without asking or saying anything more about it, the Mother continued to talk to Nealu. After 15 minutes Mother said to Nealu, “I am going to send Balu abroad.” It was a shock for Balu to hear those words from Mother's mouth as he had already quit two jobs with the intention of staying in Mother’s presence forever. "What did you say. Mother?” he anxiously asked. “Yes, we need money to run the
177: Ashram. The number ofMother As The Spiritual Master 287 residents are increasing and we have no income to support them all. So, you must go and work,” Mother replied. 'This was enough for Balu. His inner foes raised their hoods and with all vehemence he said, “No, I don’t want to work. I cannot go away from here. I came here to stay with Mother and not to do worldly work or earn money.” However, Mother went on insisting that he must go until his anger knew no bounds. His negative tendencies stood ready to attack. Suddenly in a soft voice Mother said, “Son, what did you type just a few minutes ago? If you want to become God’s ser­ vant, vou have to surrender all that is yours at His Feet. If the mind is not pure, God won’t dwell in your heart. To become God’s servant is to accept all experiences, good and bad, aus­ picious and inauspicious, with an equipoised mind. See eve­ rything as God’s Will. Son, I don't want your wealth. When I see you crying for God I am so happy and my heart overflows towards vou.” As soon as she uttered the last word she became absorbed into a divine mood. Tear drops rolled down her cheeks and her body became still. This continued for an hour after which she slowly came down to this plane of worldly existence. Balu's mind was full of remorse. He fell at her feet and begged her to forgive him. He prayed, “Mother, please purify my heart. Rid me of all impure thoughts and actions. Make me a perfect instrument in your hands. ” She consoled him and said, “Son, don't worry". You have come to Mother and now it is her responsibility to look after you and make you perfect. ” Hearing these words, Balu felt full of peace and joy. The Holy Mother once said, “Children, you are happy ifMother always shows a smiling face. If Mother says some­thing against your wish, then you will think that Mother hasno love towards you, but it is not so. Mother is always trying tomake you stronger. To reinforce you spiritually all mentalweaknesses should be removed. To achieve that end some­times she will make an external show of anger. Sometimes, inorder to teach you, this is necessary. Take for example a cowthat is merrily eating up the tender leaves of a young coconut288 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI tree. It is not enough if one says, ‘Dear cow, please don't eat the tender leaves. The plant will wither away? The cow defi­ nitely won't move even an inch. But if you take a long stick and shout at it, ‘Go away, go away.’’ the cow will immediately abstain from its mischief. Similar is Mother's show of anger. Children, Mother has not even an iota of anger towards you. Always remember that Mother has no selfish motives anti acts only for your spiritual progress. If Mother always shows her love and affection to
178: you then you won't look inwards to seek your Real Self. Children, as far as a worldly person is con­ cerned, it is enough for him to look after his wife and children, but a real sannyasin on the other hand, has to shoulder the bur­ den of the whole world. Therefore you must grow stronger.” Once it so happened that after the usual darshan. Mother could go for rest only at about 4a.m. After she had entered her hut and lay down after closing the door, one Ashramite slept in front of her room as usual in order to ensure that nobody would disturb her by going into the room. Exactly at that time a young woman who, missing the bus, had walked all the way from Quilon, a distance of 35 kilometers, reached the Ashram seeking Mother's blessings. When she learnt that Mother had gone to bed she was crest fallen but still with a glimmer of hope she called Mother aloud a couple of times. Hearing the lady's voice, the Ashramite who was lying down in front of Mother's door got up and took the lady to task for disturbing Mother and even asked her to leave the place. At that very moment. Mother, who could understand what was happening, opened the door and came out to meet the devotee. After affectionate enquiries, the Mother consoled her and assured her of solving the problems which she was facing. Turning to the person who had slept in front of her door. Mother spoke in a serious tone, “I am not here for enjoying any rest or comfort but rather to serve others and alleviate their sufferings. Their happiness is my happiness. I do not want anyone's service. I am here to serve all. I must be free to meet anybody at any time I won't permit anyone to prevent me from meeting the devotees who come seeking solace and Mother As The Spiritual .Master 289 succour from me. Do you know with what great difficulty they come here with their meagre savings just to unburden their aching heart to me? If you repeat this rudeness and try to impose rules on me that I should meet devotees only at such and such times I will dissolve this organisation. I do not want any such thing as a mission if it is not to serve ailing humanity. A mission should be for service.” Saying this, she prohibited the Ashramite and all of the other residents from sleeping in front of her hut. On another occasion a sick lady who had come to the Ashram for Mother’s advice had vomited on Mother’s cloth. A resident, one of the girls who was personally attending on Mother, picked up the dirtied cloth with a stick and was about to hand it over to the washerman. Seeing this. Mother took the girl to task and said, “If you are unable to see the one Divine in all and serve all equally, then what is
179: the use of having done so many years of meditation and service? Is there any differ­ ence between me and this sick lady?” Saying these words. Mother took the cloth herself and washed it, prohibiting the girl from attending on her for some days afterwards. The Holy Mother's mere presence itself is inspiring to the devotees. She can inspire and give them the strength to do anything and everything at any time. For example, if there is some work in the Ashram such as carrying bricks, sand and other materials for construction purposes or even emptying the septic tank, cleaning the Ashram premises or assisting the masons in concrete work, etc., the residents would run here and there trying to gather some devotees to help. Sometimes it might be three or four in the morning when the Bhava Darshan had finished and all the devotee would be ready to go to sleep. Suddenly the Holy Mother would come to where the work was going to proceed. She would always be the first tn start the work. Despite the fact that she had been siting from 6 o’clock the previous evening until 3 or 4 the next morning she would be seen working full of cheer and enthusiasm. Very soon word would spread that Mother is carrying bricks, water or whatever it may be and from all directions the devotees would come running. The most interesting part of it is that 290 MATA AMRITANANDAMAYI the work which normally would have taken 6 or 7 hours to finish would be completed within 1 or 2 hours. In order to make the devotees forget the strain of the work, the Holy Mother will make the devotees laugh with her great sense of humour and will even make a small fire near the work spot and make drinks and roast peanuts for all those who are working and herself distribute them to her devotees. In the midst of the work the Holy Mother will instruct everyone saying, “Children, while engaged in any kind of work always try to chant your mantra or sing devotional songs. Only those actions which are surrendered to the Lord are counted as real action and then action (karma) becomes Yoga. Otherwise it is Karma Bhoga (action motivated by the urge to enjoy).” “Even when the Gopis of Vrindavan went out to sell milk, buttermilk, etc., they would call out ‘Krishna, Madhava, Yadava, Kesava... ’ Even in the kitchen they would write and affix Krishna's different names on all the spice bottles and other provisions. They also discharged the duties of a house­ wife. They never sat idle but always carried Knshna's form in their heart and His Divine Names on their lips. Children, try to be like them. ” Whatever questions are asked and whoever is the ques­ tioner, whether a thcist, an atheist, a rationalist or an antago­ nist, the Holy Mother gently, calmly, and affectionately an­ swers them without hurting them or belittling their ideas. For example, one
180: day a young man who visited the Ashram told the Holy Mother, “I have no faith at all in spiritual phi­ losophy and spiritual masters. Is it not better to serve human­ ity? Many people are suffering from poverty and starvation. What are these so-called spiritualists doing for them? Are they not simply wasting their time sitting idle?” The Holy Mother calmly replied, “Son, what you say is correct. Of course, service to humanity is important. A true spiritual seeker’s life must be dedicated to that. Mother per­ fectly agrees with that point of yours. But what is real service? Real service is extending help without expecting anything. Who is doing that? If somebody has an idea to help a poorMother As The Spiritual Master 291 family undoubtedly there will be a selfish motive behind it. Everyone is after name and fame. Mother knows that spiritual advice won’t satisfy the hunger of a person who is plunged in poverty. We must have compassion and love for such people.
